of your prospect to try to like, give some definition to you guys and to try to explain what
positioning actually is because when I was first kind of going through this, it was a little bit more
complex. So I have like an overarching, you know, definition that I've kind of put that makes
sense to me and hopefully makes sense to you guys, which is positioning is how your customers
will perceive your brand and your product in their mind, okay? It's about what is in their mind
because again, as we mentioned, it's about a battle for the consumer's mind, not about the battle
of products, okay? And so at the end of the day, there's a more actual example that I'll get
through from a girl named April Dunford. She's actually an expert on positioning, but at the end of
the day, it's positioning is how your customers or how customers will perceive your brand and
product in their mind, okay? And not just customers, but prospects as well.
And so to create something that doesn't already exist in the mind is becoming more and more
difficult because there's just so many more people creating products and, you know, making
businesses and taking positions, which is why one of the best ways to clearly position yourself in
today's market is to know what's already in the prospect's mind and use it to your advantage,
okay? So you kind of look into the mind of the prospect, like, what are you already seeing? What
are you already looking at? What are you considering? What do we use? All that kind of stuff.
And then trying to figure out how your brand can fit into that, you know, a slot in their mind, okay?
The biggest thing, and I'm, you know, I'm a victim of this as well, is so many of us are marketing
and advertising as if competitor positions do not already exist or, you know, as like, whatever,
like, and I tell you guys all the time, like, stop looking at your competitors, like, just focus on your,
on doing your own thing. But that's actually terrible advice.

You should definitely be aware of what's going on with your competitors because you need to
know that not just so you can copy their ads, which is the reason why I tell most of you guys to
not look at your competitors because you just copy what they're doing, but to know what to do to
separate yourself and to position yourself, okay? And to differentiate, which is what we'll get into.
But again, a slightly more actionable definition of this, if, you know, this one doesn't make sense,
which it should, is position defines how your product is the best in the world at delivering a
differentiated value, or as April would say, some value that a well-defined set of customers care a
lot about, okay? So at the end of the day, it's about how your product is really great, is the best at
delivering this value that specific customers care about, right? That is kind of the idea of
positioning, okay? Now, why does all of this matter, right? Obviously it matters because we
wanna make a ton of money, right? But the reason why we have to resort to positioning, because
I want you guys to understand that, you know, positioning is not just branding and positioning is
also not just marketing, right? Positioning is its own thing. And it matters because the mind is so
overcrowded, okay? There is like nowadays, so many ads that people get served and so much,
like we deal with so much.
We are like in complete information overload. And this was mentioned in the original book in like
the 60s, okay? And they talked about how crowded the mind was getting then. Fast forward 60
years, guys, like it has only gotten crazier.
Instagram didn't exist then. Facebook and TikTok didn't exist then, right? The just sheer amount
of ads that people see every single day and content that people consume every single day is just
hard to even fathom, okay? There are just too many options in modern day for the mind to
comprehend it all. And that makes choices on like which one is the best nearly impossible, okay?
Which is why it's not just about having a better product.
It's about having a strong position in the market that consumers actually understand, okay? And
you create a strong position in the market through differentiation, all right? Differentiation refers to
the set of characteristics of your product and your brand that distinguish it from others, okay? As
the word says, it's just why are you different? It's not about just like I have a better product.
Nobody cares about better. They care about why are you different, okay? And examples of, or
like the best example that I can give you of differentiation through a extremely sophisticated
market, I'm gonna kind of show you how different brands have entered a market, differentiated
themselves and then been able to scale to hundreds of millions of dollars is starting in one of
everybody's favorite markets which is bars, right? Protein bars or just bars in general, okay? So
the first brand here that kind of attacked this market was Quest Bar, okay? And so in 2010, after
the rise of Power Bar, which for those of you guys that don't know, Power Bar was like the first.
They were the first like energy bar. It was for, you know, endurance runners that were running a
lot and they needed something to like, you know, keep their carbs high and keep their sugars
high. And so they had Power Bars which had a bunch of carbs, right? And so what Quest noticed

about Power Bars is that they were loaded with sugar, obviously as they needed to be for people
who are running with endurance and needed to, you know, get their carbohydrates back up and it
was low in protein, right? So what they did is they attacked Power Bars where they were weak,
which is protein, by narrowing a focus on like, hey, we're gonna make bars that are high in protein
and that say no to carbs.
And then what Quest Bars did is they were basically saying like, hey, we're not for endurance
athletes, although endurance athletes still eat them, but they are for regular people looking for a
bar that was low in carbs and high in protein to just get more of, you know, their protein in for the
day. And as a result, Quest Bar became extremely popular and at the end of the day, were they
better? That's kind of up for debate, but were they different? A hundred percent. They were
higher in protein and lower in carbs, which is essentially the exact opposite of Power Bars.
And, you know, they were $1 billion worth of differentiation. What I'm saying is like, they were
different enough to be worth billion dollars, right? A thousand million, kind of crazy, right? And
then we go into another brand that enters the market, which is RX Bar, okay? In 2013, RX Bar
called bullshit on the ingredients and they didn't actually say bullshit. They called, they said no
BS, as you could see in their logo here, but obviously people assumed bullshit, but it was a
double entendre for, you know, bad stuff if you actually look at their brand.
But what it was is they looked at the ingredients in Power Bars and in Energy Bar brands and
they were like, those ingredients are BS, right? And so what did they do? They differentiated
themselves. They developed a product, you know, an Energy Bar with cleaner ingredients, okay?
They put their ingredients on the front of the package and they didn't just hide them on the back.
Like, look at this.
They literally said three egg whites, six almonds, four cashews, two dates, no BS, right? They put
that on the front and they positioned themselves as we have nothing to hide, right? We're an
Energy Bar with nothing to hide. And they were essentially alluding to the fact that their
competitors do. They don't say our competitors are hiding things from you.
They just said, hey, we have nothing to hide. And what happens? Well, they state a fact and then
people can draw their own conclusions, right? Again, were they a better bar? And a lot of times

different? A hundred percent.
They were $600 million worth of different, okay? And so again, not better, just different. And then

And that's what matters most, okay? And so let's get into the meat, okay? I've just given you a

it's better to be different, not better, okay? You do not win by being better. You win by being
different in a way that occupies a clear mental slot. This is again from the book, Positioning Jack
Trout I'll Rise.
A lot of this stuff is, but the point of this is, is after the success of Red Bull, not to jump into
another example, but again, it makes explaining it easier. Thousands of energy drinks flooded the
market in these smaller cans, right? But what did Monster do? Monster was different. They

launched energy drinks in 16 ounce cans.
Now, is that better? It's just a bigger can, like maybe it's better if you want more, but at the end of
the day, it was just different. They were these huge cans. Everybody focuses their energy on
having a better energy drink, you know, better products, better service, better prices, all this kind
of stuff.
But at the end of the day, being different is really how you stand out, okay? Because when you
tell consumers and you tell prospects that you are better, the mind automatically, it gets
defensive. It's like, it's suspicious of that. You know, at the end of the day, if you really were
better, wouldn't you be the leader? Like, wouldn't you be number one, right? That's kind of
obvious.
That's the first thing anybody would think. Well, like if you're, if you are actually better, then
wouldn't you, wouldn't you be the biggest in the category? It's like, well, you're not. So I don't
believe you, okay? But when you tell consumers that you're different, right? It gets them to start
questioning, like, why are they different? You know, like, why are you different? Like, you know,
there's this, probably that is this, but why are you doing it differently, right? Now they're
interested.
That search for why is what feeds them to conclude that maybe you actually are better. Again,
you don't say that you're better. You allow them to come to that conclusion.
Now I know there's a little bit being cut off here, but again, when you communicate that difference

And so again, be different, don't be better. Number two, it's better to be first. Okay.
If you cannot be first in a new category, you want to try to be first in a subcategory. This is just
again, all examples to help you guys understand rather than me explaining it, just giving you
examples, just makes it hit home. Instead of being another phone, what Apple did is they were
the first smartphone.
They literally created their own category, right? And then when they dropped the iPhone, they
didn't say we're another phone because then they would have been competing with Nikia, which
was at the time, I think 40% of market share. They would have been competing with BlackBerry.
What they did is they created their own category.
They created the first smartphone, right? And what I love about this, and this is why Steve Jobs is

But my point is, is like he created this category and called it the smartphone, which automatically

obvious thing is not so obvious. But again, he made his own category. He called it the
smartphone.
And now you guys see smart devices everywhere. There's smart TVs, there's smart vacuums,

Poppy sold for, I think, $2 billion, right? They weren't soda. They were prebiotic soda. Okay.

amazing strategy to scale. Okay. I know you can't see the scale, but it just says to scale.

And I cannot like emphasize that anymore. These examples are great examples of that. Okay.
Now, if you can't be first, right? You can't create a subcategory. You can't find a subcategory that,
and just to be clear on this, that isn't like too narrow, right? Because sometimes people can make
a subcategory. Like I can potentially make a subcategory from like, oh, well, you know, I'm not
mountain water in a can.
I'm glacier water in a can. Like maybe that works. Maybe it doesn't.
But again, whenever anybody thinks of water in a can, they're going to think like with death. So
that positioning might not make sense. Right.
And so if you can't be first, you likely need to reposition what you're up against. Okay. And so
repositioning, just to be clear on this is not comparison.
Okay. We're better than our competitors is not repositioning. You should never ever.
And I wanted to create slides that said never, ever, ever, and just have a bunch of bevers, but
you should never ever say that you're better. Okay. Because if you were, you'd be number one.
And if you're not number one, you're not better. Okay. Very simple rule to follow.
So what you need to do is you need to find a contrast. Okay. So remember, if you can't be first,
then you need to reposition.
Aspirin was the first leader in pain relief. Okay. Talanol positioned itself as for people who can't
take aspirin, like literally so simple.
Okay. Instead of fighting aspirin head on and having the same thing, they use a different
ingredient and said, Hey, we're for people who can't take aspirin. And it repositioned who it was
for.
Or modern day example here. Amazon was the first leader in econ. Okay.
They position themselves as we are for the customer. What did Shopify do? Shopify positioned
themselves as for the merchant, which is if you guys haven't noticed the opposite. Okay.
And instead of fighting Amazon head on, they just repositioned who Shopify was for. Amazon's
for the customer. Well, we're for the merchant, right? Amazon has all these fees.
They charge you this. You got to use this thing over here of theirs. You got to be on prime, all this
kind of stuff.
Obviously still a great thing, but Shopify is for the merchant. Okay. How about Stoli's? Instead of
saying they were a better vodka, they said they were different.

How did they do that? Well, they said most American vodkas seem Russian. Stoli's is different. It
actually is Russian.
Very simple. Right. They didn't say we're better than these vodkas because we're Russian.
They said, Hey, most vodkas seem like they're Russian, but they're actually made. I know you
can't see this, but they're made in, you know, one's made in Massachusetts, one's made in Long
Island, but Stoli's is actually made in Russia. Okay.
And another good example here is Scope Mouthwash. Again, instead of saying they were a
better mouthwash, they said it fights bad breath without giving you medicine breath. Right.
They repositioned the leader, not by saying they were better. They just said, Hey, we smell
different, right? Listerine smells like medicine breath. We smell, you know, we smell different.
We smell clean. Okay. And so at the end of the day, again, they're not saying they're better.
They're just creating contrast. The next thing on here is finding a strategic enemy. Okay.
When you define what you are against, it becomes clear what you are for. Again, this is from
Laura Reisz. This is from now, you know, the 2025 version of positioning, which is this one here.
Again, I have these recommendations at the end. Laura Reisz is an absolute G. She's actually Al
Reisz's daughter. I don't know if I can call her a G, but she is.
Now, the reason why having an enemy works so well, and the reason why she named her book,
The Strategic Enemy, is because changing a firmly held belief, especially nowadays in every
single market, there's so many beliefs that are just firm. It is much more difficult to change that
belief versus just contrasting a new idea to something that is already accepted and already
believed is as more effective. And so a strategic enemy is this oppositional force that your brand
or category stands against.
And it can be a competitor, it can be an approach, or it could just be a concept or a belief. And
we're going to talk about ABCs later as we get into like storytelling as well, Harry Dry's ABC
method. But essentially, when you position against some kind of strategic enemy, and again, not
just an enemy, it needs to be strategic, it makes your position sharper and more memorable,
okay? The most important part of having a strategic enemy is that it actually energizes and
motivates your consumers and your customers to really rally for your cause because you're
resonating with something that they stand for as well.
And when you do this the right way, it will 100% lead to more word of mouth, more referrals, and
again, more organic brand growth, which is what every single one of you watching this video
probably wants, okay? Back to the science here, the mind understands ideas best through
contrast when we are able to contrast something where it's like, hey, you know this thing, this is

the opposite of it. You're like, oh, I get it. Now, just to be clear, strategic enemy is not about hate,
it's not about like good versus evil or good versus bad.
It's not about you being right versus the enemy being wrong. It's about acknowledging that the
consumer is making a choice and by you standing for something, it makes making that choice
easier. Ideally, making the choice in your favor, but again, when you stand for something, you're
not going to be everybody's cup of tea.
At the end of the day, there's something called the everybody trap that Jack Trout and Al Rise
actually mentioned in the original book, which is when you try to sell something to everyone,
you're going to likely sell nothing to no one, okay? And so when you stand for something, it
makes you a clear choice. Some examples, Tesla's enemy, gasoline vehicles. They make electric
cars.
Uber's enemy, taxis, right? They sell on-demand pickups. Colgate's enemy, tooth powders, right?
They put toothpaste in a tube. Crazy to think now there's almost like a pendulum swing the other
way where now people are starting to sell tooth powders again, which is kind of interesting.
But the farmer's dog enemy, brown burnt balls, aka kibble, right? They make fresh dog food.
Lalo's enemy, you might not know this brand, but they're actually kind of like a designer, a high
chair brand, but essentially their enemy was just this idea of ugly plastic chairs, right? All baby
high chairs were just ugly and like gross. And so they just sell modernized, stylished high chairs,
right? For more chic and sophisticated homes.
The final thing on here is you need to be able to say no. No is one of the most powerful words
that instantly makes your position and your strategic enemy much more clear. And again, as we
just talked about, when you have a strategic enemy and you stand for something, right? You
become a clear choice.
You become sometimes for some people, an obvious choice, right? And you can reinforce that by
saying no, okay? If you say yes to everything and yes to everybody, there's a little chance of
actually finding some kind of a strategic enemy, right? Saying no sends a message about what
you stand for and what you are fighting against, which again, enhances your position. To give you
some examples of this, Chick-fil-A said no to beef burgers, right? They don't sell beef. They also,
very interesting thing, say no to Sundays.
They are not open on Sundays. For those of you that don't know what Chick-fil-A is, it's a very,
very popular chain in America. But the point is, is they reinforce this position with their ads around
like eat more chicken, which are literally like these ads that are, you can look them up.
It's like they're painted by cows. Like the cow has like a paintbrush in his mouth, which is brilliant
advertising. But the point is, is they say no to that.

They will never sell beef burgers because that doesn't enhance their position. And just to give you
guys context, they did $21.6 billion in sales in 2023, which was third behind McDonald's and
Starbucks. But the craziest part about this is they literally have 2,500 locations and are closed
every single Sunday.
McDonald's has 13,000. Starbucks has 16,000 and almost all of them are open like 24 seven.

They clearly have an enemy. They do all the things that you need to do to establish a strong

Okay. Another one here is Uniqlo. If you guys haven't heard of this brand, what they're saying no

any positioning process is to look inside the mind of the prospect.

And if people wanted to eat a lot of ice cream and not hate themselves, guess what? You go and
get Halo Top. The key takeaways here on how to start with positioning and we're wrapping up
here now, guys. But the first thing that you need to ask yourself is how can you create a new
category? Instead of competing in an existing market, how can you invent a new subcategory
where you have no competitors, right? This even goes back to the Kyle Milligan when I was

1.Being aware of avatars - ️ Evolve Copywriting NEW Evolve
Alright, so in this module, I try not to read ahead here because it's a pretty funny example, but we
need to remind you guys that you need to be aware of your avatars. Okay, before writing ads and
applying these copywriting strategies, you guys should go through this avatar training to better
understand how to build more actionable avatars. Again, the TLDR of this two and a half hour
long video, if you haven't watched it is essentially you need to understand that there are better
ways to make avatars than just using demographics.
And we do that by focusing on avatars that start with a desire. So we find the desire that
someone has. And we make that essentially our core avatar.
And then we get more clear on that avatar by focusing on adding experiences and behaviors and
specific emotions and demographics if needed, but mainly the experiences and behaviors that
they have, which again, are going to tell us are the beliefs and the pain points that that person
has as a result of what they've been through. But for example, before I can write any kind of ad, I
need to ask myself, who am I writing this for? Because again, this is so important for you guys to
consider before you start writing copy. Okay, so if I'm writing this for more of a beginner at home
chef kind of avatar, what is the desire that they have? Well, they probably want to impress their
family, right? That's when I'm just, you know, put her, it could be him, it doesn't matter, I cook.
The experience here is, you know, she overcooked chicken breast badly in the pan. That was the
experience that she had. Now her emotion is that she feels embarrassed when dinner doesn't
taste great, right? When you overcook chicken, she gets embarrassed because it's like, gosh, this
is not good.
And then her behavior, she's always looking for like easy to cook recipes. And again, like some of
these might be relevant to the product, some might not be. But again, I'm going after this more
beginner at home chef, right? This is the type of avatar that I'm pursuing, right? And this is what
they want.
And so then we have to answer the question is like, why does this person care? Why is this
person going to care at all about what I'm going to say? Well, they're going to care because they
want to impress their family, right? They have that desire of wanting to impress their family, but
they have this belief that they can't cook chicken properly in the pan, right? Or they just might
have the belief that they just can't cook chicken at all, right? So they have the belief that they
can't cook chicken properly, right? And so they just don't make chicken, they always order in or
whatever it is, right? And so again, you can start to look at the behavior. So a better behavior,
and I just made all this up, is they, you know, she never makes chicken dinner anymore, right?
She can't make all the chicken that she ever makes, it's just bland, it's boring, because she just
like puts it in the oven, whatever it is, right? And so here, this is where it's like, this is why this
person is going to care because they have this belief. And then again, we start to talk about now

we're starting to bridge the gap towards our ad because let's just say, for example, I sell a higher
fat content chicken, right? And our product is basically this chicken that has higher amounts of
fat, you can call them chicken thighs if you want.
And that allows them to and allows people to kind of have a more friendly, you know, cut of
chicken. So it allows them to overcook the chicken without actually drying it out, right? Because
for a lot of people, they're scared to undercook it. So they always overcook it, right? And the more
that I know about this beginner at home chef and kind of what they're going through, and again,
this is just the chicken example specifically.
But my point is, is before we write anything, we need to understand who are we writing this for?
Because when we actually know who we're writing it for, we're going to realize, okay, does this
person, is this person actually going to care about it, right? Because for example, if I'm selling this
higher fat content chicken to a, you know, professional chef or to a chef that is like much more
established and like cooks all the time, I'm not going to position an ad is like, you know, scared to
overcook chicken again, right? Or like never overcook chicken again, right? Let's just say this is
the headline that I wanted to use, right? This is not going to resonate at all with a, you know, a
professional chef, right? And so this works great for a beginner chef, but for a professional chef,
let's just say this is the other person that I wanted to go after, we could say, you know, more
flavor than, you know, way than or the Wagyu of chicken, let's just call this, okay? We could call it
that way, right? And now this is probably going to resonate more with a professional chef that is
like more, you know, focused on like trying to make the most flavorful, flavorful meals, like the
best tasting meals, right? That's probably more of a professional chef's desires to make an
incredible meal, like a meal that just like overwhelms your taste buds, right? You know, the
chicken that overwhelms your taste buds, whatever it is, right? In the best way possible. Now
you're starting to think through, okay, like even if you just start with a person, you're going to have
headlines that come to you, right? Like I'm just, I'm just winging this, and these are obviously not
the best headlines, but my point with this is, is like who you're writing for and why they're going to
care is really going to help dictate what your headline looks like, okay? Because again, if I just
wrote this, the Wagyu of chicken, that potentially might be scary for a beginner at home chef,
right? Because they don't, they don't like, they don't, they can imagine burning a Wagyu chicken if
they even know what that is, right? So my point is, it's very important to be aware of your avatar
before writing the ad. You should be thinking, who am I writing this ad for? And why are they
going to care about this before ever writing your headline? And again, then once you write it, you
should always go back and take a look at like, who did I write this for? And again, are they going
to care about this?

1.How to finalize your ad ideas - ️ Evolve Copywriting NEW Evolve
Alright, so in this module, I want to make a video to talk about how to finalize and how to
crystallize your idea, and that is all going to be done through this breakthrough memo process,
okay? And I want to be honest up front that this does take more work, okay? When you are
genuinely thinking through an idea and you are coming up with an idea for an ad, a lot of the time
can prevent a lot of the failure by just doing more work up front, okay? And so the reason why I
want to zoom in on this, and as much as I wish I could do this for one of our brands because
that's currently what I'm doing with writing our ads, but all three of our brands at this time are
asking me to not show them. So that means that I'm going to have to go and just make ads for
random brands to show you guys some of the examples that you'll see down below. But what I
wanted to do in this video was at least just show you how this idea should be brought to life,
okay? And so it starts with obviously the angle, the sub-avatar design, all that kind of stuff.
But what I really want to get into is the breakthrough memo process, okay? And as you can see
here, what this starts off with is why are we making this ad, what is this ad going to be about, and
how are we going to make it, okay? So when you follow this process of why, what, and how, this
is how you get ideas fully finalized and fully fleshed out. And it'll help you realize whether or not
this is actually a good idea, okay? And the reason why I like doing it this way is because when I
have my team members pitch me an idea, they're not half-baked ideas of like, oh, I think we
should do an us versus them. But it's like an us versus them, but what? And why should we do an
us versus them? Have you thought about that? And then most of the time they're like, I don't
know, I saw an ad and then I thought if we should do it.
It's like, that's the surface level stuff that I'm trying to avoid, that this breakthrough memo process
will allow you to actually think through. And again, because you should be going very deep to
know everything that you need to know about your product, about your avatars, about your
category, about everything in general. And now you have the principles and everything from the
copywriting program.
You're going to come into this with more ideas, at least you should have, and you can start to
explain why you want to come up with the ad idea a lot more clearly and also explain what is
going to go in it. So for example, we have this ad called Negative Framing that spent close to
$280,000, okay? Now we have this new ad from X person that's doing really well and then did
well again with a UGC iteration too. So what I was thinking is if we could blend the two of these
together, so this negative framing to this new specific angle that we've unlocked or that this other
person unlocked, I think if we blend these two together, it could crush.
That is my why. And you have to understand that me as like a, you know, a growth strategist or
someone operating a creative strategy team, if somebody presents this idea to me, I'm like, okay,
I'm interested. That makes sense.

It's, it's logical. We've got an ad that's doing well. We've got another ad that's doing well.
We want to blend them together. Right? Makes sense. Okay.
What is, what are we going to make the ad about? Well, what, instead of talking about features,
we lead with what our product prevents, okay? And he gave an example of how it would look. If
you are sub avatar, this unique mechanism won't do X every time Y happens, which will not
cause this thing that you are scared of. Okay? And I like this because again, it's negative framing.
Now he's showing how this would be brought to life. He's showing the what, right? And then he
explains that basically we're going to bring in all of the negative outcomes from each of the other
solutions mentioned and say that this product won't bring that outcome. Okay? So we're
positioning this in a way, and we're telling the story in a way where it's like, Hey, these other
things that you are doing, which is where we got this idea from.
So this thing was basically explaining these other solutions, but what we wanted to do is we
wanted to add a negative frame to it. So that is how, that is the why, right? The what is, here's
what it would potentially look like. And then the how is this video will be very similar to the one
linked here.
And then he links an example of the ad, right? So this is how you go through and you crystallize
and finalize your ideas by actually fully thinking through the idea and explain why you think it's a
good idea, what the actual idea is about and how it will actually look. And this will take your team
and take you from saying like, Oh, let me do an us versus them ad. That's just how, that's just
how we should do the ad.
But that's not what, and that's not why, like what is the us versus them about and why should we
even do an us versus them? Right? Now I know a lot of this stuff might be assumed. Well, it's
like, Oh, because I want to compare to, you know, this specific thing, but great. Just put it down
and say it.
Because then what I've realized is that when you actually look at it, you might be like, this idea is
really weak, right? And that's why we have the upvoting system as well inside the growth guide.
But I want you to really think through your ideas and you do that by following what we call the
mini breakthrough memo process, which is why are we doing this ad, what is the ad going to be
about and how are we going to do it? Okay. And this is what you need to do with your ideas.
So before you just go and write an idea and don't get me wrong, like if you're inspired and you
want to write, just go and write. But ideally you should be thinking through your ideas of like,
here's why I want to do this idea. Here's what I want to do.
And here's how I'm thinking we should do it. And with all of that together, it should lead to much
more crystallized and much more finalized and higher converting ad ideas for you so that when

you go to write your actual concepts, you've thought about it and now you can just focus on
execution.

11.How to write ads that show and dont tell - ️ Evolve Copywriting
All right, guys, on this module, I'll talk to you guys about how to write ads that show and don't tell.
Okay, so again, you need to show, not tell, even with words. Okay, so if you can't actually show
the thing, then you need, again, and I'm still a big believer that even as you saw with Gully, if
you're creative enough, you can find a way to show specific things, okay? But again, even if
you're like, okay, well, I can't specifically do that, limiting belief, whatever your limiting belief is,
the problem still is that most of you copywriters will tell benefits, okay? But telling will trigger the
logical brain, okay? And so the solution is just to write visually, okay? Write the experience, write
the behavior, write the new desire, okay? The TLDR of this is just stop writing so abstract and
stop telling benefits and just write the benefits out, okay? So Harry kind of calls this, again, credit
to Harry as always, to write more concrete, all right? So again, this will make you feel energized.
That is you telling me what will happen. This will have you run through a wall, but I want you guys
to see the difference between this, which is like, this will make you feel energized. I don't know
what that means, but this will make you feel like you want to run through a wall.
Again, that's showing it. What's great about this is when you imagine, how could I show this? You
can show this. So you could literally just have a video, which is someone drinking your drink or
taking your scoop and then being like, and then sprinting through a wall.
You can use AI if you want to be creative, right? And I'm going to show you a wild example of
that. But again, this is just showing the benefit. Someone who is going to feel like they can run
through a wall, you're going to make the assumption that that person's energized, right? Or they
feel strong.
Maybe they're kind of crazy, okay? But again, that is showing that someone is energized versus
telling, okay? The wallet is high quality. Again, we're telling the person that it's high quality. The
wallet is made with titanium.
That is showing that it's quality. And again, this is a great example of why facts are so important.
Because if you just state a fact that the wallet is made with titanium, people will create the
assumption that it's high quality, okay? We're writing concrete here to help people see the actual
benefit, okay? Let them experience.
Basically, let them make the assumption and let them tell themselves the benefit rather than you
telling it for them, okay? This will boost your confidence. This is telling. What is showing? You'll
forget you were nervous to go out, right? So if someone is nervous or introverted or whatever,
again, you'll forget that you're introverted.
That is showing. How do we show that someone has confidence? Well, they're not nervous to go
out. They're confident.

You'll smell way better. That is telling, right? Girls will chase you around. That is showing.
And I'll give you guys a funny example down below. Let your prospects tell it to themselves, not
you. Now, I could keep going with the examples, but I think you guys get the point.
Whenever you write a line, just ask yourself, how could I write this more visually, right? How
could I write this in a way where people can see it? And again, that way I can show the benefit
rather than tell the benefit, okay? Again, even in copy. Now, let's go ahead and take a look at this

again, why do they care also goes a little bit deeper.

back and I can stop clicking around everywhere. There might be multiple angles in this that we
can pull.
But the main point of this is the main angle that we're focusing on. And as you see here, every ad
needs an angle, but you must focus on one main angle per ad. Okay, you likely have multiple of
them based on the functions that your product has.
Again, you guys can see there are some products that have a bunch of different functions, a
bunch of different angles that you could go after. But again, the main point of this is that in your
ad, you just want to focus on one. Okay, because if you're trying to cater to all these different
people, it's very hard to establish any kind of position.
But on top of that, it's hard to be relatable. And that's very, very important in today's market.
Okay.

And so that is the overview of angles. And again, before you write ads, you need to be aware of
the angle that you are writing it for.

Alright, now in this video, we're going to talk about the framework behind great hooks. Now, I

information, some of her stuff down below. Your hook has to create an itch, okay? And then you
can't scratch that itch, okay? And what that does is that keeps the user engaged.
I want to use the example of fishing, because it is going to be a great, like a very visual example
for you guys. But when you're fishing, you don't just want the fish to bite on the hook and then fall
off, right? Like, imagine you're fishing, and you get the fish on the hook, you're like, oh, we got a
fish on the hook, right? And then it's like, you do one reel, and then the fish falls off. And then you
do it again, you get it, oh, I got a bite, you do one reel, and the fish falls off.
It's like, it doesn't matter if you have the best hook in the world for getting the fish to grab on, if it
can't keep them on, it's a terrible hook, okay? So I need you guys to understand that. Like some

high hook rates, but it does not matter if they don't keep watching, okay? And so again, you want
the fish to stay on the hook so you can fully reel it in, right? The same idea applies to hooks on

you want sales, right? And so if you can't reel it in, then who cares, right? So again, content

2.Why you cant just rely on education - ️ Evolve Copywriting NEW E
All right, so this module, we're going to talk about why you can't just rely on education. And I'll be
honest, this is one of the number one mistakes that I see people make. And as you know, I'm
kind of continuing from why storytelling is so powerful.
This is where people start to think that okay, education alone is a way for me to tell stories, or
like, you know, if I just share this education, or share this information that it's going to But as you
see here, education alone is the least effective way to differentiate yourself. And the reason for
that, and this is from a guy named Jason, I butcher his last name, but Flatilene, I believe it is, he's
worked with guys like Iman Ghazi, Alex Ramosi, he's like the webinar kind of godfather and king,
he knows his shit. But the main thing here is that you are not just because you're sharing
information doesn't mean that you differentiated yourself, because it's just information.
And so that's the number one mistake where people think that oh, just because they're sharing
something new, and it's coming from them, that number one, people will believe them. But then
number two, that that makes you special. And so you guys need to understand that education
alone, essentially new information alone is not going to sell.
Okay. And the reason why is because, again, you have to remember the job of your ad is not to
educate people. Okay, the job of your ad is to get them to buy, right? Now, the best way to get
them to buy, especially in today's market is by differentiating yourself.
Okay. But again, education alone does not differentiate you. Okay.
Yet so many brands think that just by educating someone, they will buy. And it's so important to
make sure that you guys just really like, don't get me wrong, by sharing new information, and
sharing education, you definitely can get some people to buy. But my point is, is that we're trying
to make ads that are just, you know, massive breakthroughs, right? Like that is our goal.
That's why we're, we're doing this. And so just because you're sharing information doesn't mean
that you're going to create a breakthrough. Okay.
And that's the most important thing that you guys understand. Okay. And so problem number one
that a lot of you guys are doing right now is you're just sharing basic information.
Okay. And so let's just say you're in the weight loss niche, and you make an ad talking about how
studies have proven getting eight hours of sleep, staying hydrated, avoiding processed food, and
making sure you get 10,000 steps a day are all crucial things to losing weight. It's like, no shit,
Sherlock.
Like, yeah, a lot of people already know this. But even then, they still aren't losing weight. And
they're most importantly, not taking action.

And so the question is why? And before I answer that, why, I want to go into problem number
two, which is around new information. So obviously, a lot of you guys are just sharing basic
information that a lot of people know, and they're aware of, and they're like, yeah, I agree with
you. But I'm still not going to buy because of something else.
But problem number two is new information. So say you're in the weight loss niche again, and
you make an ad talking about how studies have proven that the reason why you're overweight is
not due to overeating. It's actually due to high cortisol from constantly being stressed.
And now this could be, to some people, new information. And this definitely can work better
because it is new. They're like, oh, I didn't know that.
Maybe that actually is the reason. And again, anything that is kind of new, that is kind of different,
gives you essentially new hope. And this is kind of an objection handling thing where we're
essentially just reassigning the objection here.
We've kind of called out that, hey, it's not because you're overeating. It's because of this thing.
And we've kind of shared that new information.
But again, the point is, people will still hear this new information and still not take action. Meaning
if knowledge was enough or knowing was enough, like having the information was enough,
people would have already taken action and they would have got the result. And again,
sometimes new information, that's all people need is a little bit more to be like, oh, that could be
why.
Let me go and do this. By the end of the day, what it is, the reason why a lot of these things don't
work, it's because of the psychological principles. And again, this is coming actually from Jason
as well.
I want to give him all of his credit on this. But essentially, this is how humans think. If you don't
know what psychological principles are, it's how humans think, it's how they feel, it's what they
believe, it's how they behave.
And those are the things that actually hold them back. And as a writer, you need to be able to
shift those beliefs that they have in order to actually help them get what they want. And most
importantly, in order for them to actually purchase your product as a result of the information that
you've given them.
And so again, this is, as Jason would say, if you figure out how to do this, if you figure out how to
shift beliefs that they have related to whatever it is that they want, you will be very rich. And in
fact, hopefully wealthy. And what is the best way to do that? What is the best way to actually shift
someone's beliefs? Well, as you guys could probably imagine, it's storytelling.

And so through storytelling, people are able to inject or interject or basically put themselves in the
shoes of the character and see themselves in the story, okay? And because through storytelling,
we can show a belief shift in our character, when our watcher or our viewer has interjected
themselves into that character, and as a result, seeing what happened to the character, then all
of a sudden their beliefs start to shift too. And the thing that I've bolded here that is the most
important thing that you need to understand about storytelling is that your job is not to convince
people. Your job is to get them to convince themselves.
And again, I could say that over and over and over again, I cannot stress how important this is.
And that is why storytelling is so powerful, because you are not the one that is shifting the belief
or telling them that their beliefs are wrong or are essentially like, you that you're doing this
because of your benefit. You're essentially showing how a person just like them went through all
of these trials and all these tribulations or whatever it is, and you'll see the different types of story,
the story archetypes that we'll talk about.
And again, it's that in itself, the story in itself is what is convincing the person. You yourself are
not doing it, okay? And so again, education is just one part of the storytelling process. It is not the
complete answer.
And that is the number one mistake that I see copywriters make is they kind of go into this
content writing and they go into this lecture mode where they think if they just provide people this
education, like, hey, you just need to know this, you just need to know that, you just blah, blah,
blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, that it'll shift the beliefs. But a lot of times it doesn't, okay? And
that in itself is not storytelling, okay? And so just to summarize this, I literally, I actually generated
this summary, it was from Google and I was like, this is, this is so bang on. And even in, this is
kind of the story that I'm creating for myself, right? And we talked about this in the previous one
where it's like, we create stories and we make sense of things based off of what we see.
But this does make sense because again, I have some kind of proof from it, but stories bypass a
lot of the critical analysis by connecting directly to the emotions and making messages more
memorable and persuasive, okay? And these narratives essentially allow audiences, like I
mentioned, to live through the character's experience, fostering this kind of empathy and enabling
a change in perspective. Okay, I know we're using some non-spencer words here, but basically
what this means is like, people will feel like they are your character, okay? Stories feel less like
direct persuasion, which allows people to lower their mental defenses and become more open to
the new viewpoints and the new information that you're giving. Because again, you guys also
have to understand that if you're just sharing new information to people as a brand, that again,
you have an agenda.
Like, that's what people think. They're like, well, you're sharing this with me because you want me

And again, because people have done this so much, because even then, you know, you've given
percentages of like, hey, percentage of people, it's like, of course you're saying that because you
want me to buy. Through storytelling, the mental barrier, the mental defenses are down because
they're essentially just experiencing it through this character, okay? And again, when a story rings
true and provides a really good narrative, it can be mistaken for evidence, even if it is fictional,
okay? So that's why sometimes people believe things that aren't even true, because again, it just
aligns with their reality or they just, you know, they're able to have that belief shift. And we'll kind
of get more into a little bit of that as well.
But stories help us make sense of the world, guys, okay? And so when you are essentially
providing these narratives that explain why or explain how and essentially why the information
matters, right? And how it impacted and how this person, you know, went through a hero's
journey or how they were, you know, on top of the world and then fell back down and then
managed to get their way back up, right? At the end of the day, these stories are what we live for.
Like, everything technically in our minds is a story. Again, the brain is a story processor, not a
logic processor.
And at the end of the day, through storytelling, you are able to basically, you know, have a
handshake with the brain that just works and that can change beliefs and change the way that
people see things, okay? So that is why you can't just rely on education and why storytelling,
again, just to emphasize what I said in the previous module, why it's so powerful because of all

around storytelling and what makes great stories so you guys can actually do this. It's so
important that you guys understand the principles behind this and understand some of the pitfalls

Okay. And so the first one is change. Okay.
If it's going to earn the attention of masses of brains, you can bet change is on the way. This
comes from Will Storr in his book, Science of Storytelling. I don't know why I can never remember
the name.
But the changes is every story you'll ever hear amounts to something changed. Okay. Our brains
basically don't work unless there is change detected.
Unexpected change is a doorway through which danger arrives and paradoxically where
opportunity arrives. Okay. So change is hope, change is promise.
And so this is one thing where for a lot of people change is very uncomfortable, but in stories
change is so exciting because they see the change happening and change a lot of times again is
exciting to us because we know like how uncomfortable it can be, right? There's danger that can

arrive, there's opportunity that can arrive, right? And at the end of the day, great storytellers are
able to create moments of unexpected change that basically grab the attention of their viewers,
right? You know, when you're in a, you're watching a movie and it's like, boom, change happens,
you're locked in, right? And so again, just to give you some examples from books and from the
story as well, from the book that I read as well, the quote is, or the line is, that spot he hasn't
eaten his supper, where can he be, right? Something has changed. Here's another one. When I
wake up, this is from Hunger Games, I believe.
When I wake up, the other side of the bed is cold, something changed, right? And so when you
allude to change, right, it creates this curiosity, right? So these lines, again, create curiosity by
describing specific moments of change, but they also hint at more change to come, okay? The
threat of change is highly effective technique for creating and keeping not only, you know,
curiosity, like keeping people curious, but keeping their attention, right? Because if people are
curious, they're probably going to pay attention, right? Now, the second thing is withholding
information. Now, change and withholding information can kind of be seen as the same thing, but
for me, change is like you're alluding to something that's going to change, but when you're
withholding information, just how like these lines do, like the change has to come. Withholding
information is essentially saying like, we know people want knowledge and we know that people
want to kind of learn this information, right? And so great storytellers will exploit this by creating
these little mini worlds or creating these situations, but stopping short of telling readers everything
about them, right? They're, again, not only just alluding to change or something's going to
change, but they're also just kind of withholding specific amounts of information, okay? So as
Harry would say this, storytelling is the art of withholding information, okay? And he follows what
is kind of called the 80-20 rule, where a good hook will tell you 80% of the story, but leave 20%
for you to work out.
And again, I'm focused more on direct response advertising here, but at the end of the day, if
there is too much of a story left for the person to sort out, they may not want to keep watching,
okay? And so this is where this visual is so good. It's such a simple concept, but it really hits
home is curiosity is shaped like a lowercase n, okay? So it's at its weakest point, right? So down
here on one side of the n, it's weakest when people have no idea what is going on or what's going
to happen. So if the change is too much or the information that you're withholding is just too
vague, right? People have no idea what's going to happen and they're not going to be curious,
right? But it's also at its weakest when people are entirely convinced they know what's going to
happen.
So this is the other side of the n where you can see this is low. So if we're on a scale of high is
the top of the n and low is the bottom of the n, well, it's also at its weakest on the other side
where people know exactly what's going to happen, okay? The place of maximum curiosity. So
right in the middle of the n here is when people think they have some kind of idea, but they aren't
quite sure they that they're onto it, but they're not 100% sure.

So they want to keep watching, right? And so we crave, this is directly from the book, we crave to
know the answers in the story in a way that we might actually crave, you know, drugs or sex or

So when you withhold information and you allude to change that something's about to change,

has stakes and it has drama.
As we already discussed previously in the raising the stakes module, even in just direct response,
having high stakes is crucial, but in stories, it's even more important, right? A lot of the time, the
more drama your story has, the better the story will perform, okay? So think about when a friend
tells you a story, right? Which one are you more interested in? How he went to the grocery store
to get groceries and drove back home? That's a story, but it's not that interesting. Or how he went
to the grocery store with his ex and ran into his girlfriend and they drove back home, right? Or
you could inverse the two. Obviously, I just inverse it to make it even more dramatic, right? If he
was going to the grocery store with his girlfriend, I might not be as interested in that first line, but
if he's going to the grocery store with his ex-girlfriend and then runs into his girlfriend and then
they drive back home, that's a completely different scenario that's not as usual as going to the
grocery store with your girlfriend, right? So again, I'm just kind of doing this for the sake of

example, but this is something where, again, people are interested in abnormal things, not normal
things, okay? People want to see these high stakes, these high drama situations, because that's
what we find interesting, okay? Number four is tension and suspense.
In quotations here, again, from the copywriting goat, create unresolved tension and then resolve
it. That is your story, okay? And again, hairy draw here, but the point of this is you want to make

That is the tension that you need to create to keep someone watching, okay? Between two
stories, this is more tactical strategy that I'll give you guys here. Again, we're going to cover this
more in Harry's Five Laws, but you essentially, when you write stories, you want to connect the
lines with two words, okay? The first one is but, and that's to kind of change direction in a story.
And you guys will see examples of this, because at first this didn't make sense to me, but I
promise this will make sense to you.
And then the second is so, okay? And this is to progress the direction in a story, okay? And the

But the point is, is like, this is a very powerful way to look at your writing and make sure that
you're not writing a shopping list, you're actually writing a story, okay? And then the fifth thing
down here is cause and effect, okay? So we are very interested in the cause and effect of other
people, you know, we're very interested in why someone did this irrational thing, why someone
did this unpredictable thing, why someone did this dangerous thing, why someone did this
incredible thing, we're interested in the cause and effect of specific actions, okay? Ads without
cause and effect, a lot of times will just be boring, right? People should be free to anticipate
what's coming next, and be able to insert their own feelings and interpretations into why that just
happened, and what it all means, right? We need to have cause and effect in our writing, right? It

needs to actually lead to something, okay? So the beauty of this is that when you have cause and
effect, people insert themselves into these places in the story, they insert their preconceptions,
their values, their memories, their connections, their emotions, their desires, and it becomes a
part of the story, right? They become a part of it, right? When you have a cause and effect, that is
what happens. And I'm going to show you guys some extremely powerful examples in the

3.Being aware of mechanisms - ️ Evolve Copywriting NEW Evolve
All right, now in this section, we're going to talk about being aware of mechanisms. And again,
this is so crucial for you guys to build belief. OK, so again, what is the mechanism that actually
actually delivers the desire? Or in this instance, if we chunk it down a little bit, that delivers the
angle.
OK, and so your child is going to enjoy taking their greens. Right. How? Well, the mechanism that
Grum's uses is that it's in a gummy bear.
Right. It's pretty obvious. It's pretty visual.
And people can put two and two together where it's like, oh, yeah, my kid loves eating gummy
bears and these are just gummy bears that have greens in them. So, yeah, I can see my kid
actually enjoy taking them. Right.
Next one. You won't be drinking microplastics like with death. Well, that's the angle.
What's the mechanism? What what is actually going to believe, make me believe that I won't be
drinking microplastics? Well, it's water in a can. Right. It's a lot easier, as you guys can hopefully
see with some of these mechanisms, if it is visual and you can see it.
And this is the problem that so many of you guys are struggling with, which, again, we're going to
get into copywriting about how to go around that. But again, the point is going back to Spencer's
Wagyu chicken, you'll add more flavor to your meals. OK, well, what is the mechanism behind
that? Well, it has a higher percentage of fat content.
Right. It's chicken with a higher percentage of fat content. I wanted to include one in here that
actually doesn't have anything to do with the product.
It just has to do with the brand. Where it's a hoodie that supports mental health. That is an angle
that they can run.
It's like they support mental health. That's a reason why people will buy. Right.
That's comfort. What is the mechanism? They donate to charity, right? They donate to mental
health charities. That makes me believe that this actually is a hoodie that supports mental health.
OK, a hoodie that feels like a hug, comfort, basically what they're known for mechanism. It's a
weighted hoodie. OK, there are so many weighted hoodies out there, but they position
themselves and they come up with the angle that a hoodie that feels like a hug.
Now, I'm not advertising this is a weighted hoodie. I'm advertising that this is a hoodie that feels
like a hug. Now, what makes me believe that? Because hugs are nice.

You know, who doesn't like a hug? Right. Some people don't, but it's a hoodie that feels like a
hug. Well, how how do I believe that? What makes it feel like a hug? Oh, it's a weighted hoodie.
Oh, OK. Yeah, I believe that. Right.
So the mechanism is essentially what builds belief and essentially is your proof that what you're
saying your product does. It actually does. OK.
Now, depending on the mechanism and depending on sophistication of specific mechanisms, that
will change, which is where new mechanisms are created. But the point is, is you guys need to
understand this from a fundamental level, that if you're going to make a claim, it has to be there
has to be some kind of mechanism that is shown to back up the claim and back up the angle
again, I'm using claim and angle interchangeably. And I know this here says, like, what is the
mechanism that delivers desire? Well, again, the fact that it's a gummy bear is helping this avatar,
which is parents who, you know, their kids potentially have vitamin deficiencies or whatever it
may be, you know, struggling to take their dreams and their greens, and so their desire is I want
my kid to take more greens.
Right. That's what they want. And so the angle is from Grunz is saying your child is going to enjoy
taking this.
And the reason why they're able to say that the mechanism that they're able to use is the fact that
it is in a gummy and kids like gummies. OK, and so I want you guys to very, you know, be very
for real mechanisms because they are important. And again, we'll go through some examples of
ads and try to spot mechanisms.
There obviously are some ads that like don't directly state mechanisms and things like that. But a
lot of the best ads do. Right.
Again, even Avis, where it's like, you know, they say we try harder and say, OK, sure you do. But
the fact that their mechanism essentially there, they're positioning essentially there. I don't have
this as an example here, but you guys should have seen that in the principal section is the fact
that they said we're number two.
And so even that in itself is a mechanism that builds the belief. It's like, oh, well, that makes
sense why they have to try harder because they're not the best company. Right.
And so my point is, is that you have to think through the mechanism, essentially what gives proof
to your claim. And that is why you need to be not just aware, but hyper aware of the mechanisms
that you have to deliver the claims that you want to make. An exercise that I like to go through is
whenever we make a claim, I always say, what is the mechanism that can prove it? And if you
start doing that, you'll start thinking a lot deeper about the ads that you're writing and probably
write a lot better ads as well.

3.The 2 types of hooks - ️ Evolve Copywriting NEW Evolve
Alright, now in this video, we are going to be talking about the two types of hooks, okay? And this
is very important for you guys to understand because, again, you need to consider before you
write your ad, what am I doing here? Am I going to, number one, write a targeted hook? Or am I,
number two, going to write, as we scroll past some, a broad hook, okay? And this is very
important to understand, and I think a lot of you guys don't actually consciously ask yourself this,
but there genuinely are two types of hooks that you can write, targeted or non-targeted, right?
Broad or specific, however you want to call it, right? And targeted is you go very specific, and I
don't want to just say very specific, we can just adjust this to say you go specific. But essentially,
you're going specific to call out your audience, right? And again, this is recommended for large
product awareness, you know, brands, but honestly, just any brand in general. And this is, if you
ask me, a little bit better for beginner copywriters that just aren't as good at grabbing more
people's attention because it's a lot harder to grab more people's attention than it is to just grab a
specific type of person, right? Where you know there are going to be certain people that scroll by
you.
Because like, hey, if someone's talking about an ad about, I don't know, again, Sky Attica, I don't
have that, it's just not relevant to me, I'm going to scroll right by it. But again, if someone actually
is dealing with that, then that is relevant to them, okay? And so that is much more of a targeted
ad versus one that is not targeted. And now, each one has pros and cons, but my point here is I
want to show you guys some examples.
So again, are you not brushing your dog's teeth, right? I've linked these for you guys so you can
go and see them. Some of them are also just from the Winning Ads Guide, which is why we have
them there. So if I click this, it's probably just going to take me there.
So you can see here, it's like, again, am I targeting a specific type of person with this? It's like,
yeah, if I don't have a dog, again, not a lot of dog ads would be relevant to people that don't have
dogs. But even then, you'll kind of see it down below. If I don't brush my dog's teeth or if I'm
someone that maybe does brush my dog's teeth, I'm going to react differently to this ad, okay?
And again, here, they're betting on the specific audience that is not because they've probably
done research that more people don't brush their dog's teeth versus people who do, okay? The
next one here is the cold toes ad, right? Your toes will never be cold again.
You guys will also find this ad in the Winning Ads Deck. This is obviously going to relate to people
who obviously have cold toes. If you're not somebody that really deals with their toes being cold,
it's just not going to be relatable for you, right? And so you're probably going to scroll through this.

4.The 2 ways to write great hooks - ️ Evolve Copywriting NEW Evolv
All right, now in this video, we're going to talk about the two ways to write great hooks. Now, first
one here is make a promise, okay? So whenever you have a hook and you make a promise, you
better deliver it, okay? And so when you make a promise, that is a great way to write a hook. But
again, you have to be able to deliver it.
Now, the second way that most of you are probably familiar with is open a loop or create a niche,
okay? And this is one of those things where like, okay, I don't have all the information, I want to
keep watching, but this better be worth it, okay? And we'll talk about all the different stages and
stuff like that as well. But essentially, these are the two ways that you can write great hooks,
okay? Now, the first thing is you have to ask yourself before you write your hook, am I going to
make a promise or am I going to open a loop, okay? Part of how you figure that out is you simply
ask yourself which one is more interesting, okay? And this is full credit to Harry. You know, you
guys, I always give credit to Harry down below when he helps crystallize things.
But this is just a really good way to see it, okay? So if you have, you know, context where like, if
you leave in the context and you guys will see the examples down below, but if you leave in the
context, if you leave in what Harry would call the meat of the ad, then just make a promise.
Because if the actual context, if the actual thing that you're talking about is super interesting, just
make a promise, right? Or somebody will find it interesting, just make a promise, okay? And so
again, you will make a promise in your hook when the context, the idea is really good and it's
really interesting, okay? Now, if your context itself, if the meat itself is not interesting, then you
want to open a loop, okay? And a lot of times in advertising, our context is essentially like the
product at times. And so we really want to make sure that, you know, we are kind of leaning more
on the set of open loops, which is what you will see me talk a lot more about down below.
But leaving it in the context is also a great way to do it. You guys will see examples of that. Now,
number one thing here that I want to talk about, because I easily got confused on this, or it is very
easy to get confused.
So I'm going to try to do my best of like, okay, when I overanalyze things or overlook at things, do
I get confused when I'm trying to identify, hey, is this out of promise? Is this at an open loop?
You'll notice a lot of stuff you see on your timeline is neither. It's just like, blah. But my point is, is I
want to make sure that before I go into the examples, that it's very clear that sometimes open
loops or most of the times open loops will have some form of a promise inside of them.
OK, because for me, it was it was very easy for me to kind of look at different open loops and be
like, oh, is that a promise or wait, or is that an open loop like they both will make you stop? OK,
and that's the reason why they both make great books. OK, but the most important thing here that
you guys have to see is that when you truly make a promise in your hook, you're essentially
giving the person all of the information that they need in the hook, like they know exactly what

they're about to watch, essentially, but they still want to click it. They still want to watch it.
Right. So that's how, you know, you've made a promise where it's like someone could read your
hook and be like, OK, I know exactly what this is about to talk about. And either I want to watch it
or I don't.
And again, if you saying exactly what you're about to talk about is interesting, then that's
essentially making a promise. OK, an open loop or opening a loop is more focused on like
withholding information. So see it this way is like promise.

5.1️⃣ - How to write open loops - ️ Evolve Copywriting NEW Evolve
All right, now in this module, we are going to talk about how to write open loops, okay? We briefly
showed some examples down below. I've got even more examples I'm gonna show you guys, but
essentially what we needed to do is just dive deeper on this, okay? So again, an open loop is an
information gap that the reader or watcher wants to fill in their heads, okay? You create this open
task in their mind and they want to close that task, right? Again, you are essentially just
withholding information to create intrigue, to create interest, so that way people keep watching
and keep paying attention. It's very important as well.
It's one thing to just kind of watch, but it's also another thing to pay attention, okay? Again,
another visual way to see this is you need to create an itch that you don't scratch, okay? So
essentially the content creator, and again, this is from Adly specifically, but the content creator
way to see this is we want to create an open loop or create an itch that is established in the first
three seconds, and then you do not want to scratch that itch or close that loop fully into the final
three seconds, okay? Now, it doesn't always need to be the final three seconds, but essentially
that is how you will maintain the longest watch time, okay? So when you create an open loop the
right way, and then if you just the very next second close it right away, well, the user can then
move on, right? They don't need to keep watching, but when you create an open loop that is
significant and is interesting to that person that they want to, again, they want to fill that space
that you've just created. If you extend that, but you do it the right way by building more expense
and providing more context and doing it in a way, again, that isn't boring and keeps the user
engaged, again, that is how you get the most effective watch time. Now, our goal, just to be clear,
is not watch time.
Our goal is sales. But again, every single time you can get a reader to read another sentence and
then another sentence and then another sentence, you are doing a good job, okay? Eugene talks
about this massively. And again, we're gonna talk about just open loop specifically, but again, the
job of one line is to get the person to read the next.
And then the job of that line is to get the person to read the next. And then after somebody has
read your entire ad, after somebody has watched your entire video, you've essentially done your
job, right? And if you're thinking from more of an image standpoint, potentially you're filling in
some of this information on the landing page specifically, it's the exact same thing. The landing
page's job is to get the person to read and consume the information that you're trying to get them
to consume, okay? And so again, focusing back on open loops here, again, the number one way
to create open loops is juxtaposition, okay? And there's a few different ways to do juxtaposition.
Some are obviously more obvious. You guys have heard me talk about this time and time and
time and time again, but juxtaposition is essentially when two things are together that should not
be together, okay? And as Mr. Eugene says, great copywriting is just a search for juxtaposition.
Or he says great writing or copywriting in general is just a search for juxtaposition.

That's from Eugene, okay? To quote Harry on this, I love Harry's, how he visualizes this, right?
Because again, juxtaposition isn't something that you can really visualize. It's not something that
you can see. But what I can see is hot tap, cold tap, okay? And this is again, fully coming from
Harry.
He talks about how we only know hot because of cold, right? And so the thing that I'm trying to
mention here is that you wanna have hot and you wanna have cold. Like you need to have those
two distinct things and you wanna essentially put them together. So it's like, oh, that is this and
that is that, right? And those two, how does that work together, okay? So example here, how a
homeless person became a millionaire.
I talked about this one before in the Evolve call, but again, this is a juxtaposition because we
have homeless and we have millionaire, right? And on top of that, we have point A, which is this
person is broke. And we have point Z, this person is now a millionaire. How did that happen?
Like, what is this gap, right? You guys saw in the framework video where we talked about, oh,
this 25 year old knows how this person's going to die, right? Because of this thing.
It's like, again, we have a point A, like we have this 25 year old and then he somehow is gonna
tell me how I'm going to die, right? Like that is the gap that has been created. Here's another one.
I quit the gym and lost 20 pounds, right? How does someone quit the gym and lose 20 pounds? It
doesn't really make sense, right? Juxtaposition, we're putting those two things together, quitting
the gym and losing weight.
Why does this work? Because most people assume that losing weight has something to do with
the gym, okay? The natural deodorant that performs like a toxic one, I've talked about this a
hundred times, natural and toxic, those don't go together, that creates space. How is that
possible, right? Remember everything, organize nothing. Again, everything and nothing is put
together.
Now, this isn't as much as like, oh, how is that possible? Although it essentially is like, how can
