5.The three enemies of storytelling - ️ Evolve Copywriting NEW Evo
Right now in this video, we're going to talk about the three enemies of storytelling. Again, stories
equal conflict, right? So we need to have some kind of conflict. How can you create conflict?
Well, again, these are Harry's three enemies to create conflict, but I also want to give credit to this
book here from Laura Reisz, which again, when you see multiple people who are very smart
talking about the same thing, it's probably important.
She breaks down how all of the brands that are world-class and crushing it today and in the 20th,
21st century, they all have a strategic enemy. And Harry, here he is talking about, again, enemies
to create conflict. So not only do enemies matter in your positioning, but again, they matter in
your storytelling and they matter in everything that you do around your story.
Okay, so it's very important to consider these. Now, again, these are Harry's kind of three
simplified ways to create conflict. And I'm going to show you some examples of them.
But the first one is very simple. It's direct competitors, right? This is where a lot of you guys think
that you're, you know, you're able to compete and beat your competitors, but there's actually no
real reason for it. Like you don't have any product differentiation.
You don't have anything that you actually do that shows that you're better than your competitors.
You're just like, we're better. And it's like, okay, well, why, right? Like a lot of you guys don't have
the why.
You don't actually have the story around why you're better, right? But here's an example of fabled
dog crates versus regular ones. And again, it says your dog deserves better than a metal jailbox.
Those aluminum wire crates, loud, right? Your dog hears every clang.
You can see an eyesore in every room, right? And then they flip to the fabled crate, fixes all of it.
It's a modern side table and a calm den-like space for your dog, right? No bars, no rattling, no
visual clutter, just clean lines, airflow and a clear gate, right? So again, this is not the best
example of storytelling specifically because they kind of just cut straight to the product. But my
point is, is again, they are competing.
They're calling other competitors specifically. And they're saying like, you're putting your dog in a
jail crate where ours makes them feel safe, right? That is the conflict that exists. And that is a
direct competitor because they both sell the exact same dog crates, right? Now, again, competing
approaches.
This one is the idea of a different way of solving a problem. Now, the ones that I pulled here, just
to kind of keep on theme with what we're talking about is Groons versus AG1. Now, you could
argue that they are direct competitors because they both sell greens, okay? And so I would kind
of give you that.

But my point is, is when we're looking at their approach, Groons' approach is taking gummies.
Whereas AG1's approach is taking powders. And so they're not really, as much as they have
created ads, calling out the competitor directly.
Again, as I showed that video in the module, they're essentially spitting out the green powder and
saying like, you're taking greens wrong, right? Like just take this gummy instead. So the
approach, the way to get your greens in is better through gummies versus the powders, right?
And so that is a competing approach. Another example of this is we actually had a client that sold
one of these like slide boards.
And this is an ad that I believe I broke down a long time ago. But the point of it was, is we
competed against Peloton saying like, you could bike all day long, right? Or you could slide,
right? And it's better on your knees, it's easier, whatever it is. And so the point of this is, is that it's
the same way of solving the problem, but it's a different approach of how to do it.
You can slide versus biking, right? So that's an example of competing approaches. Again, the
one versus Groons versus AG1 is just simple and unclear for you guys to understand. But you're
competing against competitors, you're competing against approaches, or you're basically creating
conflict through the beliefs that you stand against, right? This is the, we believe this.
And these are very powerful because it resonates with identities and people buy based off of their
identity, right? Again, comfort versus mental health. We donate and fight for those who struggle
with mental health, right? Liquid death versus plastic, or even liquid death versus boring, you
know, water, yoga mom, water marketing, right? Death to plastic is kind of their idea, right? And
this is where they bring this together. So their enemy is plastic, right? They're standing on the
belief that plastic is bad for the environment and we need to use recyclable cans, okay? So the
very easy way to remember this based off of what Harry simplifies it again, I already gave so
much credit to Harry.
I'm gonna continue to, but he calls this like the ABCs of finding your enemies, right? So
approaches, what approach are you competing against? Beliefs, what beliefs are you fighting for?
And competitors, what brands do you compete against, right? Question to ask yourself is what
are you actually competing against and who is your strategic enemy? Again, a lot of this stuff will
have been covered in the positioning section, but the point here is that even when you're
storytelling, it's clear that you need to have an enemy. If we look at the conscious bar, their
enemy is regular chocolate bars that have way too much sugar, right? The other one, the
example of the live translation earbuds, right? Their enemy was essentially these people talking
in another language, making fun of others, right? Not knowing what is going on. There's some
kind of conflict.
There's some kind of conflict and there's an enemy there, whether they're, again, it's a direct
competitor, whether it's an approach or it's a belief that you stand for. And beliefs is kind of like

the open one where a lot of those will fall under specific beliefs. But at the end of the day, you
can create conflict by going directly after competitors, going directly after the approach that is
used to solve the problem, or again, for what you stand for and what you stand against.
And this really will reinforce your positioning if you have done it well. But at the end of the day,
those are the three enemies of storytelling and essentially how you can create conflict.

5.Why you need to speak copy not write it - ️ Evolve Copywriting N
Alright, so in this module, we'll be covering a very short but very important and powerful one,
which is why you need to speak copy and not write it. And so this is something that when I first
heard it, I was like, Oh, yeah, that makes sense. So this is going to be very simple and to the
point, but I want to jump straight into an example here.
Because imagine you were selling a MacBook Air. And most of you would probably write copy or
write a headline that says, you know, if you were like, Oh, I need to state a fact, right? I want to
try to make it visual. It's like the MacBook Air is just 40 millimeters thin.
Right? Seems fair enough. Seems decent. That's something that most writers would probably
write.
Whereas if you think about, okay, I'm speaking to somebody, right? Again, that is written, you
might grab an envelope and grab a MacBook Air and do what Steve Jobs did if you're a great
marketer, and put the laptop inside of the envelope and say this is how thin the MacBook Air is.
Right? Pretty, pretty visual to see how thin it is. You're like, wow, that's crazy.
Okay, this is spoken. And the quote that you know, I'm pulling here from Harry, as well as the guy
who actually said it is, would this help a salesman sell the goods? That is a question that you
have to ask yourself, right? And that's from Claude Hopkins, obviously wrote one of the most
famous books about copywriting. But here, again, like just to specify it, it's like when you're writing
your ads, imagine you are a door to door salesperson.
You know, what do you what if you were selling, you know, I don't know something to do with
fitness, would you be like, this is a revolutionary fitness experience? It's like, probably not like
that's just okay, like, what does it do? Right? It doesn't make sense. Okay. And so again, when
you're writing, and you're you're thinking from this frame of like, I'm going to have to say this to a
person in person, you know, door to door sales, that helps you kind of clean up your writing and
think about what you're actually saying.
Okay. And again, remember that for your videos that you guys are writing the video briefs that
you're writing, they are either going to be spoken by a person, right? Sometimes the UGC creator
will actually just do a voiceover. Or nowadays, it's usually spoken by an AI, which an AI does a
voiceover.
And especially if you guys are doing UGC frameworks and UGC in general with people like
they're actually filming their own videos, then you want 100% want it to be written like a person is
going to speak it. And even for those of you that decide to use AI people, although we're not quite
there yet, same thing, right? Like you have to have it speak like a person, right? And then the
only other option that you have for videos is like text on screen, which is, again, still being kind of
spoken in the consumer's own voice, right? It's being read in the consumer's own voice when

they're when they're seeing the ad, okay? And so your ads are already being spoken. So you
need to write like it.
Okay. So again, big credit to Harry, big credit to Claude on this very simple, but very powerful
concept. And I hope this helps you guys again, visualize your copy and think about like, what am
I actually writing? And am I actually speaking this? And again, we're going to get into a little bit
more around these sections here of like show don't tell, because this is actually if you ask me
kind of more about showing than it is just about telling like, hey, this is 40 millimeters thin versus
like, you know, let me just show you how thin it is.
It's a much powerful way, but this kind of falls in the same line of like, hey, your copy needs to be
spoken and not just written.

6.2️⃣ - How to write a bridge - ️ Evolve Copywriting NEW Evolve
In this video, we are going to talk about how to write a bridge. Okay, so once you have written
your hook, okay, you have essentially accomplished goal number one, which is grab the person's
attention. Okay, that is really the job of the hook.
Now the hook does set up a lot of the ads. So, you know, hooks are hooks are very crucial.
Again, even if you're just grabbing someone's attention, doesn't mean that it's going to convert.
But essentially, again, the goal of your hook is like get someone to stop their scroll. The goal of
your bridge is to keep their attention. Okay.
And so that's one of those things where if you're testing these random hooks, and it doesn't make
any sense, and you can't bridge it properly, then that's how you know you're essentially creating
hooks that aren't just like they're not doing the right job. Okay, so hooks and bridge work together.
But the reason why I wanted to make a module specifically on bridges is because again, this is
how we keep an audience hooked in and engaged.
And this is how the customer essentially makes this very important decision to decide whether or
not they want to keep watching, or if they want to drop off. Okay, because once you've grabbed
someone's attention, very quickly, their brain is going to decide whether or not this is something
they want to keep watch, right? Like most people will stop and read the hook. And then either
they keep watching, because then it leads into something interesting, which is essentially the
bridge, or again, we lose them.
Okay. And so one thing that I want to call out here, and you'll see this down below is essentially
bridges can be great hook sometimes. And this is a strategy that I want to give full credit to Sarah
Levenger for helping us identify this.
But essentially, what this is, is this is called in media res, where you basically just start right in the
action. And again, a lot of the times just by cutting the hook out completely, so just having no
hook, and just going straight into the action can actually lead to breakthrough ads. So there's
going to be a pro tip that I recommend for you guys to try down below, which is essentially like a
hookless ad, as long as it makes sense.
And again, you guys will see some examples. But one of the ads that we made, which, you know,
a single ad spent over a million dollars was actually just an in media res hook. Okay.
And that's also known as if you're like, what is an in media res, we'll probably talk more about that
in the storytelling if we do. But this is essentially just what I call like a bridge hook, where you just
don't have a hook, you just start at the bridge. Okay.
And so again, we will get into that. But sometimes you don't even need to set up the bridge, the

bridge itself can be the hook. Okay, so that's how powerful bridges are.
Now, I've got six ways here. Now, again, this is not every single way that you can bridge an ad.
What I will say is I have reviewed I don't know how many ads at this time, and I have access to
again, multiple ad accounts spending a million dollars plus a month.
These are the six ways that I've found to write effective bridges, or at least the commonalities that
a lot of them have, especially doing a lot of scrolling through ads by doing a lot of scrolling
through our own ad accounts, and obviously the ones that we consult on. But the six ways are
number one to introduce the mechanism. And then we kind of bridge this back to the benefit.
I know we're using bridge twice here. So it's probably not the best word to use. But this is actually
a strategy that I actually don't see a lot of people doing.
And if you have a great mechanism, you can just introduce the mechanism right away. And you
don't even need to like really bring the product but in product aware markets like this is a
phenomenal way where if you write an open loop, or you write some kind of, you know, really
strong headline that gets somebody to stop, and then you just introduce the mechanism and you
just show it happening, you just get to the point. What I found is that this is extremely effective
way.
And this is the exact strategy that we use to make the million dollar ad. Now I really was
searching for probably about an hour and a half, two hours just scrambling through just other
people's ads. And I actually couldn't find a lot of people doing this.
So if you are not doing this, or you're not testing this, I do have some examples of like
frameworks that you can follow down below, I would highly recommend that you test this as one
of your bridges and potentially use this as one of your hooks as well. Okay, this works really,
really well for brands that have like more visual products. With that said, number two, we have
escalate the problem.
And so we escalate the problem by being more relatable, or essentially emotionally charging it.
And again, I'll show you the differences of that down below. Number three, we escalate the
desire.
So again, we escalate the desire through being more relatable or charging it with emotion. Again,
sometimes we use both, I'll explain it down below. We then have repositioning the problem.
So this is where we kind of start right away. And you've probably seen some of those ads where
it's like, you don't have this. This is not what you have.
It's this instead, again, and ideally, you need to do this with some form of an authority. And there's
actually some really interesting AI ads that you guys will see. And then we have allude to new
information, again, ideally from an authority where this is one of those things where you're

essentially having this authority share this new information that they found, okay.
And essentially, this new information is what helps like reposition the problem or helps people
better understand the problem. But when you bridge with like, and then I found this thing, again,
you guys will see that down below. It's really powerful.
And then another way that we have this and this is common in some bigger brands as well is just
setting a timeline. So you basically make your bridge like, hey, here's what I'm going to explain in
this video, or here's what happens over this period of time. Okay.
And so this bridge can be really helpful as well. Because essentially, you're setting it up and
people are able to understand like what they're about to watch and what they're about to get.
Okay.
Now, example bridges, this is just from the introducing the mechanism. And I also have here plus

6.The storytelling arcsplots - ️ Evolve Copywriting NEW Evolve
In this module, we're going to be talking about the storytelling arcs or plots, essentially the
common types of stories, or it's kind of a controversial thing to be honest, because jumping right
into it, Christopher Booker is a writer that basically studied stories, all different types of stories,
you know, myths and movies and whatever, right? And he basically categorized all stories into
seven specific categories or all plots into seven specific categories, okay? And as much as these
plots are a great framework, they are not something that is really needed. Now, I think if you're
more of a beginner storyteller, like this can be helpful for you to try to think through like the plot of
what you're writing. But again, it comes down to you having excessive knowledge on your
category, and knowing what actually goes into making a great story to write a great story, okay?
Just by writing a story a specific way is not what actually makes it work, right? So we'll get into
the seven different plots.
And of course, and as it says here, in typical Spencer fashion, the two more that I've added, but
just because you write this problem, fight, win style of ad like this, this, this has nothing to do with
actual storytelling. This is just like a plot that you know, words will follow, but taking everything
that you've learned, and again, thinking about the change, the withholding information, the cause
and effect, the tension, the stakes, all of those things, that is what is the most important piece. So
I don't want you to see this and be like, Oh, I just need to go problem, fight, win or problem,
agitate solution, right? That is not the goal.
Everything that we talked about above is what you need to be doing in your storytime. Okay. And
so as we scroll down here, the first one that we're gonna get into is called overcoming the
monster, or also known as problem, agitate solution, or PPS, or pass.
Essentially, this is like the enemy is the focus, okay? The win is this removal of pain. It's not really
chasing this dream. It technically is a bit of a dream.
But to give you guys examples of what this looks like, this mini story here is an example. And
every single one will have one of these mini stories where you could see how this kind of flows.
So for example, for years, my energy would crash by 2pm, right? I tried caffeine powders, even
prescription, all this kind of stuff.
Nothing worked for long. This was the first thing that actually stopped the crash. And again, this is
a very common, very typical one that you guys will see that a lot of people go through, for years, I
dealt with this problem.
And then I tried all these specific solutions. And then because of this thing, I finally got the win
that I wanted to, right. But as you can see here, even though you're following this arc, it doesn't
mean that it is a story.
Okay, this is very typical direct response stuff that a lot of people do. And a lot of you guys

probably know like this doesn't work all the time, right? This is not something that is consistently
that works. But a lot of the stuff I talked about above, if you incorporate those things, your ad will
work.
Okay. Now scrolling down, I do again have an extended section here, which is just another
example of this overcoming the monster, this problem fight when can be seen as again, problem
agitate solution, or even more escalated is this, this idea called the 15 curve or 15 curve, again,
English major Spencer, where problems essentially compile on top of the other. And so what I
would encourage you to do is that if you're going to have this overcoming the monster kind of
plot, or storyline, it should kind of escalate with problems, right? So I was dealing with this
problem.
And then I dealt with this new problem as a result of trying to fix it. And then I and then it led to a
bigger problem. And then I got into this and then I was like another bigger problem.
And it's just like this relentless, you know, escalating level of problems. And then you get to the
point where it's like, oh, the solution feels so much better, right? Like think about when you guys
are watching, you know, like a movie or like you see Creed and it's like the guy's just getting the,
you know, crap kicked out of him. Point is, it's like, they go through all these trials and all these
tribulations, and then they finally get that big win.
And that big win is what, you know, everybody is essentially waiting for. And so again, when you
do this, your attention stays locked in because it's like, you know, there has to be this solution
coming, but you just keep seeing all the things that they keep going through. It's like problem after
problem after problem.
And so that's a bit of a better way where you overcome like what I would say is a big monster,
where it's like problem fight, lose, problem, fight, lose, problem, fight, lose, and then it's problem,
fight, and then they actually win, right? Okay, so that is overcoming the monster. The next one
here is rags to riches. This is like the hero's journey.
This is also one that I think people think hero's journey is this idea of like problem, fight, win. And
this does have some level of hero's journey and journey in it. But if we look at this point here, this
is what I would consider like a true hero's journey, which is this rags to riches is what Christopher
would call it.
And it's low point, struggle, breakthrough, success, right? This isn't about beating one problem.
It's about becoming a new person, right? Becoming the person that goes from like absolutely
nothing to something, right? And again, here's an example of I used to wake up exhausted, no
matter how much I slept. I plan my day around naps and coffees.
Now I wake up clear headed and focus. I honestly don't recognize the old version of me, right?
And again, these are just very like simplified ways to kind of show you a line of each point. My

point with this is, is again, people, they love heroes, okay? They love a hero story.
But the thing is, is they love them even more when they're relatable, okay? So if you can create a
hero that is just like them, that went through and did the thing and now has the better life as a
result of using your process, it can help show them what they want is possible, right? But again, it
needs to be relatable. The other person needs to see themself as the same person and as the
hero for it to actually be effective. Now, there are times where if someone is at even a lower point
than you, and then you see that person actually overcome with the product.
So for example, the story of the chemo, being able to grow their hair on chemo, that video that
you guys should have watched, or that ad that you should have watched, is a great example of
like this rock bottom low point to then getting success. And as someone who sees someone
who's at a lower point than them actually get success, that builds belief, right? And so that's an
example of, again, it might not be relatable for everybody, but they see someone's at an even
lower point than them, and they're able to use your process to get success, then obviously that is
what a lot like it's going to be extremely effective, okay? The example that I have here is SpiderMan doesn't start out at the stud who has everything, okay? He starts out with a family member
passing away, his life is going to shit, and then all of a sudden he becomes the hero, he gets the
girl, all that kind of stuff, right? So that relatability in the beginning is what makes it such a
powerful superhero in the movies, and a powerful superhero, a powerful hero in your ads, and
what will make it powerful in your ads, okay? So just understand that this hero's journey, this very
low point that people are in, and then you kind of take them to the struggle, and then they find this
big breakthrough, and then they get the success, and again, they become a new person. The
next one that I have here, big shout out to Sarah Levenger, of course, is InMediaRes.
This is one that I added in, so this is a Spencer edition. You could technically start any of these
right in the action if you wanted to, but I wanted to create a separate one here just to show you
guys how effective it can be, because we've actually made an ad, it was a million-dollar ad, spent
over a million dollars, still spending a ton of money that was fully InMediaRes. So this is where
we just start right in the action, and the most important thing that people want to see, and then we
kind of go into this discovery phase, and then reinforce that win or that action, okay? So the idea
of this is we're starting right in the middle of the action to capture attention immediately, and then
provide the additional context after, and fill in the points after.
And the reason why this works is because you show the thing happen that the person wants,
right? You go straight into the action, you show them, for example, in this one, it's 3 p.m., and I
just crushed my biggest task of the day with no brain fog, no caffeine crush, nothing. Like, boom,

you know, this energy thing, whatever it is, you know, I've been chasing the wrong solutions for
years.

Now, every afternoon feels like a win, right? So we kind of reinforce it. Now, again, is this the best
example? Not really, but my point with this is, like, this InMediaRes is you start right in the action.
Just get right to the thing that somebody cares about, like, and I talked about this before in the
other section, where if you write a hook, and then you get into the bridge and you write your ad,
watch your ad over and be like, where do I get to the point? Where's the thing that people want to
see? And then just cut and start your ad right there and see what happens.
And a lot of times, especially in direct response, if you just get right to the point, and then you
explain after, and you allow the people to, like, see what you're trying to do, you get right to the
important meat that you're trying to get into, and then you allow for people who are curious to
discover more and get reinforced on that kind of stuff, you can do that. But this is a very effective
strategy for, again, just getting straight to the point and not having any kind of buildup. It's just
boom, you start right in the action, and you go and you see a lot of movies do this, right? You've
probably seen some war movies where it's like, boom, like, things exploding, and it's like, boom,
the movie starts and you're right in the action, right? That's InMediaRes, where you're not like,
there's not really any lead up or dialogue or anything like that.
It's just like, boom, we're right in the action, okay? The fourth one down here is the quest, okay?
So this is when we kind of start with a goal, and then obviously, we go through obstacles, and we

desperate. It's not like, oh, I'm at this low point in my life.

Why is it not working? It's everything else that we talked above. But again, this is just to show you

is called the Hemingway app.

you what grade level it's at. Okay.

All right, so in this video, we're going to be talking about how to prevent and how to handle
objections in writing. And I want to put emphasis on that first word, which is prevent, because if
you don't have any objections by writing a copy, then you don't have to handle them. You can
actually just write copy that's so good that you can just prevent them from happening in the first
place.
With that said, that's obviously very hard to do. But the number one thing that I'm going to talk
about today is one, how to identify objections. And then number two, talk about how to handle
those objections in your writing, and then kind of show you guys different ways of how to handle
them and how I would recommend you to handle each one.
And then we'll also at the very end, talk about the price objections specifically. So first thing here
is product market objections, okay? So these are objections where it's like, I've tried this product
before, it didn't work, or this product category specifically just doesn't work, or just any objections
in general around product and around market, okay? And so the way that you handle these
objections are, this is kind of in order of most effective to least effective. And we're going to talk
about each one, like what is reassigning? What is facts? What is because and what is reversals?
So we'll get into each one of these.
But essentially, the number one thing here that we need to understand is what are the customer's
objections about the products or about the market that we're in, okay? So product slash market
specific objections. The second one here is claim objections. So this is people when they say,
they don't believe your claim, they don't believe the promise that you're making, or this sounds
too good to be true, okay? And again, we're going to get into why we use facts or why we use
because or reassigning or reversal strategy.
Again, we'll talk about what each one of these means. I'm going to come back and talk about this
in a second. The question here is like, what are the objections on your claims, right? If you're

before, but they have just, you know, they just have an objection around your claim specifically
about what your product actually does, okay? And even then, if people have had a good
experience with a specific product, their objection is not that they don't believe your product can
work.
It's that you're saying your product is doing more than what this other product says. So they might
not just, they just might not believe your claim and what you're saying. Okay, so that's the
difference between the two of them.

either or here.

this won't work for me specifically, right? So this is the big one that is kind of the reason why I
named this avatar objection is because a lot of people think they're special. And like, because of
who they are, and what they've been through, and all this kind of stuff that your product won't
work for them specifically, because of a belief that they have just about themselves, right has
nothing to do with the claim has nothing to do with the product. It's really just about the them

You don't actually state a claim. You just state facts that essentially communicate a claim. OK,

Spencer spelling objections die because you're not making arguable claims.

usually is based off of like experiences and influence.
Like beliefs kind of start as like, internal, like intuitive, these felt beliefs, and then like, as they
become more influenced, they become stronger. And then as you start to experience it, they
become like the strongest. So again, some are more concrete, some are less, some help us,
some hurt us, but we all have them.
Okay. And they are constantly being reinforced every single day. Now, that is possible because
of the belief cycle.
And this is something that when I saw this, I was like, Yeah, yeah, I get it. Now, this, this makes
sense. Now, I understand market sophistication, I understand, like, you know, deeper, and
everything just makes more sense.
And why people believe things that they do makes more sense. And what's really interesting
about this as well is just how beliefs actually dictate your experience. Beliefs drive how we think,
how we feel, how we behave, how we act, okay.
And then from there, how we think, feel, behave, and act determines our experiences, right? The
things that we do usually determine our experience. And so my point with this is, is that if we just
take a different example here from product, so let's just say you sell some kind of diet plan, just
for the sake of this, because everything's built around it, this person is going to have this desire of
wanting to lose weight. And again, desires and beliefs are different, right? Somebody could have
the desire that they want to get rich, or somebody could have the desire that they want to lose
weight.
But here, their beliefs, I have no willpower. Well, if this person doesn't believe they have any
willpower, that is a belief that you need to know, right? Because then you can start to understand
some of the thoughts that they have, I shouldn't try this diet, I'll just fail, right? Or their feelings, I
feel defeated before starting, right? Or behaviors, I'm just going to keep eating what I eat.
Because again, I have no willpower, there's no point in me starting a diet, right? And then from
there, their actions are usually inaction, which they don't start the diet at all, because the belief is
so strong that they don't have the willpower to do it.
And then because that's their action, while their experiences, they just gain more weight because
they're not, they just, they just keep eating the same shit, right? That's their behavior, just keep
eating what they eat. They're not taking any action, because they believe they don't have the
willpower. And then that proves their belief even further, especially when they don't start the diet,
or that they keep eating the same things, and they continue to gain weight, that they're like, gosh,
I have no willpower.

And it gets reinforced, and it gets stronger. Okay. And so the reason why I want to go through
that example is because again, the conversion from your ad will live or die based on belief, this
person wants to lose weight.
But if you don't handle this belief of not having willpower, you're never going to be able to, you're
never going to be able to convince this person because they have this belief. I don't have
willpower. I can't do that.
Right. That's where the conversion comes from. Okay.
They obviously want to lose weight, but if they're battling these internal beliefs, like you have to
unpack those. Okay. And so what this means, long story short, is that we must identify the beliefs
of our customers.
Now, how do we do that? We do it through research. More clearly, we look at actions, we look at
behaviors, we look at experiences, and we look at emotions. Okay.
Because again, going all the way back to the flywheel here, these are the things that kind of tell
us what someone's beliefs are, because we can look at their thoughts, feelings, behaviors, and
actions. And if we go backwards, we can usually see what their beliefs are. Same thing if we're
starting with their experiences, like, oh, this person experienced this thing.
Well, let's go back and take a look at, well, what was their action? Or what was their lack of
action? Or what are their behaviors? And how do they feel? And what are the thoughts that
they're thinking? And like, once you start to unpack this, then you can really get down to their
beliefs. Okay. And so again, when you do research to look at actions, behaviors, experiences,
and emotions, you're going to be able to identify it.

All right, in this video, we're talking about how to raise the stakes, okay? Why do we need to raise
the stakes in our ads? Well, because people care more when there is more at stake, okay? So
what is a stake? Again, it's not the steak that you eat, but a stake is essentially like what you
stand to gain or to lose, okay? And so the bigger the potential gain or loss, the more people will
pay attention, right? To give you some examples of this, you know, game show for $10,000
versus a game show for $5,000,000 is high stakes. There is just way more to gain in a
$5,000,000 game show versus one that's worth $10,000. Obviously, $10,000 is still a lot of
money, but $5,000,000, that is insane.

9.Why you need to understand authority - ️ Evolve Copywriting NEW
Alright, so in this module, we are going to be going through why you need to understand
authority. And to be honest, like all the other ones above, this one is extremely important. And if
I'm honest with you, if I look at the brands that scale the hardest, authority is very prevalent in all
of them because of how important belief is.
And when it comes to strengthening belief, authority is by far one of the best ways and clearest
ways to, you know, build believability in customers to get them to actually buy your thing and to
make sure that your ad actually converts. So many of you guys don't apply any of the stuff that
we talked above. And then on top of that, you don't have any authority in your ads, which is
exactly why your ads are failing.
Okay. And so I have a quote here, again, usually always love to kick things off with that. But if
you are not establishing yourself as an authority in your market, someone else will.
And obviously, if that someone else does, they're going to win. And this is a, this is just a joke, by
the way. I tried to come up with a quote for authority because I didn't actually have one.
But at the end of the day, this is a true statement, right? If you're not actually establishing yourself
as an authority in your market, someone else is going to, and again, they're probably going to
beat you. Okay. And so again, authority has one job in your ad, and that is to build absolute
conviction in your potential customer or in your prospect, as good old Eugene says.
Okay. And we're going to get into a lot of other ways of how to build conviction as well. But
authority is one of the best ways.
And I feel like honestly, you have to have some form of authority. And what I like about this is this
is not going to talk about just having a doctor in your ad, right? Or having, you know, a vet to talk
about your pet product. Like we're going to get into basically every single way that I can think of.
And I've spent hours just preparing for this training, not hours, honestly, it's been years of just
studying ads, but kind of trying to put all of my findings together and doing deeper research to put
everything in here for you guys. Okay. And so the first thing that we need to talk about before we
get into how to build authority or the different types of authority that you can have in your ad, we
need to talk about the levels of authority.
Okay. So level one is you are the authority. Okay.
Whenever your ads have an authority in it, or like, again, has a person that has personally done
the thing. Again, for example, if I have personally scaled to 100k per day, you're probably going to
believe me on how to scale to 100k per day. If I put on 30 pounds of muscle at 45 years old,
you're probably going to listen to me about how to build muscle as you get older, right? My point

with this is, is that if the person who is talking has done the thing, they are going to be seen as
the strongest level of an authority, right? If Brad Pitt was talking to you about how to be an actor,
you're probably going to listen, right? Why? Because he is the authority.
Okay. Now let's get to level two. Level two is you learned from an authority.
Okay. And so level two is essentially saying that I learned from someone who's scaled to 100k
per day. Here's what they did.
Or my coach, my, my trainer put on 30 pounds of muscle at 45 years old. Here's what he did,
right? So this level two is not, you're not the actual person, but you are someone who learned
directly from that person. Okay.
And so again, this is still a strong level of authority, obviously not as strong as being the person
who has done it. But if you have learned from that person, then that also kind of establishes
authority, right? Now, level three is, you know, of an authority. Okay.
And so this is, you know, of someone who has done it. Okay. So I heard of someone who scaled
to 100k per day, and here's what I, you know, here's what I learned from them, or here's what I
know, right? Because you can still learn from someone that you don't potentially, that hasn't, you
know, coached you or taught you, you can still learn things from them.
But to try to avoid kind of any kind of confusion, again, I know a man at the age of 45 that put 30
pounds of muscle on, right? Or again, I know Ronaldo does this when he kicks a ball, right? I
know of him and like, here's what I pulled away from him, right? So it still has some level of
authority because essentially you've studied, but obviously not as much as if you learn directly
from Ronaldo, or you essentially, you know, are Ronaldo yourself. Okay. And so the reason why
this is important before we get into how to actually build authority is because I want you guys to
understand in your ads as well, is that the authority in there doesn't have to be the actual person,
right? You don't have to have Ronaldo.
You can see just from understanding the different levels of authority that you can just learn
something from Ronaldo and use that in your ad. Okay. Now do not, that's not me saying call out
Ronaldo.
My point is, is like, you need to understand levels of authority and why what I'm about to show
you actually works. Okay. So now let's go ahead and how to build authority.
I actually have, I believe, 10 different ways for you to build authority. So if you're looking at some
of these and you're like, whoa, I can't do that. Then the ones at the end, I've put them at the end
for the sake of like, I want you guys to understand all of these because I do need to mention all
these and these are all real ways to build authority.
When we get to the end, there are ways to build authority that a lot of you probably are not

thinking about. Okay. Or maybe you're doing it subconsciously, but you just aren't aware of it.
Okay. So number one, credential authority. This is the most standard one.
Okay. That everybody thinks of when they think of, oh, I need an authority, right? This is, you

So number one, most people are content writing, not copywriting. And what I mean by that is most marketers are not actually copywriters anymore. They are content writers. Okay. You guys know that you have ads that you need to make. And so you write copy that fills those ads. That is what majority of you do. Okay. Essentially, if you do go deeper, you transition from a content writer to a natural copywriter. Okay. And so a content writer is someone who just writes words that are educational or entertaining and give information on a topic, right? A lot of you guys are just out there providing information to you, information to users, like that's what you guys do. Um, whereas a copywriter is someone who writes something that reinforces a position or establishes one in the user's mind and can influence her and can influence a behavior, AKA get the user to actually take action. And so this is the biggest difference between copywriters and people who just think they're copywriting, but they're not like, if you are actually copywriting, you are influencing behavior. Okay. And you are likely reinforcing a position or establishing a position in a person's mind. And so that is the difference between people who just write words versus people who actually write words that do, that mean something and do something. Okay. And again, the goal of copywriting is get somebody to do the action that you want them to do, right? You want to influence them, right? When you're writing content, you're not really trying to influence people. You're just providing content to people, right? Like a movie, maybe it has like this, you know, like a great, great movie, Interstellar for anybody who's seen it. Um, hopefully you have, you haven't even watched that. But my point is, is like, that doesn't really influence me to go do anything. It doesn't, you know, reinforce this specific position in me or get me to go and take a specific action. ===== 1.The 3 checks for great copywriting -  Evolve Copywriting NEW Ev.txt =====

And it really helps you think differently about your copy. So the first check the first like rule and the reason why I don't like using the word rules because I kind of like saying hey, like just check your copy. Like, it's not a rule. Obviously, not every single ad follows this and not every successful ads follows this. But a lot of them do. Okay, it's pattern recognition. And so the first thing is, is can you visualize it? Okay. The second is can you falsify the words? Third, can nobody else write this? Okay. If you have three nos, so basically if you say, can you visualize this? You're like, no. And I can you falsify the words? You're like, no, I, you know, it's not. And by the way, this just means like, can somebody say it's true or false? If someone can't say it's true or false, then that's an issue. And if somebody can't or, and if somebody else can write this exact ad that you just wrote, that gives you three nos. And if you have three nos, you probably written something that is not that great. Okay. However, if you have three yeses, you're probably scaling. Okay. And so to break these down kind of one by one, because obviously I could just end the video there. And if you've got the point, then great. You can move on, but we've got a lot more to cover here. So when someone reads your copy, you need to ask yourself, like, does it create a visual in that person's mind? And if it does, it's probably a good thing. And it's probably helping you. If it doesn't, then the user won't clearly understand your message because they can't see it. Right? For example, if I say, this is the softest shirt money can buy. Okay. You're going to assume that it's soft, but what is soft? That's so many different things. Don't tell people it's the softest. Tell them that it feels like cashmere. And as a result of that, they're going to think, oh, that's soft. Okay. Visualize. ===== 3.Why you need to write facts -  Evolve Copywriting NEW Evolve.txt =====

All right. In this module, we are going to be talking about why you need to write with facts. I'm actually really excited about this module. I'm not gonna lie. This specifically gets me fired up because I think this actually pushes a lot of you guys to really think about what you're advertising. Uh, but more importantly, it just brings, I think the best out of a lot of people's writing. So, uh, the number one reason why you need to write with facts is because facts are unbiased. Okay. If I say I'm five foot 10, right. It's fact. Um, there's no bias to that. I'm not saying it's good. I'm not saying it's bad, right. But opinions are completely biased, right? If I'm like, Oh, if you're five foot 10, you're short. It's like, that's just an opinion. Like some people might see five foot 10 as tall. Do you think somebody who's five foot sees someone who's five 10 and thinks like that guy's tall? Like maybe, right? Maybe they don't. It's, it's just opinions are, you can always argue them and people will always have bias on opinions. Okay. But facts are facts. Okay. And so this saying right here, I just, Oh, I love, I love the saying so much and you guys will know exactly who this is from, but, uh, you want to turn a matter of opinion into a matter of fact. Okay. So again, shout out to Harry. Whenever you are stating something, just ask yourself, is this an opinion? If it is an opinion, just try to turn into a fact. Okay. And the biggest thing with this is again, facts are true opinions are not, but the biggest thing with this is that facts, just like how we talked about before, are usually going to communicate something more. Right. And so when in doubt, just give a fact. Okay. Uh, and so I want to do a few exercises with you guys, cause I think it's fun. ===== 7.Why you need to use unique mechanisms -  Evolve Copywriting NEW.txt =====
BESTAND: 7.Why you need to use unique mechanisms -  Evolve Copywriting NEW.mp4
============================================================

All right, so in this module, we are going to be talking about one of the most important things in marketing, if I'm honest with you is going to be why you need to use unique mechanisms. Now you've probably heard of unique mechanisms before, but in this video, I'm actually going to show you the three different types of unique mechanisms that actually break them down, as well as help you guys understand like why mechanisms are so powerful and give you some phenomenal examples around unique mechanisms. So here's a quote that I want to start off with in typical Spencer fashion is new and different is better than better. And this one is actually not from Harry. It's from Cal Milligan, who's a legend. And what I want to talk about before we get into the examples and the actual three different types is that unique mechanisms essentially serve one main job one core job to me what they actually do is they inspire hope. Okay. And what I mean by that is that they create this belief that like this will actually work that this is not like everything else. Okay. And if your mechanism does that you have succeeded, okay, unique mechanisms are what makes the difference between someone buying from you, or someone buying from a different brand or buying from someone else. And now this comes into, you know, play just because of the fact that again, like whether like if you serve them the mechanism that resonates with them, and again, inspires hope for them, they will buy from you versus someone who doesn't do that, but more importantly, when you position unique mechanisms the right way, it makes it feel like you are the only place that has that mechanism, you are the only product you're the only brand that has that specific mechanism, and other people don't, right. And so not only does it build the belief and inspire the hope, but it creates this bias that like they have to now buy from you versus someone else. So this plays in both factors where if you're not going to use a mechanism, someone else will and they're probably going to probably going to win the customer because they're creating belief, but more importantly, they're also isolating themselves that they're the people that own this mechanism. And that if you want the mechanism that's actually going to deliver the result, you have to purchase from them. And so again, just to break down unique mechanisms a little bit further, I'm talking about the lizard brain here or Kyle kind of talks about the lizard brain. And basically, that is just like your default brain is it is it wants to categorize any messaging that it gets so it doesn't have to expend energy again, if we had to think about every single piece of content that we see, it would like we wouldn't be able to survive like it would it would be a disaster, right. And so the brain has these filters, all right. And most marketing messages get ignored because they just get categorized in this thing. They just get filtered essentially, because again, when the brain categorizes your mechanism, it filters it, but it just sees it as like, oh, it's just another one of those like, oh, it's just it's just that right. We already have seen it. It's not new, right. And again, if your message and your mechanism is being filtered, it means it's being ignored because it already knows that it's like, it's just one of those we can we can cancel that out. All right, brain, we don't have to think about this. Okay. And the thing is, is that the desires are there, right? The desire is there, okay, in your customers, and it does want to be served. But your message about how to serve desires is getting ignored, because it's the same as everything else. And this bottom line here is really just an emphasis for copywriting in general, that if you don't know how to communicate to people, like if you don't know how to write messaging the right way to communicate that you can actually help these people, it's just going to get filtered. And it's just going to get ignored, because it's the same as everybody else's. Okay. And so again, it doesn't have to be better. It just has to be new. Now, if you can make it better, a lot of times if you make something better, it will as a result be new, because it's a better version of it, which then makes it new. And so this is not me saying that like, don't, don't make your product better. What I'm saying is that there's a reason why some of these people who have these sophisticated products that really don't have anything, you know, if you look at them that are like overly unique, but they just position it in a way that is new, right, they use a mechanism that talks about how it's new, or they pull and they identify that, hey, we've built this for this new avatar for this new thing, then it starts to create this belief. And that's how some of these brands who are literally just copying other people's products are scaling super hard. Okay. So again, this is just the fact, like I can't ignore the fact that again, a lot of the times, your mechanism doesn't even have to be better. It just has to be new. Now, again, if you want to build something long term, you should make it better. And then as a result of making it better, it does give you something new. Okay. Now, again, the main core message here that Kyle really hammers down, and I want to hammer down as well, is that the mechanism does all of the work. The reason why you can make the claims that you can make in that the product does what it can do is because of the mechanism. Okay. And your job is to help the customer understand that especially direct response, how the mechanism does it now, again, you can get overly technical with this and you can overly explain the mechanism and and forget about what desire is and what you're actually trying to help this person accomplish. But it's important that the customer feels like your mechanism can actually do the thing. Right. And so again, just having some quotations here, when you're thinking about writing this, it's like this mechanism is what allows you to get x, right? You're not even saying it's like we're, you know, we just created this mechanism, right? It's it hasn't we're not doing it for you. It's like this mechanism does it all right. And you can kind of like remove yourself from it where it's like this mechanism that we created so powerful. It's like it's it's what allows you to get x. And it's what allowed you know, x person to get y because they also use the mechanism and the mechanism works for them, right? You'll probably if you if you look closer now at some really great ads, they do this, they they are able to explain how the mechanism helps people get what they want. Okay. And again, the biggest point here, because I said, you know, the core job of the mechanism is to inspire hope. And what happens when people have hope they believe, right. And so the mechanism is what builds belief. And that is super important for you guys to understand. Okay. So now that we've kind of discussed mechanisms, and we know the importance of them, which I'm sure a lot of you guys already know. Now let's get into the three different types of unique mechanisms. Okay. So number one is the new mechanism. Now I used to call new mechanism unique mechanism interchangeably. But this is actually like a true new mechanism. Okay. And so when you have a unique element, ingredient or component that is truly unique, okay, it's a proprietary formula. It's you know, some kind of, you know, specific proprietary piece of technology that you use some kind of specific chemical, the example that I can give you is you guys have ever heard of the brand called kizik, I obviously pulled up this care pod brand, but there's another brand called kizik, where it's like they had this new technology that they invented where it's like, you basically put your foot into a shoe. When you're looking at your product, and in trying to find even some of the other mechanisms, because we're gonna get some more down below, it has to be useful. Do you feel like that compared to, hey, like not only do you feel like that, but look at me like I am proof I am an authority that I got through this. Right. All right, so in this module, I went to a video to talk about how to finalize and how to crystallize your idea. And that is all going to be done through this breakthrough memo process. Okay. And I want to be honest upfront that this does take more work. Okay. When you were genuinely thinking through an idea and you are coming up with an idea for an ad, you, a lot of the time can prevent a lot of the failure by just doing more work upfront. Okay. And so the reason why I want to zoom in on this, and as much as I wish I could do this for one of our brands, because that's currently what I'm doing with writing our ads, but all three of our brands at this time are asking me to not show them. So that means that I'm going to have to go and just make ads for random brands to show you guys some of the examples that you'll see down below. But what I wanted to do in this video was at least just show you how this idea should, should be brought to life. Okay. And so it starts with, you know, obviously the angle, the sub-avatar design, all that kind of stuff. But what I really want to get into is the breakthrough memo process. Okay. And as you can see here, what this starts off with is why are we making this ad? What is this ad going to be about and how are we going to make it? Okay. So when you follow this process of why, what, and how this is how you get ideas fully finalized and fully fleshed out, and it'll help you realize whether or not this is actually a good idea. Okay. And the reason why I like doing it this way is because when I have my team members pitch me an idea, they're not half-baked ideas of like, oh, I think we should do an us versus them, but it's like an us versus them about what and why should we do an us versus them? Have you thought about that? And then most of the time they're like, I don't know. I saw that. And then I thought we should do it. It's like, that's the surface level stuff that I'm trying to avoid that this breakthrough memo process will allow you to actually think through. And again, because you should be going very deep to know everything that you need to know about your product, about your avatars, about your category, about everything in general. And now you have the principles and everything from the copywriting program. You're going to come into this with more ideas, at least you should have, and you can start to explain why you want to come up with the ad idea a lot more clearly and also explain what is going to go in it. Now we have this new ad from X person that's doing really well and then did well again with a UGC iteration too. So what I was thinking is if we could blend the two of these together, so this negative framing to this new specific angle that we've unlocked or that this other person unlocked, I think if we, if we blend these two together, it could crush that is my why. And you have to understand that me as like a, you know, on growth strategist or someone operating a creative strategy team, if somebody presents this idea to me, I'm like, okay, I'm interested. That makes sense. It's, it's logical. We've got an ad that's doing well. We've got another ad that's doing well and we want to blend them together. Right. Makes sense. Okay. What is, what are we going to make the ad about? Well, what, instead of talking about features, we lead with what our product prevents. Okay. And he gave an example of how it would look. If you are sub avatar, this unique mechanism won't do X every time Y happens, which will not cause this thing that you are scared of. Okay. And I like this because again, it's negative framing. Now he's showing how this would be brought to life. He's showing the what, right? And then he explains that basically we're going to bring in all of the negative outcomes from each of the other solutions mentioned and say that this product won't bring that outcome. Okay. So we're positioning this in a way and we're telling the story in a way where it's like, Hey, these other things that you are doing, which is where we got this idea from. So this thing was basically explaining these other solutions, but what we wanted to do is we wanted to add a negative frame to it. So that is how that is the Y right. The what is here's what it would potentially look like. And then the how is this video will be very similar to the one linked here. And then he links an example of the ad. Right. So this is how you go through and you crystallize and finalize your ideas by actually fully thinking through the idea and explain why you think it's a good idea, what the actual idea is about and how it will actually look. And this will take your team and take you from saying like, Oh, let me do an us versus them ad. That's just how, that's just how we should do the ad. That's not what, and that's not why, like what is the us versus them about and why should we even do an us versus them? Right now? I know a lot of this stuff might be assumed. Well, it's like, Oh, because I want to compare to, you know, this specific thing, but great, just, just put it down and say it, because then what I realized is that when you actually look at it, you might be like, this idea is really weak. Right. And that's why we have the upvoting system as well inside the growth guide. But I want you to really think through your ideas and you do that by following what we call the mini breakthrough memo process, which is why we use that. What is the ad going to be about and how are we going to do it? Okay. And this is what you need to do with your ideas. So before you just go and write an idea and don't get me wrong, like if you're inspired and you want to write, just go and write, but ideally you should be thinking through your ideas of like, here's why I want to do this idea. Here's what I want to do. And here's how I'm thinking we should do it. And with all of that together, it should lead to much more crystallize and much more finalized and higher converting ad ideas for you so that when you go to write your actual concepts, you've thought about it and now you can just focus on execution.

All right, now in this video, we're gonna talk about how to write with labels and customer language. Now, one of the biggest mistakes that I feel like copywriters make is writing in copywriter language instead of customer language. And it's using these words that you just wouldn't use. And this kind of goes back to even Harry's point of like, pretend you're a door-to-door salesman. Would you say like, this is the revolutionary all in one. It's like, no, you'd just be like, hey, plug this thing in and put it at the sky and it'll give you internet. So stop trying to use this fancy copywriting language and instead just use normal customer language, okay? You shouldn't use a thesaurus to sound smarter. You should use customer quotes to sound more like them, right? Really, really good example. I believe I actually stole this from Harry along the lines of like, throw out the thesaurus, where it's true, like don't use a thesaurus to sound smarter. Just use your customer's language to sound more like them and you'll probably write more effective copy. Now again, your potential customers already have words for their problems, for their desires and their identities. And when you use their words instead of your own, two crucial things happen, okay? Number one is trust. You're speaking their dialect. So you're one of them, not selling them, okay? I want you to think about, you know, if you are in another person's country, right? Let's just say you're in Dubai and you see a guy like me speaking and I like, the guy comes over and he's like, hey, what can I get you sir? And I like answer in Arabic. And when I say dialect, again, the words that they use. Attention, right? Their brain will light up when they see familiar language, right? They'll be like, oh, those are my people, right? So when you're able to leverage labels and use the terms and nicknames that they use and that they identify with, you're able to grab their attention because they say, oh yeah, that's me, right? All right. So in this module, we're going to talk about how to write unaware ads. And we're going to kind of just talk about the hooks in general and unaware ads in general, um, because a lot of what you will learn in unaware ads specifically is going to have to do with storytelling. Um, but the main thing and the most important thing is for you guys to like really understand what unaware ads are, why they work and essentially get into some of the hooks and help you understand how you can kind of enter somewhat of a unsophisticated and untapped space, but also to understand that they are extremely hard to make. Okay. So again, unaware ads appeal to the largest market, but are the hardest to create, as I just said, which is why when you crack them, and let me just go ahead and zoom in, which is why when you crack them, they can become the most scalable ads in your account and convert the largest percentage of the market. So again, if you guys have never seen like a standard buyer's pyramid or a standard kind of buyer's diagram, essentially there's usually only 3% of the market that is ready to buy. Now, uh, there's about 7% of the market that is truly open to buying. And then there's about 30% of the market that are essentially not really thinking about buying anything. Um, and then there's the next 30% that think they're not interested. So these people are definitely more of the people that you can convert, but they're not consciously aware of what they could buy. And then you have the bottom 30%, which is definitely like people who have like zero intent whatsoever buying, um, again, and these percentages will change. But the point is, is that depending on how you look at this, essentially 60% or more of your market, again, depending on how you want to group these, I'm just calling it 60% just for the sake of simple. So people that who just aren't thinking about it and don't think that they're interested, uh, if you want to try to group in some of these people, you can. These are people who are sitting in this unaware stage. Okay. And our ads target people who are not actively seeking a solution because they either one don't recognize they have a problem or, and again, the difference between unaware and problem aware is people who are problem aware, know they have a problem, but it's just not painful enough for them to like really solve it, which is why in problem aware, you really want to hammer down the problem, but unaware, they don't even realize it's a problem. And then the second thing is they don't realize a new way to satisfy their specific desire. Okay. And so these are the people to quote Eugene, uh, who are logical prospects for your product and yet in their own minds are hundreds of miles away from accepting that product. Okay. So essentially by identifying with our prospect in the hook, they will feel as they are watching or reading a piece of content and through that content, become aware of the problem that exists or become aware of a way to satisfy the desire that they are not aware of. Although I think there still are some unaware ads that you're, they're kind of clear that they're ads, but they still function in an unaware way, but essentially it just doesn't look, feel, or sound like an ad it more looks like an educational or entertaining piece of content. It's a story that someone is sharing or information that people just genuinely want to consume. Um, but a really big important thing here is camouflage doesn't mean unaware. There are ways that you can camouflage ads that are not unaware. Okay. There are definitely camouflage ads that can start problem aware. Um, the difference is essentially what you're camouflaging around. Again, this is just one-on-one from Eugene Schwartz. Um, and the most important thing for you guys to understand is that when it comes down to where ads price doesn't mean anything to them yet, uh, the name of your product doesn't mean anything to them yet. And what your product does, what benefit it provides, the claims that you make, they don't care about any of that because again, they're not consciously thinking of solving a problem or, you know, they're not actively looking for solutions. They don't care about the product. Okay. And so with that in mind, okay, when you're starting unaware ads, you essentially have to forget about your product and concentrate just on your potential customer or as Eugene quotes it, uh, the prospect's identity. ===== 4.The 2 ways to write great hooks -  Evolve Copywriting NEW Evolv.txt =====

============================================================

All right, now in this video, we're gonna talk about the two ways to write great hooks. Now, first one here is make a promise, okay? So whenever you have a hook and you make a promise, you better deliver it, okay? And so when you make a promise, that is a great way to write a hook, but again, you have to be able to deliver it. Now, the second way that most of you are probably familiar with is open a loop or create an itch. Okay? And this is one of those things where like, okay, I don't have all the information, I wanna keep watching, but this better be worth it, okay? And we'll talk about all the different stages and stuff like that as well, but essentially these are the two ways that you can write great hooks, okay? Now, the first thing is you have to ask yourself before you write your hook, am I gonna make a promise or am I gonna open a loop, okay? Part of how you figure that out is you simply ask yourself which one is more interesting, okay? And this is full credit to Harry, you know, you guys, I always give credit to Harry down below when he helps crystallize things, but this is just a really good way to see it, okay? So if you have, you know, contexts where like, if you leave in the context, and you guys will see the examples down below, but if you leave in the context, if you leave in what Harry would call the meat of the ad, then just make a promise, because if the actual context, if the actual thing that you're talking about is super interesting, just make a promise, right? Or somebody will find it interesting, just make a promise, okay? And so again, you will make a promise in your hook when the context, the idea is really good and it's really interesting, okay? Now, if your context itself, if the meat itself is not interesting, then you wanna open a loop, okay? And a lot of times in advertising, our context is essentially like the product at times. And so we really wanna make sure that, you know, we are kind of leaning more on the set of open loops, which is what you will see me talk a lot more about down below, but leaving it in the context is also a great way to do it. You guys will see examples of that. Now, number one thing here that I wanna talk about, cause I easily got confused on this, or it is very easy to get confused, so I'm gonna try to do my best of like, okay, when I overanalyze things or overlook at things, do I get confused when I'm trying to identify, hey, is this at a promise? Is this at an open loop? You'll notice a lot of stuff you see on your timeline is neither, it's just like, bleh. But my point is, is I wanna make sure that before I go into the examples, that it's very clear that sometimes open loops or most of the times, open loops will have some form of a promise inside of them, okay? Cause for me, it was very easy for me to kind of look at different open loops and be like, oh, is that a promise? Or wait, or is that an open loop? Like, they both will make you stop, okay? And that's the reason why they both may create hooks, okay? But the most important thing here that you guys have to see is that when you truly make a promise in your hook, you're essentially giving the person all of the information that they need in the hook. Like, they know exactly what they're about to watch, essentially, but they still want to click it. They still wanna watch it, right? So that's how you know you've made a promise, where it's like someone could read your hook and be like, okay, I know exactly what this is about to talk about, and either I wanna watch it or I don't. And again, if you saying exactly what you're about to talk about is interesting, then that's essentially making a promise, okay? An open loop or opening a loop is more focused on like withholding information. So see it this way as like, promise, here's everything, and then open loop is like, what's gonna happen? You're kind of withholding that information. Like, I'm just gonna give you a little bit of information, meaning, again, we don't give enough for people to fully understand what's going on. And so because of that, open loops are a very strong way to keep people watching and have them listen to what you're saying, okay? So make a promise, I'm gonna start here. So the first one is, he covered his floor with 100,000 pennies, okay? Again, by reading this hook, you're like, okay, either I wanna watch this or I don't, right? There's no like information being withheld. Now, in the actual title itself, and I want you guys to see this, right? 135 million views, okay? So he covered his floor with 100,000 pennies, here's what it looks like now. Again, this here's what it looks like now is essentially a bit of an open loop because you're like, why? I wonder what it looks like, right? So again, there are always going to be a lot of these strategies kind of being combined. And I talk about that down below as well. But the main thing here is like, listen, you promise someone that they are going to see 100,000 pennies on a floor, okay? So if I'm going to watch this, I better see 100,000 pennies. If not, you've broken my trust and I'm just going to scroll, right? So a different kind of like open loop or a more open loop version, something that doesn't have like a strong promise is you'll never believe what he covered his floor with, okay? Now in that instance, I could be covering my floor with anything, but because I covered it with 100,000 pennies, it's obviously a little bit more interesting, right? Because why? Because nobody covers their floor with 100,000 pennies. If I said, you know, again, this is an example of context being interesting versus context being boring. If I said he covered his floor with hardwood, nobody would care, right? Because it's just like everybody does that. But if I was trying to potentially sell hardwood, you'd be like, you'll never believe what he covered his floor with, right? I know what's going to happen. I know that Lucy was clearly like not really happy, was a sad dog. And now after meeting the pack, she's obviously living a better life, right? And she's much happier and obviously she's doing better. And so I know what I'm going to get, but I still am very interested and I want to see what's going on with Lucy. Look how cute she is, right? You can just state it and it can work great. Okay. So again, uh, an open loop, it is an information gap that the reader or watcher wants to fill in their heads. Okay. You create this open task in their mind and they want to close that task. Right. Again, you are essentially just withholding information to create intrigue, to create interest. So that way people keep watching and keep paying attention. It's very important as well. In this video, we are going to talk about how to write a bridge. Okay. So, uh, once you have written your hook, okay. Okay. So hooks and bridge work together. Um, but the reason why I wanted to make a module specifically on bridges is because again, this is how we keep an audience hooked in and engaged. Because once you've grabbed someone's attention very quickly, their brain is going to decide whether or not this is something they want to keep watch, right?
