===== 7.3? - How to preventhandle objections in writing -  Evolve Copy.txt =====
BESTAND: 7.3⃣ - How to preventhandle objections in writing -  Evolve Copy.mp4
============================================================

All right. So in this video, we're going to be talking about how to prevent and how to handle objections in writing. And I want to put emphasis on that first word, which is prevent, because if you don't have any objections by writing a copy, then you don't have to handle them, you can actually just write copy. That's so good that you can just prevent them from happening in the first place. Uh, with that said, that's obviously very hard to do. Um, but the number one thing that I'm going to talk about today is one, how to identify objections. And then number two, talk about how to handle those objections in your writing. And then kind of show you guys different ways of how to handle them and how I would recommend you to handle each one. Uh, and then we'll also at the very end, talk about, uh, the price objections specifically. So, uh, first thing here is product market objections. Okay. So these are objections where it's like, I've tried this product before it didn't work or this product category specifically just doesn't work or just any objections in general around product and around market. Okay. And so the way that you handle these objections are, this is kind of an order of most effective to least effective. And we're going to talk about each one, like what is reassigning? What, what is facts? What is because, and what are, what is reversals? So we'll get into each one of these. Um, but essentially the number one thing here that we need to understand is what are the customer's objections about the products or about the market that we're in? Okay. So product slash market specific objections. The second one here is claim objections. So this is people when they say don't, you know, they don't believe your claim. They don't believe the promise that you're making or this sounds too good to be true. Okay. And again, we're going to get into why we use facts or why we use because, um, or reassigning reversal strategy. Again, we'll talk about what each one of these means. I'm going to come back and talk about this in a second. The question here is like, what are the objections on your claims, right? If you're making a claim, they may have not heard of your product before, right. Or of your category before. Um, but they have just, you know, they're, they just have an objection around your claim specifically about what your product actually does. Okay. And even then, if people have had a good experience with a specific product, uh, their objection is not that they don't believe your product can work. It's that you're saying your product is doing more than what this other product says. So they might not just, they just might not believe your claim and what you're saying. Okay. So that's the difference between the two of them. And then the third one here is avatar specific objections. So I was trying to think of a different name for this, where it's like, this really essentially is belief objections. So avatar slash belief can work either or here, but essentially this is when people are like, well, I, you know, I'm not that type of person or like this won't work for me specifically. Right. So this is the big one that is kind of the reason why I named this avatar objection is because a lot of people think they're special and like, because of who they are and what they've been through and all this kind of stuff that, um, your product won't work for them specifically because of a belief that they have just about themselves, right. Has nothing to do with the claim has nothing to do with the product. Uh, it's really just about the them and themselves. Right. Um, so again, what are their personal objections and their, their own beliefs? Right. So for example, you know, if you were trying to sell me on, I don't know, something to do with building your car, right? I could believe that your product is going to work, but my objection is, is like, I shouldn't be anywhere near fixing a car because I don't know anything about it. Right. And so the thing is, this is where my personal objection would essentially come in. Okay. So these are, again, our avatar and belief based objections. Okay. Uh, so I will come back to these, but what we need to get into now is essentially talking about how to handle the objections. Okay. And so before we get into like the individual strategies, I want to just talk about sophistication level objections as well, because again, depending on the stage that you're in, people are just going to have generalized objections. So even if you guys aren't potentially thinking through or doing the research, which you should be to identify the specific objections that your customer has, you at least need to understand that if you're in stages three to four or in stage five, which most of you are, there are just standard objections that will exist based off of the market sophistication. Okay. And so stage one to two, the main objection is, does this even work? Right. So we have a brand right now, which is, you know, a relatively new product. And again, due to like the infancy of the market, we need to kind of handle the objection with direct proof and clear mechanism explanation that an authority, of course, and everything that we talk about that this product actually does work, it's a new product. People haven't really seen it before, but it does work. Okay. Uh, stage three to four is how is yours different? Right. Again, these people have tried ship before the objection is no longer, if the product will work. It's now a matter of like, I don't believe these types of products work. Right. It's they have this kind of preconceived, you know, based on the experience that they've been through, they have a belief around the product specifically. Right. And so you have to handle this with new mechanisms and authority to essentially reframe why past failures weren't their fault. Again, we're going to talk about how you do that through because or through reassigning. Um, but essentially you need to handle the objection of like, well, why is like this, this isn't any different than what I've tried before. Either did or didn't work. Right. And so we need to understand that. Uh, and then here we have stage five, which this isn't for me. So now again, when you are in the most sophisticated markets and we're selling, you know, like for example, potentially shoes or apparel or jewelry, right. Um, people will have the objection of like, well, that's just not for me. Like that's not my style. Right. There's no objection on whether or not your shoes are going to fit, right. Or your shoes are going to help you walk. Right. And there's no objection around like how potentially yours is different than other shoes. Obviously if you have something unique about your shoes, like Kizik, for example, it's a great, you'll, you'll see them down below in the show. Don't tell module how their new mechanism works and how their shoes are different to solve a problem. But essentially you need to really lean into the identity marketing here. Because again, people who are in stage five, they're just skeptical of everyone and everything. And the objection is no longer if the product does the job or any kind of like new mechanism or things like that. It's mainly about making a copy about who they are. And essentially what your product means. And this is where a lot of positioning kind of comes into play. But the end of the day, stage five is really about this. You know, this isn't for me. Like that is the objection. I'm not, you know, this kind of person, essentially. So those are just the sophistication stages. So these are like general objections that you guys will just deal with in each sophistication stage. But now getting into like how to actually handle objections. The first one here is going to be writing with facts. Okay. And again, giving full credit to Haley. And again, giving full credit to Harry here. And I believe he might've got this from Claude Hopkins, but essentially people can easily deny your claims and say, that's not true or that is true. Right. But when you simply state a fact that imply a claim, you allow the prospect to kind of create their own assumption and basically form their own opinions. Right. You don't actually state a claim. You just state facts that essentially communicate a claim. Okay. And so when you write with matters of facts or matter of facts over matter of opinion, typical Spencer spelling objections die because you're not making arguable claims. If you guys remember the ad that I believe I showed from New Balance, which was like worn by super models in London and dads in Ohio, it's like, what are you going to argue with about that? Like the fact that it's weird. Okay. Maybe. But at the end of the day, they just stated a fact. They didn't make any claim. They are essentially stating facts that allow you to make your own claim. Like, wow, they're very diverse. Our brand is people want to look fashionable and also for people who just want to be comfortable. Right. So your brain just starts to make all of these claims rather than New Balance saying the claims themselves. Okay. The question to ask yourself is can they prove you wrong? Right. So when you're writing copy, if you say, Oh, I have delicious cookies. It's like, yeah, they can definitely prove you wrong. It's like says who you, of course you're going to say your cookies are delicious. But if you say that our cookies have sold out 43 times, well, those, what does the assumption that someone make? Those must be some damn good cookies. Right. And even then for, for care pods, some of our best ads were, Hey, this is sold out, you know, seven or eight different times now. And again, people who have not even heard of care pod before, like, why is this sold out eight times like this? This must be pretty good. Right. So again, when we write with facts, we essentially are able to dodge and, and again, prevent objections. Now the biggest thing here is obviously some people write facts, like, you know, we've sold out five times, but then they're actually making that up. So again, that kind of re sophisticated the market. So if you actually are able to state facts that are like 100% true, that doesn't matter what kind of context you have, whether or not somebody's seen you sell on your website before. But for example, again, the, you know, this is worn by models in, or in, in London and worn by dads in Ohio. It's like, that is a fact that everybody knows, right? Because they've seen it and you can also Google it and validate that fact. So again, it's one of those things where when you write with facts, the objections essentially are just prevented because you're not creating anything that people can object. Okay. It's just a fact. Right. Now we have the second one here, which is another great one, which is called writing with reversals. And this is from Capels, whatever the person's biggest objection is, you essentially just make that the selling point, right? So you're not fighting the objection. You're literally agreeing with them and then very simply solving it. Right. You basically show that your product was designed specifically for people with that objection. Right. ===== 8.4? - How to pitch without rejection -  Evolve Copywriting NEW.txt =====
BESTAND: 8.4⃣ - How to pitch without rejection -  Evolve Copywriting NEW.mp4
============================================================

All right, now in this video, we're gonna talk about how to pitch without rejection, okay? And the first thing that we need to talk about is before we make any pitch, and what I refer to as a pitch is, essentially like a CTA is what we're trying to get at here, or essentially when you are introducing the product in a way that is like after you've built it up, right? I'm talking more about these ads that have been explaining, have been providing new information, have potentially been showing new mechanisms, although usually when you show new mechanisms, you are essentially revealing your product as well, and you're kind of in a way pitching. But again, before you make any pitch, it's number one, so important to make sure that you have delivered your payoff or basically delivered your promise, okay? And so if you said, hey, here's four reasons why, you've given your four reasons why. If you've created a curiosity gap, you've filled the curiosity gap. Again, you've delivered on your promise, because if you try to make a pitch before you've actually done the thing that you said you were going to do, people will not buy. It just doesn't make sense. Now again, obviously there are specific product aware ads, but even if you're starting your ad at a product aware level, if you haven't used a new mechanism or used some kind of authority to build trust, build belief, and we'll talk about that down below, and you haven't really answered whatever it is that you set up, and then you're trying to make a pitch and saying like, hey, buy my product, it just doesn't work, okay? And there are potentially some products where it's like, the products just sell themselves, and again, there are people that get away, but if you're struggling to make winning ads and it's not that easy for you, you need to really make sure that before you make any kind of pitch, you need to make sure that you have delivered on your promises and delivered on your payoff, okay? Great payoffs usually lead to great shares, which is why a lot of the times the best ads will have a lot of shares, is because when people make payoffs that are actually good, they will feel like they get value, they will feel like they've discovered something new, like they're first to this thing, and they feel like it could help someone they know, right? Which is why when you deliver great payoffs without ruining my screen here, people will share, right? It's like, oh, I got value, I gotta share this with someone else so they can also get value, or again, I discovered something new here, I was the first to see this, I need to share this with someone else, right? Or hey, I know someone that actually goes through this, I should share this with them, right? Again, great payoffs will cause that, okay? And as a side tip, your payoff, essentially where you deliver the thing when you fully close the gap or when you finish the final reason or you've delivered your promise, sometimes those payoffs can also be your hook, okay? And this is full credit to Adley, where she does a phenomenal job of this, and I was trying to find the example where she uses, but I'll just explain it to you guys, where in organic, what she will do sometimes is like she will show the thing that is about to happen. So they kind of call it, or her term for it is like a flash forward. Essentially, like imagine, you know, you're watching this video, and then you just see this candle explode, and you're like, what the heck? And then it goes back into like you, maybe you're at the grocery store, and you're like, or you're about to go get some fireworks or something like that, or your husband is sleeping on the bed, and you're like sneaking into the bedroom, right? So the person knows what's gonna happen. The payoff essentially has been delivered, and I'm talking about organic here specifically, but then the rest of the video is like filling in the gap, okay, and explaining how we got to that point. And so again, this could work really well, and we actually just had a spend winner. It wasn't a breakthrough, but it got killed too early by one of our, one of the media buyers. Anyways, it doesn't matter. We're retesting it. The point is, is that we showed the desired outcome. So in organic, we kind of show the payoff, the thing that's like, ah, that's funny. You're like, ah, I get it, right? Whereas here in marketing and direct response, we kind of wanna show that desired outcome first, right? Or like what it is that we're gonna get to, just put that right in the hook, and then explain what, how we got there, explain what you mean by that, right? So again, payoff here for this brand is like laundry detergents have harsh chemicals, right? And so we start this hook right at the payoff where it's like harsh chemical detector. It's like, boom, you could see it, like it lights up or whatever it is. And then he basically goes into the rest of the video. I'll go ahead and pull it up. But again, going back to pitching, that's why it's important to make sure that you have delivered your payoff, okay? So you need to explain this harsh chemical and why it's a problem. You need to explain how her poops have never felt so good before you make a pitch, okay? Now, ensure you've established belief. This is the second most important thing. And a lot of you guys might be going through the one, two, three, four process, which is essentially how to write hooks, how to write bridges, how to handle objections, and then how to pitch. Basically the rest of your ad after the bridge is just building belief. Like that's what you're doing, right? You're building relatability by talking deeper about the problem. You're explaining how this new mechanism solves it, right? You're providing proof, you're leveraging authorities, you're basically preventing slash handling objections through that rest of the video. Like that is what the hold is meant to do. And for a lot of you guys, that's what your landing page is also meant to do as well, right? If you're just running simpler static ads, you need to make sure that you're establishing beliefs before you make a pitch. Because if you pitch someone that doesn't believe you, they're not going to buy. And that's the most important thing where belief, everybody takes a different amount of belief. Here's my point with this without going on a tangent. At some point, that's why some people click the video at different times, because somebody might just need one or two pieces of, maybe it just needs to be relatable and I just need to see the new mechanism. I've seen it, I'm good. I have enough belief, I'm going to go buy now. Whereas someone might need more proof, someone might need more authority, someone might need to have more of their objections handled, right? Before then they believe you enough to go and buy, which is why ensuring that you establish belief, and again, the more sophisticated your market, the more you need to build belief with your customers, right? And the more that you essentially need to build identity, and make people feel like it's for them, right? Because as we've talked about in the objection handling process, if you're in a really late stage four, stage five market, people start to buy things based off of their identity, right? And so it's very important that you actually build that belief in them, that you show them it's for them. And then once you've done those, once you've delivered the payoff, and once you've built belief, only then does it make sense for you to pitch. ===== 9.How to raise the stakes -  Evolve Copywriting NEW Evolve.txt =====

All right, in this video, we're gonna talk about how to raise the stakes, okay? Why do we need to raise the stakes in our ads? Well, because people care more when there is more at stake, okay? So what is a stake? Again, it's not the steak that you eat, but a stake is essentially like what you stand to gain or to lose, okay? And so the bigger the potential gain or loss, the more people will pay attention, right? To give you some examples of this, game show for $10,000 versus a game show for $5 million is high stakes. There is just way more to gain in a $5 million game show versus one that's worth 10,000. Obviously 10,000 is still a lot of money, but 5 million, that is insane. Incredibly high stakes, right? Poker hand for $5 versus a million dollars. Like think about the adrenaline rush when you're watching someone, you know, just a random person play poker for $5 a hand versus a million dollar hand, like crazy high stakes, right? There is more to gain, right? On the other side, proposing on a beach, like a private beach where no one is or proposing in the middle of the stadium. The stadium has higher stakes because there is way more to lose, essentially, when you are proposing in front of 50,000 people versus no one, right? There's just bigger stakes, right? What that person stands to gain or lose, or in this instance, what they stand to lose by proposing in front of someone in a stadium, like if they say no, 50,000 people just watch that guy get rejected, right? High stakes, okay? And so people care more when there is more at stake. Now, after writing your ad, one thing that you really wanna do is you wanna ask yourself, how can we have more at stake? How can we create more drama? Because what high stakes do is they create drama, okay? And how can we make the ad feel like a big deal? If the ad feels like a big deal, and again, you guys might even get some kind of emotion where you kind of put yourself in the shoes of someone who has an opportunity to win $5 million, right? Or think about how nervous you would be proposing in front of 50,000 people, right? If you can feel that emotion, that's how you know you've raised the stakes, okay? And so what I wanna go through here, and this is stuff that, full credit by the way, this came from Adley is actually the first person that I heard this from over at Viralish. And then funny enough, I actually heard Harry talking about this too. So when you see two incredibly smart people talking about the same thing, it's probably pretty important, okay? And so stakes, the way that you can raise stakes, this is just the way that I've created. I didn't learn this from either one of them. This is just, I was like, hey, I need something easy for me to be like, okay, how do I raise stakes, right? Because that's always my next question, okay. Great, Spencer, we need to raise the stakes, how? Well, there's five ways that I've been able to identify it. First one is make it bigger. And this is escalation, right? I don't want the Kardashians raising my kids. Way more visual, on top of that, it's a way, like, it's a way bigger thing to say, the internet versus Kim Kardashian. And so this is ways of how you can make things bigger, all right? The question that you have to ask yourself is, can I make the problem or solution more extreme? Now, number two, add a witness, okay? This is when you introduce an audience, an authority, or some kind of social pressure, okay? Again, dog pees in the house, how can we add a witness? Dog pees in front of our landlord. Oh my goodness, like, they're screwed, they're probably gonna get evicted, right? Our brains go and start creating these, like, that is the worst case scenario, right? Again, that is called adding a witness, and not just a witness, an authority witness, right? Same thing here, fishing under a bridge. Cop found us fishing under a bridge. Again, even though you're doing the same thing, just by saying a cop found us here, it's like, all of a sudden, the stakes are higher. What do they have to gain? What do they have to lose, right? Again, same thing with this, losing weight, right? How can we add a witness here? Your kids watch you do the hard thing, and they can learn how to do it too. Now we've added a witness, like your kids are watching you lose the weight. That's more at stake. Your kids are learning from what you're doing, right? Every pee in the house reinforces that behavior, right? Now we're just making this more urgent. We have dental issues, lifetime of problems, right? Dental issues again, lifetime of people complimenting you on your smile, right? We're making it permanent and when it becomes permanent, it raises the stakes, okay? Cheap razors are gonna create ingrown hairs that create permanent scarring or dark spots. High quality wallets, your grandkids will use this wallet. Again, visual on top of that, it's permanent, right? We're reinforcing the fact that it's permanent and again, when you can raise the stakes by making things permanent, again, it's what's lost or gained forever if they don't act, right? That's how we want to increase the stakes, raise the stakes by making things permanent. Now, the final one down here is make it cost more and this one is definitely more on the negative side, like low valence side, because what we're saying is like, this is gonna cost you more than you think. You can't really say, I mean, you could also flip this on, like you're gonna gain more than you think, but most of the times, we have to raise the stakes by saying, hey, it's gonna cost you something more than what you actually think it is. So whether it's financial, relational, time-based or emotional price tags, again, crashes from coffee, that's something that people are experiencing. How can we make that cost more? Copywriting 101:
If you run an e-commerce brand, the most crucial part of your business is marketing.
And if the most crucial part of your business is marketing, then you need to get good at it.
To be good at marketing, you need to be good at writing words that convince people to buy your
stuff.
And to be good at writing words that convince people to buy your stuff, you need to understand
who those people are.
And once you understand who they are
You need to learn how to communicate that understanding to them.​

"The Power of Your Copy Is In Direct Proportion To The Depth of Your Research"​
-Eugene Schwatz​

How you communicate your understanding to them is…

✍️Copywriting

So, for you to actually scale your eCom brand, the #1 skill that you will need is copywriting.
Easy right?
Well, the problem is that most people, especially nowadays with AI, are mediocre at copywriting.
And people prefer to press buttons in Meta to try to hack the system
Instead of learning a LEGIT skillset of copywriting.
And the truth is, being a good copywriter is hard.
Getting good at copywriting is like learning a new language.
And for most of you who aren’t people who grew up speaking 2 languages
Learning a language is damn hard.
So the truth is
If I were to just give you guys a template and a prompt, which I will, and tell you that that is all
you need to create winning ads…

I would be doing you all a disservice.
The problem is, I've been copywriting for the last five years in e-commerce.
So a lot of this stuff just comes “Natural to me” because I’ve done more reps.
But, by being in Evolve
You have the skillset of being able to find the information that will equip you with the knowledge
of your specific customers.
However, the only thing that will hold you back from actually getting to the next level is your
ability to execute it.
AKA your copywriting
And then after your copywriting, unfortunately, there is another caveat which is your ability to
visually communicate it…
Although I will have a solution for you very shortly
My point is this: Copywriting is not a “nice-to-have” skill. It’s a MUST-HAVE SKILL.
It’s not really an option unless you can pay someone actually good
And even if you can pay, a lot of you don’t know how to attract them.
So…
What we're about to cover is just a spec of what comes into copywriting.
But I need you to understand that, as I mentioned in the first line, learning copywriting is like
learning a new language.
At first, it will be really rough.
You're going to have to try to remember to do specific things.
You’re going to make mistakes
You’re going to sound like a baby speaking its first few words.
But after enough practice, it will just start to flow naturally.
And at the end of the day,
If there's one skill that will keep food on your table for the rest of your life, even outside of eCom

It's copywriting.
So lean in to this.
And be excited to develop and learn the skill.
Let’s dive in.

The Rules Of Copy:
#1 - Nobody Cares About You, Your Company, or Your Product
The Beginner Mistake: Talking about how great your product is, how long you've been in
business, your awards, and your mission.
The Truth: People are interested in themselves. Period. Frame everything through THEIR lens their problems, their desires, their outcomes.
The Shift:
●​
●​

❌ "We've been in business for 20 years"
✅ "For 20 years, we've helped people like you solve [problem]"

#2 - Results Are All That Matter. Remove Your Emotion.
The Beginner Mistake: "I think this headline is better" or "This sounds good to me"
The Truth: The market decides what is good and what is not. Your job is to test, measure, and
let data tell you what converts. And most importantly, USE THE DATA to complete feedback
loops.
The Discipline: Never argue about copy. Test it. If it doesn't convert, it's wrong - no matter how
much you like it.

#3 - Write How People Talk, Not How They Read
The Beginner Mistake: Writing in "proper" formal English like you learned in school.

The Truth: Copy should sound like a conversation. Use contractions. Short sentences. Start
sentences with "And" or "But." Write like you're talking to a friend.
The Test: Read your copy out loud. If it sounds weird or stiff, rewrite it.
Pro tip: Use the https://hemingwayapp.com/ to confirm your writing is at a 6th grade level or
below (can ask Claude to rewrite it if needed).
Examples:
●​
●​

❌ "This supplement facilitates enhanced metabolic function"
✅ "This tells your body to burn fat instead of storing it"

#4 - Copy is never too long… Only too boring.
The Beginner Mistake: "People won't read long copy" - so they write short, incomplete copy.
The Truth: People who are interested in what you're selling will read EVERYTHING, as long as
it’s engaging.

#5 - Every Sentence Should Create a "Slippery Slide"
The Beginner Mistake: Writing sentences that let the reader stop and think.
The Truth: Your sentences should be a slippery slide. Once they start reading, they can't stop
until the end.
How to Do It:
●​
●​
●​
●​
●​

End sentences with intrigue
Use open loops
Create curiosity gaps
Make each sentence lead naturally to the next
Never give them a natural place to stop until the CTA

Example:
●​
●​

❌ "Our product has three ingredients. They work together to help you."
✅ "Our product has three ingredients. The first one does something no other
supplement can do

#6 - Research is 80% of the Work
The Beginner Mistake: Just sitting down and writing with minimal research. Not stopping while
writing to do more research if needed.
The Truth: Great copy comes from great research. The writing is just organizing what you
learned.
What to Research:
●​
●​
●​
●​
●​

✅ Customer reviews (their exact language)
✅ Competitor claims (what's already been said)
✅ Amazon Q&A sections (their questions = your objections)
✅ Reddit/Facebook groups (raw, unfiltered pain points)
✅ Product mechanism (understand it deeply)

The Rule: Spend 2-3 hours researching for every 1 hour writing.

⭐️ #7 - The Daily Practice (Kyle Milligan x Joe Schriefer)
If you do this for 90 days and you'll be better than 90% of copywriters.
1.​ Read a piece of copy (active reading - analyze the subtext - what is being
communicated?)
2.​ Write a page of copy (doesn't have to be for an ad, can be for a landing page, a tweet,
anything.)
3.​ Come up with an idea (keep asking "what if...")
a.​ "What if I approached this project from a completely different angle?"
b.​ "Why did this ad stop me? What's the hook pattern?"
c.​ "How would I improve this ad if it was mine?"
d.​ "Why did I just buy this? What was the trigger?"
e.​ "What would make me buy this faster?"
f.​ "I just got frustrated by X - that's a pain point someone could sell into"
g.​ "Everyone in this niche says X - what if we said Y instead?"
h.​ "Nobody's addressing Z objection - what if we led with that?"
Pro Tip: Keep a notes app on your laptop/phone (Apple Notes, Google Keep, Notion) Create a
"Copy Ideas" section, hooks that stopped my scroll, etc.

More Copywriting Strategies
#1 - Understand what humans value

-​
-​
-​
-​
-​

What is their dream outcome?
How likely are they to get it?
How long does it take them to get it?
How much effort does it take them?
And how much sacrifice does it require?

#2 - Understand “frames” you can use in copywriting:

Examples:

❌ "Lose weight fast" = Only has FAST, missing NEW, EASY, SAFE​
●​ ✅ "12,000 women blocked the enzyme destroying their appetite hormone and ate 24%
●​

less in week one - without trying. Try it risk-free." = Has all four​

❌ "Sleep better with our blackout curtains" = Only BIG implied​
●​ ✅ "The first blackout blind that blocks 100% of light using military-grade fabric - install in
●​

under 5 minutes without drilling. 15,000 parents got their toddlers sleeping through the
night in the first week.​

#3 - Avoid categorizing yourself with others and blending in
The Trap:​
The moment you compare yourself to an existing category, you lose NEW/ONLY positioning
Saying "like X but Y" categorizes you as an "X alternative"
You've now entered a competitive comparison instead of owning a new category​

The Fix:​
Don't say what you're "like". Don't use market category language (unless reframing it)
Position as the FIRST thing that does X, not another version of Y
Examples:

❌ "Better than other collagens" = Categorizes as collagen
✅ "The first supplement that blocks the enzyme destroying your appetite hormone" = New
category

❌ "Like whey protein but vegan" = Categorized as whey alternative
✅ "The first complete protein that digests in 23 minutes and builds muscle 40% faster than

whey - from a banned Icelandic algae" = New category (fast-digesting complete protein from
algae)

❌ "Like a Dyson but affordable" = Dyson alternative

✅ "The first device that adds moisture without making your room feel like a rainforest - by

releasing particles 90% smaller than traditional humidifiers" = New category (micro-particle
moisture)

#4 - You need to show, not tell (even with words)
The Problem: Most copywriters TELL benefits:
"This will make you feel energized." → Telling triggers the logical brain (skepticism)
The Solution: SHOW the transformation through story, imagery, behavioral change
Showing triggers the emotional brain (belief)
Examples:

❌ TELLING: "You'll have more energy and feel great"
✅ SHOWING: "Imagine waking up without hitting snooze three times. Walking past the vending
machine at 3pm without even thinking about it. Going to bed without the nightly pantry raid."

Ergonomic Mouse:

❌ TELLING: "Your wrist pain will disappear."
✅ SHOWING: "Remember that shooting pain you used to get around 2pm? The one that made
you shake out your hand every few minutes? Gone. Now you're on your third Zoom call and you
haven't even thought about your wrist. You're clicking without wincing."

#5 - How to write body copy that isn’t fluff.
OBJECTION → CLAIM → PROOF → BENEFIT
OBJECTION - What they're thinking/doubting (address it head-on)
CLAIM - Your counter-statement (what's actually true)
PROOF - Evidence that validates your claim (study, testimonial, mechanism)
BENEFIT - The emotional payoff (what their life looks like after)

Cycle through this pattern multiple times in your body copy for different objections.
Example:
Objection (implied): "I've tried supplements before and they didn't work"
Claim: "This isn't another appetite suppressant. It's the first supplement that blocks the enzyme
destroying your appetite hormone."
Proof: "In a 12-week study, women ate 24% fewer calories in week one without trying."
Benefit: "Imagine eating only 1,200 calories and feeling satisfied. Not deprived. Just... done
eating."

#6 - The Roles of Market Awareness Levels

Market awareness is something you should already understand
But when it comes to writing, you must understand that when you write an ad, you have to go
through each stage
Unaware → Problem Aware → Solution Aware → Product Aware → Most Aware
Problem Aware → Solution Aware → Product Aware → Most Aware
Solution Aware → Product Aware → Most Aware
Product Aware → Most Aware

BIG CREDITS:
$100M Offers - Alex Hormozi
This is a must-read for anybody in the online marketing space, if you ask me. Not only does this
apply to copywriting, but it also applies to your actual offer on your landing page.​
https://www.amazon.com/100M-Offers-People-Stupid-Saying-ebook/dp/B099QVG1H8/

Take Their Money - Kyle Milligan
This is a great copywriting book for those who are just getting into copywriting and need some
kind of simplified, easy-to-follow guide.
A Massive amount of this document is credited to him.
It has an aggressive name, but the principles this teaches are what I think anybody who wants
to be successful in eCom has to know.​
https://www.amazon.com/Take-Their-Money-Write-Really-ebook/dp/B0B94NRCV

Eugene Schwartz Breakthrough Advertising:
This is the godfather of copywriting books (and is the core of the EVOLVE program)​
https://breakthroughadvertisingbook.com/lp3-breakthrough-advertising/

Right. And so at the end of the day, something like this, like in modern day marketing, it's very rare to see a brand actually reinforcing position. And so let's get into the meat. Okay. I just giving you a bunch of examples and I put a New York steak there because I'm feeding him for some steak right now, but let's get into the meat. Okay. How, what are the takeaways from this? So number one is it's better to be different, not better. Okay. You do not win by being better. You win by being different in a way that occupies a clear mental slot. This is again, from the book positioning, Jack trial, I'll rise. A lot of this stuff is, but the point of this is, is after the success of Red Bull, not to jump into another example, but again, it makes explaining it easier. Thousands of energy drinks flooded the market in these smaller camps, right? But what did Monster do? Monster was different. They launched energy drinks in 16 ounce cans. Now is that better? It's just a bigger can. Like maybe it's better if you want more. But at the end of the day, it was just different. They were these huge cans. Everybody focuses their energy on having a better energy drink, you know, better products, better service, better prices, all this kind of stuff. But at the end of the day, being different is really how you stand out. Okay. Because when you tell consumers and you tell prospects that you are better, the mind automatically, it gets defensive. It's like, it's suspicious of that. You know, at the end of the day, if you really were better, wouldn't you be the leader, like wouldn't you be number one? Right. That's kind of obvious. That's the first thing anybody would think. Well, like if you're, if you are actually better than wouldn't you, wouldn't you be the biggest in the category? It's like, well, you're not. So I don't believe you. Okay. But when you tell consumers that you are different, right? It gets them to start questioning, like, why are they different? You know, like why, why are you different? Like, you know, there's this brownberry that is this, but why, why are you doing it differently, right? Now they're interested. That search for why is what feeds them to conclude that maybe you actually are better. Again, you don't say that you're better. You allow them to come to that conclusion. Now I know there's a little bit being cut off here, but again, when you communicate that difference by also being visually distinctive, like this is very important guys, very important thing here. So when you communicate that difference by also being visually different, it's a powerful combination. I know you can't see this word, but this is combination that solidifies a brand's position in the mind. And so this is kind of at the bottom here, but I cannot begin to explain how important this visual difference is because again, seeing is believing. And because seeing is believing that has a big part as to why they were worth $5 billion fast forwarding back into the meat to personally interject and provide my own example in apex, we have a brand doing $15 million a month. They might be watching this, but they do this simply by just being visually different. Okay. There is no way for people to tell at all if their product is actually better. It's just visually different. Okay. And so again, be different. Don't be better. Number two, it's better to be first. Okay. If you cannot be first in a new category, you want to try to be first in a subcategory. This is just, again, all examples to help you guys understand rather than me explaining it, just giving you examples, just makes it hit home. Instead of being another phone, what Apple did is they were the first smartphone. They literally created their own category, right? And then when they dropped the iPhone, they didn't say we're another phone because then they would have been competing with Nokia, which was at the time, I think 40% of market share. They would have been competing with BlackBerry. What they did is they created their own category. They created the first smartphone, right? And what I love about this, and this is why Steve Jobs is such a legend. It's so simple, but it's so genius, right? And we'll get into a little bit more about like how sometimes the obvious thing is not so obvious, but again, he made his own category, he called the smartphone and now you guys see smart devices everywhere. And I cannot like emphasize that anymore. These examples are great examples of that. Okay. Now, if you can't be first, right, you can't create a subcategory. You can't find a subcategory that, and just to be clear on this, that, that isn't like too narrow, right? Cause sometimes people can make a subcategory. Like I can potentially make a subcategory from like, Oh, well, you know, I'm not not in water in a can, I'm glacier water in a can, like maybe that works. Maybe it doesn't. But again, whenever anybody thinks of water in a can, they're going to think like with death, so that positioning might not make sense. Right. And so if you can't be first, you likely need to reposition what you're up against. Okay. And so repositioning, just to be clear on this is not comparison. Okay. We're better than our competitors is not repositioning. You should never ever, and I wanted to create slides that said never, ever, ever, and just have a bunch of bevers, but you should never ever say that you're better. Okay. Because if you were, you'd be number one. And if you're not number one, you're not better. Okay. Very simple rule to follow. So what you need to do is you need to find a contrast. Okay. So remember if you can't be first, then you need to reposition. Aspirin was the first leader in pain relief. Okay. Talanol positioned itself as for people who can't take aspirin, like literally so simple. Okay. Instead of fighting aspirin head on and having the same thing, they use a different ingredient and said, Hey, we're for people who can't take aspirin. And it repositioned who it was for. Or modern day example here, Amazon was the first leader in Ecom. Okay. They position themselves as we are for the customer. What did Shopify do? Shopify positioned themselves as for the merchant, which is, if you guys haven't noticed the opposite, okay. And instead of fighting Amazon head on, they just repositioned who Shopify was for. Amazon's for the customer. Well, we're for the merchant, right? Amazon has all these fees. They charge you this. You got to use this thing over here of theirs. You got to be on primeless kind of stuff. Obviously still a great thing, but Shopify is for the merchant. Okay. How about Stolleys? Instead of saying they were a better vodka, they said they were different. How did they do that? Well, they said most American vodkas seem Russian. Stolleys is different. It actually is Russian. Very simple, right? They didn't say we're better than these vodkas because we're Russian. They said, hey, most vodkas seem like they're Russian, but they're actually made. I know you can't see this, but they're made in, you know, one's made in Massachusetts, one's made in Long Island, but Stolleys is actually made in Russia. Okay. And another good example here is Scope Mouthwash. Again, instead of saying they were a better mouthwash, they said it fights bad breath without giving you medicine breath, right? They repositioned the leader, not by saying they were better. They just said, hey, we smell different, right? Listerine smells like medicine breath. We smell, you know, we smell different. We smell clean. Okay. And so at the end of the day, again, they're not saying they're better. They're just creating contrast. The next thing on here is finding a strategic enemy. Okay. When you define what you are against, it becomes clear what you are for. Again, this is from Laura Rice. This is from now, you know, the, the 2025 version of positioning, which is this one here, again, I have these recommendations at the end. Laura Rice is an absolute G. She's actually Al Rice's daughter. I don't know if I can call her a G, but she is. Now the reason why having an enemy works so well, and the reason why she named her book, The Strategic Enemy, is because changing a firmly held belief, and especially nowadays in every single market, like there's so many beliefs that are just firm. It is much more difficult to change that belief versus just contrasting a new idea to something that is already accepted and already believed is as more effective. Okay. And so a strategic enemy is this oppositional force that your brand or category stands against, and it can be a competitor, it can be an approach, or it could just be a concept or a belief. And we're going to talk about ABCs later as we get into like storytelling as well. Uh, Harry Dreis, ABC method, but essentially when you position against some kind of strategic enemy, and again, not just an enemy, it needs to be strategic, it makes your position sharper and more memorable. Okay. The most important part of having a strategic enemy is that it actually energizes and motivates your consumers and your customers to really rally for your cause because you're resonating with something that they stand for as well. And when you do this the right way, it will 100% lead to more word of mouth, more referrals, and again, more organic brand growth, which is what every single one of you watching this video probably wants. Okay. Back to the science here, the mind understands ideas best through contrast when we are able to contrast something where it's like, Hey, you know this thing. This is the opposite of it. You're like, Oh, I get it. Now, just to be clear, strategic enemy is not about hate. It's not about like good versus evil or good versus bad. It's not about you being right versus the enemy being wrong. It's about acknowledging that the consumer is making a choice. And by you standing for something, it makes making that choice easier. Ideally making the choice in your favor. But again, when you stand for something, you're not going to be everybody's cup of tea and at the end of the day, there's a, there's something called the everybody trap that, you know, Jack Trout and Al rise actually mentioned the original book, which is when you try to sell something to everyone, you're going to likely sell nothing to no one. Okay. And so when you stand for something, it makes you a clear choice. And some examples, Tesla's enemy gasoline vehicles. They make electric cars. Uber's enemy taxis, right? They sell on demand pickups. Colgate's enemy tooth powders, right? They put toothpaste in a tube. Crazy to think now there's almost like a pendulum swing the other way. We're not, people are starting to sell tooth powders again, which is kind of interesting, but the farmer's dog enemy Brownburn balls, AKA kibble, right? They make fresh dog food. Lalo's enemy. You might not know this brand, but they're actually like a kind of like a designer, a high chair brand, but essentially their enemy was just this idea of ugly plastic chairs, right? All baby high chairs were just ugly and like gross. And so they just sell modernized, stylish high chairs, right? For more chic and sophisticated homes. The final thing on here is you need to be able to say no. No is one of the most powerful words that instantly makes your position and your strategic enemy much more clear. And again, as we just talked about, when you have a strategic enemy and you stand for something, right, you become a clear choice. You become sometimes for some people, an obvious choice, right? And you can reinforce that by saying no. Okay. If you say yes to everything and yes to everybody, there's a little chance of actually finding some kind of a strategic enemy, right? Saying no sends a message about what you stand for and what you are fighting against, which again, enhances your position. To give you some examples of this, Chick-fil-A said no to beef burgers, right? They don't sell beef. They also very interesting thing, say no to Sundays. They are not open on Sundays. And so how milk does found their hole was actually very interesting. So the first step in any positioning process is to look inside the mind of the prospect, right? Like what is already there. Okay. And to do that, you need to know who the prospect is through research. And a lot of the times when you're searching for a position for your brand, the answer is obvious. And like, when you look back, it's always obvious, right? But it's actually so easy to miss the obvious, which is like this weird paradox, but it's obvious, but it's so easy to miss, especially if you're just like solely focused on your product and solely focused on just trying to sell it. And so again, it's often hard to find the obvious thing because it's too easy to see, like that would be like, it would be too obvious, right? Which is weird paradox. But at the end of the day, in this over competitive candy bar, chocolate bar market, the only way to drive milk duds, you know, into the kid's minds and establish a strong position was to reposition the category, right? And so little would be gained by just saying, Hey, we're, we're another candy bar, you know, we're milk duds, all this kind of stuff, like we're better. It's, it's, it's too overloaded, right? But what was the obvious hole that they found? What was this obvious yet not so obvious hole that they found? It was the size and the shape and the price of traditional candy bars. Okay. A candy bar just doesn't last very long. Okay. You know, you have a couple of bites and you're done, right? But milk duds are different. Okay. They come in a box instead of a package and they give kids 15 individual slow eating chocolate covered caramels, which means milk duds will last a long time. And this was something that came through their research where they're like, you know what, people eat these chocolate bars really fast, right? And they noticed that in movie theaters, a lot of people would eat milk duds because they last longer. And they're like, well, that's kind of obvious. Why don't we establish that as the position? So again, what was the new position? Well, milk duds are America's long lasting alternative to the candy bar. Right. And again, they leverage it against candy bars because then it made it obvious. Right. As a result of changing their position and saying like, we last longer than candy bars, the months to follow the company sold more milk duds than it ever had. Okay. The solution to their positioning problem was found by looking at the unobvious, yet again, obvious problem that existed in the prospect's mind. Right. The, the facts were there. They were different in how they were shown in a box. Right. And in their actual, you know, size, like visually it was different, but then they also noticed that why do people eat them in the movie theater? Well, it's because they last longer. Well, that is the obvious thing. Right. And so when milk does reposition to that, that is how they took off. Okay. And so the first one is change. Okay. If it's going to earn the attention of masses of brains, you can bet change is on the way this comes from Will Storr in his book, science of storytelling. Our brains basically don't work unless there is change detected. Unexpected change is a doorway through which danger arrives and paradoxically where opportunity arrives. Okay. So change is hope, change is promise. And so this is one thing where for a lot of people, change is very uncomfortable, but in stories, change is so exciting because they see the change happening and change a lot of times again, is exciting to us because we know like how uncomfortable it can be, right? There's danger that could arrive. There's opportunity that could arrive. Right. Because people are curious. They, they're probably going to pay attention. Right. Now, the second thing is withholding information. And so great storytellers will exploit this by creating these little mini worlds or creating the situations, but stopping short of telling readers, everything about them, right? So as Harry would say this storytelling is the art of withholding information. Okay. And he follows what was kind of called the 80 20 rule where a good hook will tell you 80% of the story, but leave 20% for you to work out. And again, I'm focused more on direct response advertising here, but at the end of the day, if there is too much of a story left for the person to sort out, they may not want to keep watching. Okay. And so this is where this, this visual is so good. It's, it's such a simple concept, but it really hits home is curiosity is shaped like a lowercase N. Okay. So it's at its weakest point, right? So down here on one side of the end, it's weakest when people have no idea what is going on or what's going to happen. So if the change is too much or the information that you're withholding is just too vague, right? People have no idea what's going to happen and they're not going to be, you know, curious, right? So for on a scale of high is the top of the end and low is the bottom of the end. Well, it's also at its weakest on the other side where people know exactly what's going to happen. Okay. The place of maximum curiosity. All right, now in this video, we're gonna talk about the three enemies of storytelling. Again, stories equal conflict, right? So we need to have some kind of conflict. How can you create conflict? Well, again, these are Harry's three enemies to create conflict. But I also want to give credit to this book here from Laura Rice, which again, when you see multiple people who are very smart talking about the same thing, it's probably important. She breaks down how all of the brands that are world class and crushing it today and in the 20th, 21st century, they all have a strategic enemy. And Harry here is talking about, again, enemies to create conflict. So not only do enemies matter in your positioning, but again, they matter in your storytelling and they matter in everything that you do around your story. Okay, so it's very important to consider these. Now, again, these are Harry's kind of three simplified ways to create conflict. And I'm gonna show you some examples of them. But the first one is very simple. It's direct competitors, right? This is where a lot of you guys think that you're, you know, you're able to compete and beat your competitors, but there's actually no real reason for it. Like you don't have any product differentiation. You don't have anything that you actually do that shows that you're better than your competitors. You're just like, we're better. And it's like, okay, well, why, right? Like a lot of you guys don't have the why. You don't actually have the story around why you're better, right? But here's an example of fabled dog crates versus regular ones. And again, it says your dog deserves better than a metal jail box. Those aluminum wire crates, loud, right? Your dog hears every clang. You can see an eyesore in every room, right? And then they flip to the fabled crate, fixes all of it. It's a modern side table and a calm den-like space for your dog, right? No bars, no rattling, no visual clutter, just clean lines, airflow and a clear gate, right? So again, this is not the best example of storytelling specifically because they kind of just cut straight to the product. But my point is, is again, they are competing. They're calling other competitors specifically. And they're saying like you're putting your dog in a jail crate where ours makes them feel safe, right? That is the conflict that exists. And that is a direct competitor because they both sell the exact same dog crates, right? Now again, competing approaches. This one is the idea of a different way of solving a problem. Now, the ones that I pulled here just to kind of keep on theme of what we're talking about is Groons versus AG1. Now you could argue that they are direct competitors because they both sell greens, okay? And so I would kind of give you that. But my point is, is when we're looking at their approach, Groons' approach is taking gummies, whereas AG1's approach is taking powders. And so they're not really, as much as they have created ads calling out the competitor directly. Again, as I showed that video in the module, they're essentially spitting out the green powder and saying like, you're taking greens wrong, right? Like just take this gummy instead. So the approach, the way to get your greens in is better through gummies versus the powders, right? And so that is a competing approach. Another example of this is we actually had a client that sold one of these like slide boards. And this is an ad that I believe I broke down a long time ago. But the point of it was, is we competed against Peloton saying like, you could bike all day long, right? Or you could slide, right? And it's better on your knees, it's easier, whatever it is. And so the point of this is, is that it's the same way of solving the problem, but it's a different approach of how to do it. You can slide versus biking, right? So that's an example of competing approaches. Again, the one versus Groons versus AG1 is just simple and clear for you guys to understand. But you're competing against competitors, you're competing against approaches, or you're basically creating conflict through the beliefs that you stand against, right? This is the, we believe this. And these are very powerful because it resonates with identities and people buy based off of their identity, right? Again, comfort versus mental health. We donate and fight for those who struggle with mental health, right? Liquid death versus plastic, or even liquid death versus boring, you know, water, yoga mom, water marketing, right? Death to plastic is kind of their idea, right? And this is where they bring this together. So their enemy is plastic, right? They're standing on the belief that plastic is bad for the environment and we need to use recyclable cans, okay? So the very easy way to remember this, based off of what Harry, you know, simplifies it, again, I already gave so much credit to Harry, I'm gonna continue to, but he calls this like the ABCs of finding your enemies, right? So approaches, what approach are you competing against? Beliefs, what beliefs are you fighting for? And competitors, what brands do you compete against, right? Question to ask yourself is, what are you actually competing against and who is your strategic enemy? Again, a lot of this stuff will have been covered in the positioning section, but the point here is that even when you're storytelling, it's clear that you need to have an enemy. If we look at the Conscious Bar, their enemy is regular chocolate bars that have way too much sugar, right? The other one, the example of the live translation earbuds, right? Their enemy was essentially these people talking in another language, making fun of others, right? Like not knowing what is going on, like there's some kind of conflict, there's some kind of conflict and there's an enemy there, whether they're, again, it's a direct competitor, whether it's an approach or it's a belief that you stand for and beliefs is kind of like the open one where a lot of those will fall under specific beliefs, but at the end of the day, you can create conflict by going directly after competitors, going directly after the approach that is used to solve the problem, or again, for what you stand for and what you stand against and this really will reinforce your positioning if you have done it well. But at the end of the day, those are the three enemies of storytelling and essentially how you can create conflict.

In this module, we're going to be talking about the storytelling arcs or plots. It's like problem after problem after problem. Right? This isn't about beating one problem. It's about becoming a new person, right? Becoming the person that goes from like absolutely nothing to something. Right. And again, here's an example of I used to wake up exhausted. No matter how much I slept, I planned my day around naps and coffees. Now I wake up clear headed and focused. Um, my point with this is, is again, people, they love heroes. Okay. They love a hero story, but the thing is, is they love them even more when they're relatable. Okay. So if you can create a hero that is just like them that went through and did the thing and now has the better life as a result of using your process, it can help show them what they want is possible. Right. But again, it needs to be relatable. The other person needs to see themselves as the same person and as the hero for it to actually be effective. Now there are times where if someone is at even a lower point than you, and then you see that person actually overcome with the product. So for example, the, the story of the chemo, um, you know, being able to grow their hair on chemo, that video that you guys should have watched with that ad that you should have watched is a great example of like this rock bottom low point to then getting success. And as someone who sees someone who's at a lower point than them actually get success, that builds belief. Right. And so that's an example of, again, it might not be relatable for everybody, but they see someone's at an even lower point than them and they're able to use your process to get success. Then obviously that is what a lot like is going to be extremely effective. Okay. The example that I have here is Spider-Man doesn't start out at this stud who has everything. Okay. So this is where we just start writing the action and the most important thing that people want to see. And then we kind of go into this discovery phase and then reinforce that, that win or that action. Okay. So the idea of this is we're starting right in the middle of the action to capture attention immediately and then provide the additional context after and fill in the points after. And the reason why this works is because you show the thing happened that the person wants, right? You go straight into the action. You show them, for example, in this one it's 3 PM and I just crushed my biggest task of the day with no brain fog, no caffeine crush, nothing like boom, we're right in the action. We're kind of right in this like a dream outcome more or less. And then we go into the discovery phase of like, I used to be useless by this time, right? That's when I realized, you know, this energy thing, whatever it is, you know, I'd been chasing the wrong solutions for years now and every afternoon feels like a win, right? So we kind of reinforce it now. Yeah. Is this the best example? Not really. Um, but my point with this is, is like this in media res is you start writing the action, just get right to the thing that somebody cares about. Like, and I talked about this before in the other section where if you write a hook and then you get into the bridge and you write, write your ad, watch your ad over and be like, where do I get to the point? Where's the thing that people want to see? And then just cut and start your ad right there and see what happens. And a lot of the times, especially in direct response, if you just get right to the point and then you explain after and you allow the people to like see what you're trying to do, you get right to the important meat that you're trying to get into, and then you allow for people who are curious to discover more and get reinforced on that kind of stuff. You can do that, but this is a very effective strategy for again, just getting straight to the point and not having any kind of buildup. It's just boom, you start writing the action and you go, and you see a lot of movies do this, right? You've probably seen some war movies where it's like things exploding and it's like, boom, the movie starts and you're right in the action, right? That's in media res where you're not like, there's not really any lead up or dialogue or anything like that. It's just like, boom, we're ready in the action. Okay. Um, and the fourth one down here is the quest. Okay. So this is when we kind of start with a goal and then obviously we go through obstacles and we have our discovery and then we win, right? So here the hook is very aspirational. It's not desperate. It's not like, Oh, I'm at this low point in my life. Or again, if we scroll back up to, uh, the overcoming the monsters, like, Oh, I have this problem in my life, right? This is saying, Hey, I have this goal, right? So this hook is aspirational, right? And so I've always wanted steady energy all day. No, with no crashes, you know, no burnout, you know, but I don't, I, you know, I don't want stimulants or shortcuts. I don't want to take these pills or take or drink energy drinks, right? And after digging into how energy actually works, I found this, right? And this is my first time my days feel the way I always want them to. Again, this is just, and by the way, all of these examples are just like an energy product, right? Whatever that may be energy supplement, whatever it is, right? And again, I'm not adding all of the other things like authority and stakes and all that kind of stuff. This is just a very basic example to give you guys a direction, help you see it, because as I mentioned above, and I'll mention it again, the goal of this is not to use this as your, Hey, this is how I storytell. I wrote a quest dad. Why is it not working? It's everything else that we talked above, but again, this is just to show you kind of the flow of how this works. Okay. The next one that we have down here is voyage and return. This one's super interesting. So this story says like, basically I tried the extreme path so you don't have to. And this is really powerful for skeptical buyers, right? And so this story is, you know, I went all in on trying to fix my energy, you know, biohacks, extreme routines, supplements, all this kind of stuff, right? This really builds this authority. This person that goes through all of this chaos kind of becomes an authority as a result of that. And then they realize here it actually just made things worse, right? And then once I stripped it back to this one simple change, everything stabilized and now I have the outcome that I want, right? So again, this is this idea of this person who just goes through like all of this chaos, who's like on this like voyage to try and find the answer and then finds the answer and shares the realization and now has a better normal because of it. Okay. So again, I tried the extreme path so you don't have to. The next one that we have here is rebirth. So the flow of this is stuck wake up transformation, right? So no enemy, no grind. This is just a moment of awareness that unlocks change. Okay. So this is different from obviously the ones above where you could see here. I thought feeling tired all the time was just a part of adulthood, right? Then I made one small change. Suddenly my mornings felt easier and that's when I realized how long I'd been running on empty, right? So these are people that are just kind of in this stuck, you know, this could potentially be considered slightly unaware, like people who just accept their circumstances, whatever it is. And then they wake up and they're like, holy cow, like this is not actually a thing that should be normal, right? You wake up, you go through the process and now all of a sudden you've transformed and you've become the person that you want to become, right? This is this rebirth, right? So a moment of awareness where you make someone aware of this, right? So rebirth can kind of be positioned more in the unaware category of somebody is just accepting what is right. They're just kind of stuck in this day, the day of thing. And then they, you know, they wake up, they become aware and then they transform. Okay. Uh, seven is confusion, AKA comedy. I'm not sure why Christopher Booker calls this confusion, but basically this is a, another really great story arc, which is misunderstanding, insight, and clarity. So this is along the lines of I understood the problem or made the wrong conclusion. And now I fixed it. Right. So to give you guys many stories, right? I kept buying products for energy. It turns out energy wasn't the problem. Once I fixed this one thing, everything else worked better. I wish I knew this sooner. Right. Now, number two over here is I thought I needed more energy. So I kept buying energy products. Turns out that wasn't the issue at all. Right. Once I fix this, everything else started working. Okay. So the idea behind this is you're somebody that is basically you're, you're just, you've just misunderstood, right? You, you didn't realize something and then you got an insight that then gave you clarity, right? So this is starting at the point of I kept doing all this thing, but I didn't realize I was wrong. Like nobody told me I was wrong. Right. And so this is really good for also sophisticated markets and overcoming objections where a lot of people just have, you know, misunderstandings a lot of the time. Okay. Number eight is tragedy. So this is this downward spiral also known as decline without redemption. So this one specifically is obviously not for DTC as it actually ends in tragedy and all of our ads are to be more positive, not negative with that said, the reason why I want to bring this up is because this is commonly used in like smoking companies, like anti smoking campaigns or anti drunk driving ads or environmental campaigns. Right. Like if we don't act, this is the future, right? There's some charity or some nonprofits that do this, you know, starving and abuse addiction, those kinds of things. But again, the goal of tragedy is not really conversion. The goal is more or less like awareness or behavior prevention. Right. And so this is not something that you guys want to run and direct to consumer. But anyways, that's that's tragedy. Okay. So what we've done is that, or what I've done is that is I've just kind of recreated this as like the two paths. Okay. And so what this is, is this essentially shows consequences and a way forward. Okay. And so this is great for explainer style videos, you know, voiceovers and talking head in quotation creatives, uh, where the goal is to shift direction and not really dramatize pain. Okay. And so this mini story here, if you keep pushing through fatigue with caffeine, here's what usually happens. The crashes get harder, focus gets worse and your energy never really stabilizes, right? But there is another path when you support energy the right way, they stop swinging up and down, right? So you kind of go down one path of like one path is if you don't do this, this is what it's going to look like where the other path is the one that's kind of with your product and you explain how their life can look that way. Right. So this is a really powerful method to essentially show somebody the, the problems that will happen as a result of, you know, continue to do the thing. This is different than this one up here where this one is saying, Hey, I have this problem and then I led to another problem and then that problem led to a bigger problem. And then after that, you know, I realized this was too. Well, so this is somebody who's gone to the problem where up here, what we're doing is we're future pacing and we're essentially saying like, Hey, if you keep doing this, this is what's going to end up happening, but there is a better way. There's a better path. Okay. And this is also a strategy kind of not, not entirely, but it's kind of known as like the Pixar framework. But the idea is to essentially future pace the ad. Okay. It talks about what happens and shows how things progress. And if you guys want to see a masterclass kind of on this Pixar framework, but more or less experience what it feels like to tap into the emotions of your customers and have them experience everything that we discussed above. I'll, you know, I'll leave this video for you to watch. You guys can watch it. I'm not going to pull it up, but as she does, I'm not a very emotional guy. It actually does make me emotional, which is kind of crazy. So it's a very like good example of like how, again, these plots and these storytelling arcs are helpful, right? And like I said, the reason why they're helpful is because these can be great for iterations on angles of winning ads. Right? So if you identify that maybe you're on this, you know, problem, new problem, bigger problem solution to all kind of story type and arc in one of your winning ads, why don't you try one that's maybe a little bit different? How can you maybe try one that's, you know, the confusion where it starts with a misunderstanding or how could you try an in media res, right? So the idea of this is this is helpful for just trying different ways of saying the same thing. But if you guys want to see a masterclass of like future pacing, a plus plus plus storytelling, there's an ad here from Google that is very powerful. So that is everything around the storytelling arcs and plots. And again, the biggest thing that I want to mention here is this is not to replace everything that we talked about. I don't want you to forget everything we talked about, about story and conflict and enemies and the laws of storytelling, and then just focus on these arcs, you really need to focus on everything that we discussed above. And then this can be helpful for just an extra piece of information to be like, Oh, this looks like it's more of a rags to riches plot. I wonder if we maybe start this as a two paths or like, what if we try a different version that shows kind of like the two paths where we show someone who is potentially going down the path that they shouldn't and how they can go down a different path with our product, right? That's kind of the idea of why I wanted to share this because I'm always one that's like, what are some iterations? What are some variations that I can do to try to squeeze the most out of the angle and the, you know, the avatar that I have working. And this is a great way to do that. So I'll leave it there again, hopefully you guys enjoyed and I'll see you in the next one.
