All right. Now in this module to round out the storytelling section, what we're going to do here is we're talking about specific prompts to help you guys with storytelling. Now I've basically put this into two different categories. One is brand slash market focused, and then the other is going to be customer focused. Okay. Now for this first one, I do actually have legitimate prompts, like prompts in the way that's like, here's something, here's a question that could prompt you to tell a story. And then the other one is actually an AI prompt that you can get to the AI with all of your reviews to have it search for specific stories that you can tell from those reviews. Okay. And so the first one, again, brand and market, meaning that, Hey, if there's a specific element of your brand, you can probably tell a story about it. Okay. So again, a chocolate bar using all natural ingredients. Well, again, a typical branded ad that somebody might write is we use all natural ingredients to make a better chocolate for you. It's like no story. Nobody cares. Okay. Market or like, for example, a market story ad or something that we could use. Okay. We have this brand element and then there's something that's going on in the market. Right? We have this enemy of chocolate bars in general, right? Or candy bars with that leads us to the ad that you guys have seen, which is, do you know what the number one ingredient in most chocolate bars is? I'll give you a hint. It's not cacao. Right now we have a story and people care. They're interested. Right? So again, this stuff now, when you guys are looking at this after understanding everything might be obvious where it's like, no wonder this doesn't work. Right. Unless somebody is looking specifically for an all natural ingredient chocolate bar, which there is a sub sector of the market that is, it's just not going to resonate with as many people as letting people know like, Hey, most chocolate bars, their number one ingredient isn't chocolate. So that's a problem. Okay. And so again, this is where we could take a brand element and tell a story around it. Right. Story equals people care. Another one down here is a another brand story ad just based off of, again, this could be in the market in general. It doesn't even have to be based on a specific ingredient. But for example, this hook starts with, and this is the original hook that you guys should have seen in the open loop section, which is, can I make a better chocolate bar than Mr. Beast? Now again, I mentioned, be careful with name dropping, but I wanted to show you guys the creativity here behind someone who's saying like, I'm John and I made this chocolate bar. Right. Now again, is that to an extent a story because you're talking about, you know, I'm John and here's how I'm trying to make the best chocolate bar. Potentially people will care, but when we position it, where it's like, can I make a better chocolate bar than this person? Right. And we create some curiosity. We create a gap. Now, all of a sudden, we have a story that people are actually going to care about rather than, you know, having to listen to John, tell me about his chocolate bar that he started. Right. And so at the end of the day, again, see the difference between those two. And then there's another really great example here where, again, this is a story being told around how to sell a pet toy. So let's go ahead and click into it. Sometimes crazy cat ladies aren't so crazy after all. This is how Marlene, a cat mom of 17 years, created a new toy that changes how we think about cat playtime. Marlene's. Right. So there we go. We could have easily started with like, oh, here's this cat toy. That's great for cats. But again, sometimes crazy cat ladies are actually right. Boom. Curiosity cat. What do you mean by that? Well, this is Marlene. She's a cat mom of 17 years. And now we start to get into the story. Okay. And so again, just another example of a story. And now people care more to learn about the cat toy versus here's our cat toy. It's the best. Okay. Again, some prompts for this. Big credit to Davey Fogarty as well. If you guys haven't watched his story telling video, it's a pretty good video. It's got a bunch of like really good prompts in there. It shows a bunch of examples, actually breaks down some of the science as well of like what happens to the brain. But the point is, is here's just some prompts here, which is what is something that everyone thinks is true about your product, but isn't that's a direct one from him. What's something about your product that would frustrate people? Another one from Davey. And then what's a story that proves your product or service. And again, this is just product is different from competitors. Right? So again, this is us kind of focusing on this enemy. And then as we get down here, we go even further into that, which is what's, what made you angry that you had to create this product, right? Like what is your enemy that you're going after? Make a story about it. Again, what do your competitors hope customers never figure out that has some drama into it? What are your competitors hiding? Right? You see a lot of transparent. I actually just bought some protein bars called Jacob's protein. And it's like, none of this, none of this, none of this, like we're the cleanest protein bar. They're calling out. Our competitors don't want to let you know what's actually in their bars. Right? Rx bar was the OG of that because Rx bar just put their ingredients right on the front of the bar. And so it was a very bold statement to be like, we have nothing to hide. Right? This is an example of storytelling of like, they hope customers don't figure that out, but you're bringing it to the surface. Right? And then again, another great one to just prompt a story is like, who is this product not for? Sometimes by saying who your product is not for, you can create a story and you can find an identity that you can actually, you know, build around and actually attract more people that way. Okay. So those are just some prompts. There's tons of different prompts that you guys can think through. These are the six that I've given you guys to think about how you can make a story around your brand or your market, but there's stories everywhere in your market. There's, and there's even like a current events one. I think there's another one that Davey brought up, which is like, you know, what's something recent that's happened in your space that you could talk about? Right? That is also another storyline. Okay. So just some examples there of how to tell stories from a brand or a market perspective. Again, one that's not even on here is like a founder's ad, right? Like why did you create your product in the first place? Right? So examples there should hopefully make sense. Okay. The next one here is customer focus. And so again, this is where you find story starters in your reviews and build from there. So for example, the customer quote of the rat vac made it incredibly easy to solve my pest problem and gave me my garage back. It just works. Now, a lot of you guys might take this quote and just pop it straight on to the actual, uh, you know, ad and be like, let me see if this works. And nine times or 99 times out of a hundred, probably 999 times out of a thousand. It doesn't work. Right. And so why it's like, well, because this quote is just in itself, it's not that interesting, right? But there are interesting perspectives or there are interesting things about this quote. The one that I love is gave me my garage back. So how can we take this and turn it into a story that people care about? Right? Well, here's a customer quote story ad example of rats took over my garage. Here's how a vacuum took it back. Right. Now we've got this curiosity. I'm like, how does a vacuum take back a garage? And obviously people aren't like too weirded out about rats being a garage, but again, rats took over my garage. Here's how I took it back with a vacuum. Right. And so the idea is, is now we have a plot. We have a story. We have something that people might be interested in. How did this happen? Right. Versus you're just trying to sell me something. Okay. And so again, customer focus ones, I have a prompt for you guys here. Again, you guys will use this AI prompt. Just take this prompt. Give it to your Claude project. If you have a Claude project or Claude, if you're using Claude code, the one that actually is like plugged into your computer and plugged into your files, you should have your reviews downloaded and then you can just have it go through all of the reviews. And what this will do is this will rank the best reviews for you. I actually just use this for one of our newer brands and I have a insane idea for a story that I want to write. Unfortunately, I can't show you guys a story which is very frustrating and annoying, but I am going to obviously talk a little bit more about that in the modules to come. My point is this is an AI prompt that you guys can use to find reviews that are story worthy so that way you can start making a story from the quote. Okay. So those are the two prompts to help you storytell. Again, when it comes to storytelling, this is just a skillset that it is, it takes a lot more effort. It does take more energy. It takes more thought process, but when you can storytell and you can wrap your product in a narrative, right? Don't forget the original question, which is how can you fit your product in your avatar's life? This is prompts to help you storytell. Remember to think through, how can I wrap this in a narrative? Hi guys, I wanted to ask a more specific question about the copy used on Facebook. As explained in the course, the strategy to follow is 3:2:2

So for every sub-avatar batch I test, I create 3 different angles, 2 different FB copies, and 2 different FB headlines. My question is: how do you actually analyze the performance or the impact that the copy and the headline had on that specific batch? I mean, I think copy and headline are two elements that shouldn’t be underestimated and I believe they have their own weight, but how can I analyze them to understand whether they performed well or not? Thanks.

A. Context

I always heard that primary texts matter a lot. But I never actually found anybody talk about how to actually write primary texts for ads. Obviously basic copywriting principles apply and it needs to be congruent with the creative as well. But is there any specific thing we need to pay attention to? Maybe a framework which can be applied to writing primary texts?

B. TLDR

Primary text matters. But how do we actually write them properly?

C. Question

What are the specific frameworks, techniques, or key considerations for writing primary text in ads beyond basic copywriting principles?

---
How to write open Loops + Q&A - January 7th, 2026

## Meeting Purpose

Weekly Q&A and a tactical lesson on writing effective open loops for ads.

## Key Takeaways

- **Write Open Loops to Maintain Attention:** Use Juxtaposition, Fear, and Contrarian angles to create curiosity gaps that maintain attention, targeting 90% retention at 6 seconds.
- **Scale Smartly at $250k/Day:** At high volume, prioritize backend stability (legal, compliance, supply chain) over aggressive growth to prevent catastrophic issues like lawsuits or payment processor holds.
- **Brand Your Winners:** A $300k/mo dropshipping store with a 30% return rate is a signal to brand the hero product. Use customer surveys to guide product development and secure credit terms with manufacturers.
- **Evolve Event in Marrakech:** The annual event is set for mid-to-late April in Marrakech, Morocco, and will be capped at 100 attendees on a first-come, first-served basis.

## Topics

### Evolve Updates & Community News

- **Copywriting Program:** New modules will be released "Netflix-style," with potential live deep-dives on specific topics.
- **Travel:** Spencer and Sean will be in Italy and Dubai (for Agency Founders) in January.
- **London Meetup:** 28 members are attending a meetup organized by Wei Shaun Eng.

### Tactical Lesson: Writing Open Loops for Ads

- **Goal:** Maintain attention, not just grab it. The target is 90% retention at 6 seconds.
- **Method 1: Juxtaposition**
	- Pair two things that shouldn't go together to create a curiosity gap.
		- **Example:** "I quit the gym and lost 20 pounds."
		- **Exercise:** Practice with random concepts (e.g., "pour water in my gas tank") to train creative thinking.
- **Method 2: Curiosity Through Fear**
	- Leverage the brain's danger-detection instinct. "Sticks" are often more effective than "carrots."
		- **Example:** "Watch out for these ingredients when buying shampoo."
		- **Note:** This is not clickbait if the ad delivers on its promise and ethically connects to the product as a solution.
- **Method 3: Do the Opposite**
	- Stand out by directly contrasting industry norms.
		- **Example:** Liquid Death's "grandma drinking water" ad vs. the typical "fancy model" ads from competitors.
- **Broad-to-Niche Strategy**
	- Start with a broad hook to capture wide attention.
		- Funnel down by introducing specific avatar details later in the video.
		- **Rationale:** This is more scalable than calling out a niche avatar immediately.

### Q&A: Scaling & Strategy

- **Problem: Fake DMCA Takedowns at $250k/Day**
	- **Solution:** Apply for Facebook's Brand Rights Protection Program. This requires a trademark and allows Facebook to automatically remove infringing ads.
- **Problem: Scaling Stalled at $10k/Day Spend**
	- **Diagnosis:** The current sub-avatar is saturated, causing frequency to rise.
		- **Solutions:**
		1. **Broaden the Hook:** Use a broad hook and funnel down to the sub-avatar.
				2. **Test New Concepts:** Dedicate a separate campaign to test new sub-avatars and angles.
				3. **Refine Current Angle:** Improve existing ads to push the current sub-avatar further.
- **Problem: Post-Q4 Sales Dip for Seasonal Brand**
	- **Context:** A watch accessory brand's ad spend dropped from $20k/day to $5k/day after the gifting season.
		- **Solution:** Pivot Q1 messaging to focus on **status** and self-improvement. The dip is likely seasonal, and sales should rebound around Valentine's Day.
- **Problem: Transitioning from Dropshipping to a Brand**
	- **Context:** A dropshipping store earns $300k/mo but has a 30% return rate.
		- **Solution:** Brand the store.
		1. **Survey Customers:** Identify the hero product and what customers value.
				2. **Improve Product:** Work with manufacturers to enhance the hero product based on feedback.
				3. **Secure Credit Terms:** Negotiate credit terms (e.g., 30% upfront, 70% in 30 days) to protect cash flow.
- **Problem: Deciding Between Two Brands**
	- **Context:** A dropshipping brand with copyright issues is unprofitable. A new brand is launching on TikTok Shop and Amazon.
		- **Solution:** If the old brand is unprofitable and a distraction, shut it down to focus fully on the new venture.

<audio><source type="audio/mpeg"></audio>

FILE: STORY-BASED AD FOR NATIVE _ UGLY COPYWRITING SOP.txt
======================================================================

▄▄▄

STORY-BASED AD
FOR NATIVE / UGLY

COPYWRITING SOP
Relatability vs. Twisting the Knife
Third-Party Page Strategy • Script Frameworks • Common Mistakes

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

SECTION 1: THE CORE CONCEPT
There are two fundamentally different emotional engines you can use in story-based ads. Most
copywriters confuse them, use the wrong one for the wrong audience, and kill their ads before
they ever get to the offer.
This SOP breaks down exactly when to use each, how to write each, and where most people
mess it up.

The Two Engines
Relatability Engine

Twist the Knife

What it does

Makes the reader feel SEEN
and understood

Makes the reader feel PAIN
they didn’t fully acknowledge

Audience

Solution-Aware (they know
solutions exist, seeking the
right one)

Unaware / Problem-Aware
(they know the problem,
haven’t acted yet)

Emotional trigger

“Oh my god, that’s literally me”

“Damn, this is worse than I
thought”

Reader’s reaction

Trust + curiosity (“if she found
a fix, maybe I can too”)

Urgency + fear (“I need to fix
this NOW”)

Tone

Warm, personal, “I’ve been
there”

Confrontational, wake-up call

Risk if done wrong

Boring / no urgency

Reader feels attacked and
scrolls away

SECTION 2: THE THIRD-PARTY PAGE STRATEGY
This is the trust multiplier most media buyers completely ignore. Story-based ads perform
significantly better when they come from a page that is NOT your brand. Why? Because people
don’t trust brands. They trust people, communities, and authorities.

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

Why It Works
When a reader sees an ad from “ReviveWell Supplements” telling a story about how their
product changed someone’s life, their brain immediately flags it as an ad and puts up
resistance. But when that SAME story comes from a page called “Menopause Diary” or from a
person named “Jennifer Walsh”—the guard drops. It feels like someone sharing their
experience, not a company selling you something.

Types of Third-Party Pages to Create

1. The Community/Diary Page
Create a page that sounds like a niche community or personal blog. The name should describe
the PROBLEM SPACE, not your product.

EXAMPLES:
“Menopause Diary” — for women’s health supplements
“Back Pain Recovery Journal” — for posture/pain products
“Sleep Struggles” — for sleep aids
“Dad Bod Comeback” — for men’s fitness supplements
“Balding at 30” — for hair loss products
“Anxious Gut” — for gut health products

2. The Personal Name Page
Create a page with a first + last name that matches your target avatar. This makes the ad feel
like a personal post shared by a real person, not a brand.

EXAMPLES:
“Jennifer Walsh” — 35-45 year old woman avatar
“Marcus Reid” — 30-40 year old man avatar
“Sarah Kim” — younger female audience
“Dr. Amanda Torres” — if your story involves medical credibility

KEY RULE:

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

The name should match the demographic of your avatar. If you’re targeting 45-year-old moms in
the midwest, “Jennifer Walsh” hits different than “Aisha Patel.” Match the name to who your
audience sees as “someone like me.”

3. The Authority/Professional Page
If your ad angle involves a professional (doctor, nurse, trainer, nutritionist), create a page under
their name or title. This is extremely powerful for health, fitness, and wellness niches.

EXAMPLES:
“Dr. Rachel Simmons, ND” — naturopathic angle
“Nurse Katie” — casual medical authority
“Coach Davis Strength” — fitness authority
“Nutritionist Marie” — food/supplement angle

How to Set It Up on Meta
1.​ Create the page: Set it up under your Business Manager. Use a profile photo that
matches the page identity (stock photo of a person for personal name pages, a
logo/graphic for community pages).
2.​ Post organic content: Before running ads, post 5–10 organic posts that match the
page’s identity. If it’s “Menopause Diary,” post about menopause tips, questions,
relatable content. This makes the page look real if anyone clicks through.
3.​ Run a page like campaign: Before you run any ads, run a small Page Like campaign
on each third-party page. Even 50–200 likes makes the page look established and
trustworthy. A page with zero followers sharing a personal story feels suspicious. A page
with 150 followers feels like a real person with a real following.
4.​ Run the ad from this page: In Ads Manager, select this page as the identity for your ad.
Your brand page can still be linked as the “Instagram account” or in the landing
page—but the ad itself comes from the third-party page.
5.​ Rotate pages: Create 3–5 third-party pages, each with a different angle—one doctor,
one nurse, one personal name, one community diary, etc. The point is NOT to run the
same ad from different pages. Each page = a different character telling a different
version of the story. The doctor explains the science. The customer shares her
experience. The community page feels like a support group post. You’re not rotating for
freshness—you’re rotating because different people trust different voices.

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

Third-Party Page + Ad Type Matrix
Ad Type

Best Page Type

Why

UGC Story (Relatability)

Personal Name Page

Feels like a real person
sharing their story on their
own timeline

Expert/Education Angle

Authority Page (Dr., Nurse,
Coach)

Reader trusts the information
because it comes from a
credible source

Community Discussion

Community/Diary Page

Feels like a post from a
support group, not a product
pitch

Before/After Transformation

Personal Name Page

The transformation is more
believable from “a real person”

Problem Callout (Twist the
Knife)

Community/Diary Page

Feels like the community is
having an honest conversation
about the problem

IMPORTANT:
The third-party page is the VEHICLE. The script is the ENGINE. A perfect third-party page with a
bad script still fails. A great script from your brand page still works—just not as well. Use both
together for maximum impact.

SECTION 3: THE RELATABILITY ENGINE
(SOLUTION-AWARE)
Use this when your avatar ALREADY KNOWS they have a problem and has probably tried
other solutions. They don’t need you to convince them they have a problem. They need to feel
like YOU understand their specific experience better than anyone else.

How It Works
You tell YOUR story (or a customer’s story) in first person. But every single line is engineered so
the reader self-identifies. You’re not saying “you have this problem.” You’re saying “here’s what
happened to me” and letting them map it onto their own life.

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

The Psychology
•​

Pattern Recognition: The reader’s brain is scanning your story and matching it against
their own experience. Each match = more trust.

•​

Proxy Hope: Because you found a solution, and because they’ve identified with your
story, they automatically believe the solution might work for THEM.

•​

No Resistance: You never told them they have a problem. They concluded it
themselves. Self-drawn conclusions are 10x stronger than anything you could say.

The Relatability Script Framework
Follow this 7-step structure. Example niche: men’s hair loss supplement. Page: “Balding at 30.”

1.​ The Hook (Lines 1–3): Open with a specific, unexpected detail from YOUR life that your
avatar will instantly recognize. Not generic. Hyper-specific.

EXAMPLE:
“My girlfriend ran her fingers through my hair and pulled her hand back with like 15 strands in it.
She didn’t say anything. She just looked at her hand.”
→ Every guy losing hair has had this exact moment. Instant recognition. NOT an attack—it’s a
shared experience.

2.​ The Escalation (Lines 4–12): Stack specific symptoms/situations the reader also
experiences. Each line = another “that’s me” moment. You’re building a case through
lived detail, not through pain amplification.

EXAMPLE:
“I started wearing hats everywhere. Even to dinner.”
“I’d angle my head in the bathroom mirror trying to convince myself it wasn’t that bad.”
“Every shower I’d look at the drain and count.”
→ Each line is a mirror. Reader thinks: “this is literally me.”

3.​ The Self-Discovery Moment (Lines 13–16): Show the moment YOU realized how bad
it had gotten. The reader experiences the same realization in real time.

EXAMPLE:

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

“Then my buddy showed me a photo from his wedding last year. And I saw the back of my head.
And I just sat there staring at it.”

4.​ The Failed Attempts (Lines 17–22): List solutions you tried that DIDN’T work. This is
critical because your solution-aware reader has tried these same things. More
relatability. More trust.

EXAMPLE:
“I tried minoxidil for 6 months. Made my scalp itch like crazy and I couldn’t tell if it was doing
anything. Tried biotin gummies. Tried the expensive shampoo. Spent like $400 on stuff that
didn’t move the needle.”
→ Reader: “I tried those too and they didn’t work for me either!” = maximum trust.

5.​ The Education Bridge (Lines 23–28): Explain WHY those solutions failed using a
credible source. This is where you differentiate your product. Frame it as new
information the reader has never heard.

EXAMPLE:
“Then I found this dermatologist’s post explaining that most hair loss products only work on the
surface. They don’t address DHT at the follicle level. He said it’s like watering a plant that has no
roots—doesn’t matter how much you pour on top.”
→ This reframes the problem and positions your product as the only logical answer.

6.​ The Result (Lines 29–35): Show the transformation. Be specific with timelines and
details. The reader needs to see themselves in this outcome.

EXAMPLE:
“Month 2 I noticed less hair in the drain. Like noticeably less.”
“Month 4 my barber said ‘whatever you’re doing, keep doing it.’”
“My girlfriend grabbed my hair last week and went ‘babe, feel this.’ There’s actual thickness
now.”

7.​ The Soft CTA (Lines 36–40): Address the reader directly for the first time. Summarize
the problem, name the product, and position it as where to start. No hard sell. Keep the
same conversational tone.

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

EXAMPLE:
“So if you’re counting hairs in the drain every morning and nothing you’ve tried is working—it’s
probably not the products. It’s what’s happening at the follicle. The stuff I used is called
[Product]. Link’s in the comments.”

SECTION 4: TWIST THE KNIFE (UNAWARE /
PROBLEM-AWARE)
Use this when your avatar KNOWS they have a problem but hasn’t done anything about it yet.
They’re living with it. They’ve normalized it. Your job is to make the cost of inaction impossible to
ignore.

How It Works
You take a pain they’re already aware of and show them how much worse it really is, how much
it’s costing them, or where it’s heading if they don’t act. You’re not creating new pain. You’re
amplifying existing pain until doing nothing feels more painful than taking action. And you do this
through YOUR story—not by pointing at them.
The Psychology
•​

Loss Aversion: People are 2x more motivated by what they might lose than what they
might gain. You’re showing them what they’re actively losing.

•​

Future Pacing (Negative): You paint a picture of where this problem leads if they keep
ignoring it. Makes the future cost real and immediate.

•​

Breaking Normalization: They’ve accepted this problem as “just how it is.” You shatter
that acceptance—by showing how YOU shattered your own.

The Twist-the-Knife Script Framework
Follow this 6-step structure. Example niche: men’s back pain product. Page: “Back Pain
Recovery Journal.”
Important: All examples use “I” language. You twist the knife through YOUR story. The reader
feels the cut by proxy—without ever feeling attacked.

1.​ The Confrontation Hook (Lines 1–3): Open with the LIE you were telling yourself—the
same lie your reader is telling themselves right now. Then shatter it.

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

EXAMPLE:
“I’d been waking up stiff every morning for 3 years and kept telling myself it was just ‘getting
older.’ It wasn’t.”
→ This CONFRONTS—but through the storyteller’s own denial. The reader recognizes the
same lie they tell themselves.

2.​ The Cost Stack (Lines 4–10): List everything this problem cost YOU—but pick costs
your reader is also paying without fully realizing it. Each line makes them calculate their
own losses by proxy.

EXAMPLE:
“I stopped going to the gym because I couldn’t bend over to tie my shoes without wincing.”
“My kids would ask me to wrestle and I’d say ‘not right now buddy’ and the look on their face...”
“I’d lie in bed at night cycling through every position and none of them worked. Just staring at the
ceiling at 2am.”
→ Each line stacks another cost the reader hasn’t fully calculated in their own life. The knife
goes deeper through recognition.

3.​ The Future Projection (Lines 11–15): Share the moment YOU saw where this was
heading. A doctor’s warning, a breaking point, a realization. The reader maps it onto
their own timeline.

EXAMPLE:
“And then my doctor looked at the scan and said ‘if you’d come in a year from now, we’d be
talking about surgery.’ That word hit me like a truck.”
→ The reader now sees where THEIR path leads. But you delivered it through YOUR wake-up
moment.

4.​ The Permission Shift (Lines 16–19): Acknowledge it wasn’t your fault. Release the
shame. This is critical—you twisted the knife, now hand them a way out.

EXAMPLE:
“Turns out it wasn’t because I was lazy or out of shape. My spine had been compensating for a
muscle imbalance that nobody ever told me about.”

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

5.​ The Solution + Mechanism (Lines 20–25): Present the fix with a clear mechanism.
After all that pain, they NEED to understand why this works.
6.​ The Urgency CTA (Lines 26–30): Tie the CTA back to what YOU lost by waiting—then
bridge to the reader for the first time. “If your [symptom] is doing what mine was…”

EXAMPLE:
“I wasted 3 years choosing to ‘deal with it’ when the fix took 10 minutes a day. If your back is
doing what mine was, don’t wait for the word ‘surgery.’ Link in bio.”

SECTION 5: THE CRITICAL DIFFERENCE (WHERE MOST OF
YOU MESS UP)
This is the #1 mistake. Read this section twice.

The Mistake
You guys see a story-based ad that describes symptoms in detail and assume it’s “twisting the
knife” because the content sounds painful. But describing painful symptoms is NOT the same as
twisting the knife.

The Litmus Test
Ask yourself one question about each line of your script:

THE QUESTION:
“Is this line describing MY experience (so the reader recognizes it)?
Or is this line making the reader REALIZE their situation is worse than they thought?”

If it’s describing YOUR experience = Relatability.
If it’s making them realize the cost of inaction = Twisting the knife.

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

Side-by-Side: Same Topic, Different Engine
Niche: Men’s hair loss. Both use “I” language. The difference is the INTENT behind each line.

Relatability (Solution-Aware)

Twist the Knife (Unaware /
Problem-Aware)

“My girlfriend pulled her hand back with 15
strands in it.”

“I was losing clumps every day and kept
pretending I didn’t see it.”

“I started wearing hats everywhere.”

“I started wearing hats everywhere and told
myself it was ‘style.’ It wasn’t. I was hiding.”

“I’d angle my head in the mirror trying to
convince myself it wasn’t that bad.”

“Every month that mirror got harder to lie to.
And I kept lying anyway.”

“I tried minoxidil for 6 months and nothing
changed.”

“I’d spent $400 on stuff that didn’t work and I
was STILL telling myself ‘give it one more
month.’ That ‘one more month’ cost me a year.”

Reader feels: “That’s me. He gets it.”

Reader feels: “...that’s exactly what I’m doing
right now.”

THE KEY DIFFERENCE:
Relatability = “Here’s what happened to me.” (reader feels understood)
Twist the Knife = “Here’s the lie I told myself and what it cost me.” (reader feels called out by
proxy)
Same “I” language. Completely different emotional payload.

SECTION 6: WHICH ENGINE DO I USE?

Signal

Use Relatability

Use Twist the Knife

Awareness level

Solution-aware (tried things,
looking for the right one)

Unaware / Problem-aware
(knows the problem, hasn’t
acted)

Audience has tried solutions?

Yes — they need to trust YOU
specifically

No — they need a reason to
act NOW

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

Main barrier to purchase

Skepticism (“why would this
one work?”)

Complacency (“it’s not that
bad”)

Your product is…

A better version of something
they’ve tried

Their first real attempt at fixing
this

Emotional goal

Build trust through shared
experience

Create urgency through
amplified pain

Best format

Long-form story ad, UGC
testimonial

Short-form hook-heavy,
direct-to-camera

Best page type

Personal name page or
community page

Community/diary page or
authority page

SECTION 7: THE 6 MOST COMMON MISTAKES

Mistake #1: Using Twist the Knife on Solution-Aware People
If someone has already tried 4 products, they don’t need you to tell them their life is falling apart.
They KNOW. What they need is to feel understood and to believe YOUR solution is different.
Twisting the knife on this audience creates resentment, not urgency.

Mistake #2: Using Relatability on Problem-Aware People Who Haven’t Acted
If someone knows they have the problem but hasn’t done anything about it, a cozy “I’ve been
there” story won’t move them. They need a wake-up call. They need to see the cost of inaction.
Relatability without urgency = they bookmark your ad and never come back.

Mistake #3: Confusing “Describing Pain” with “Twisting the Knife”
Describing what happened to you is storytelling. Making the reader realize their situation is
worse than they think is twisting the knife. Saying “my girlfriend found hair on her hand” is NOT
twisting the knife. Saying “I kept pretending she didn’t notice but deep down I knew she did”
IS—because it forces the reader to confront their own denial.

Mistake #4: Twisting the Knife Without a Permission Shift

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

If you amplify pain without releasing shame, you create a reader who feels terrible AND blamed.
They won’t buy. They’ll scroll away feeling worse. Always follow the knife with “turns out it wasn’t
my fault, here’s why.”

Mistake #5: Breaking Character in a Relatability Ad
The entire relatability engine depends on the reader believing this is a real person sharing a real
story. The moment you switch to “marketing voice” (“this revolutionary product”, “don’t miss
out”), you break the spell. Keep the CTA in the same conversational tone as the rest of the story.

Mistake #6: Running Story Ads from Your Brand Page
You wrote a perfect first-person story ad and then ran it from “ReviveWell Supplements.” The
reader sees the brand name FIRST, and now every word of that “personal story” reads like a
scripted ad. Use a third-party page. The story needs to come from a person or a
community—not from the brand that profits from the sale.

SECTION 8: QUICK-REFERENCE CHEAT SHEET

Before You Write, Answer These 4 Questions:
1.​ Has my avatar tried other solutions? (Yes = Relatability, No = Twist the Knife)
2.​ What’s their main barrier? (Skepticism = Relatability, Complacency = Twist the Knife)
3.​ Am I telling MY story or confronting THEM? (My story = Relatability, Confronting = Twist
the Knife)
4.​ What third-party page am I running this from? (Personal name, community, or authority?)

The Golden Rules
•​

Relatability: Never break first person. Every line = a mirror. Solution curiosity builds by
proxy. Run from a personal name page.

•​

Twist the Knife: Never amplify without a permission shift. Stack costs through YOUR
story. Future-pace the consequences. Run from a community or authority page.

Story-Based Ad SOP — Native/Ugly

•​

Third-Party Pages: Match the page name and identity to your avatar. Seed organic
content and run a page like campaign before running ads. Rotate 3–5 pages with
different angles.

•​

Both: People buy from people who make them feel understood, not attacked. Even
when twisting the knife, the goal is “I see you” not “you’re broken.”

END OF SOP



======================================================================
FILE: Understanding the importance of copywriting.txt
======================================================================

Copywriting 101:
If you run an e-commerce brand, the most crucial part of your business is marketing.
And if the most crucial part of your business is marketing, then you need to get good at it.
To be good at marketing, you need to be good at writing words that convince people to buy your
stuff.
And to be good at writing words that convince people to buy your stuff, you need to understand
who those people are.
And once you understand who they are
You need to learn how to communicate that understanding to them.​

"The Power of Your Copy Is In Direct Proportion To The Depth of Your Research"​
-Eugene Schwatz​

How you communicate your understanding to them is…

✍️Copywriting.

So, for you to actually scale your eCom brand, the #1 skill that you will need is copywriting.
Easy right?
Well, the problem is that most people, especially nowadays with AI, are mediocre at copywriting.
And people prefer to press buttons in Meta to try to hack the system
Instead of learning a LEGIT skillset of copywriting.
And the truth is, being a good copywriter is hard.
Getting good at copywriting is like learning a new language.
And for most of you who aren’t people who grew up speaking 2 languages
Learning a language is damn hard.
So the truth is
If I were to just give you guys a template and a prompt, which I will, and tell you that that is all
you need to create winning ads…

I would be doing you all a disservice.
The problem is, I've been copywriting for the last five years in e-commerce.
So a lot of this stuff just comes “Natural to me” because I’ve done more reps.
But, by being in Origins
You have the skillset of being able to find the information that will equip you with the knowledge
of your specific customers.
However, the only thing that will hold you back from actually getting to the next level is your
ability to execute it.
AKA your copywriting
And then after your copywriting, unfortunately, there is another caveat which is your ability to
visually communicate it…
Although I will have a solution for you very shortly
My point is this: Copywriting is not a “nice-to-have” skill. It’s a MUST-HAVE SKILL.
It’s not really an option unless you can pay someone actually good.
Which most of you can not.
So…
What we're about to cover is just a spec of what comes into copywriting.
But I need you to understand that, as I mentioned in the first line, learning copywriting is like
learning a new language.
At first, it will be really rough.
You're going to have to try to remember to do specific things.
You’re going to make mistakes
You’re going to sound like a baby speaking its first few words.
But after enough practice, it will just start to flow naturally.
And at the end of the day,
If there's one skill that will keep food on your table for the rest of your life, even outside of eCom

It's copywriting.
So lean in to this.
And be excited to develop and learn the skill.
Origins will NOT be the copywriting masterclass that will get you to millions of dollars a month.
But it will hopefully set you on your quest to becoming a great copywriter through reading books,
learning from some of the greats, and MOST IMPORTANTLY, writing tons of copy.
Let’s dive in.

The Rules Of Copy:
#1 - Nobody Cares About You, Your Company, or Your Product
The Beginner Mistake: Talking about how great your product is, how long you've been in
business, your awards, and your mission.
The Truth: People are interested in themselves. Period. Frame everything through THEIR lens their problems, their desires, their outcomes.
The Shift:
●​
●​

❌ "We've been in business for 20 years"
✅ "For 20 years, we've helped people like you solve [problem]"

#2 - Results Are All That Matter. Remove Your Emotion.
The Beginner Mistake: "I think this headline is better" or "This sounds good to me"
The Truth: The market decides what is good and what is not. Your job is to test, measure, and
let data tell you what converts. And most importantly, USE THE DATA to complete feedback
loops.
The Discipline: Never argue about copy. Test it. If it doesn't convert, it's wrong - no matter how
much you like it.

#3 - Write How People Talk, Not How They Read
The Beginner Mistake: Writing in "proper" formal English like you learned in school.
The Truth: Copy should sound like a conversation. Use contractions. Short sentences. Start
sentences with "And" or "But." Write like you're talking to a friend.
The Test: Read your copy out loud. If it sounds weird or stiff, rewrite it.
Pro tip: Use the https://hemingwayapp.com/ to confirm your writing is at a 6th grade level or
below (can ask Claude to rewrite it if needed).
Examples:
●​
●​

❌ "This supplement facilitates enhanced metabolic function"
✅ "This tells your body to burn fat instead of storing it"

#4 - Copy is never too long… Only too boring.
The Beginner Mistake: "People won't read long copy" - so they write short, incomplete copy.
The Truth: People who are interested in what you're selling will read EVERYTHING, as long as
it’s engaging.

#5 - Every Sentence Should Create a "Slippery Slide"
The Beginner Mistake: Writing sentences that let the reader stop and think.
The Truth: Your sentences should be a slippery slide. Once they start reading, they can't stop
until the end.
How to Do It:
●​
●​
●​
●​
●​

End sentences with intrigue
Use open loops
Create curiosity gaps
Make each sentence lead naturally to the next
Never give them a natural place to stop until the CTA

Example:
●​

❌ "Our product has three ingredients. They work together to help you."

●​

✅ "Our product has three ingredients. The first one does something no other
supplement can do

#6 - Research is 80% of the Work
The Beginner Mistake: Just sitting down and writing with minimal research. Not stopping while
writing to do more research if needed.
The Truth: Great copy comes from great research. The writing is just organizing what you
learned.
What to Research:
●​
●​
●​
●​
●​

✅ Customer reviews (their exact language)
✅ Competitor claims (what's already been said)
✅ Amazon Q&A sections (their questions = your objections)
✅ Reddit/Facebook groups (raw, unfiltered pain points)
✅ Product mechanism (understand it deeply)

The Rule: Spend 2-3 hours researching for every 1 hour writing.

⭐️ #7 - The Daily Practice (Joe Schriefer)
If you do this for 90 days and you'll be better than 90% of copywriters.
1.​ Read a piece of copy (active reading - analyze the subtext - what is being
communicated?)
2.​ Write a page of copy (doesn't have to be for an ad, can be for a landing page, a tweet,
anything.)
3.​ Come up with an idea (keep asking "what if...")
a.​ "What if I approached this project from a completely different angle?"
b.​ "Why did this ad stop me? What's the hook pattern?"
c.​ "How would I improve this ad if it was mine?"
d.​ "Why did I just buy this? What was the trigger?"
e.​ "What would make me buy this faster?"
f.​ "I just got frustrated by X - that's a pain point someone could sell into"
g.​ "Everyone in this niche says X - what if we said Y instead?"
h.​ "Nobody's addressing Z objection - what if we led with that?"
Pro Tip: Keep a notes app on your laptop/phone (Apple Notes, Google Keep, Notion) Create a
"Copy Ideas" section, hooks that stopped my scroll, etc.

Copywriting Strategies To Hit $100k/Mo+
#1 - Understand what humans value

-​
-​
-​
-​
-​

What is their dream outcome?
How likely are they to get it?
How long does it take them to get it?
How much effort does it take them?
And how much sacrifice does it require?

#2 - Understand “frames” you can use in copywriting:

Examples:

❌ "Lose weight fast" = Only has FAST, missing NEW, EASY, SAFE​
●​ ✅ "12,000 women blocked the enzyme destroying their appetite hormone and ate 24%
●​

less in week one - without trying. Try it risk-free." = Has all four​

❌ "Sleep better with our blackout curtains" = Only BIG implied​
●​ ✅ "The first blackout blind that blocks 100% of light using military-grade fabric - install in
●​

under 5 minutes without drilling. 15,000 parents got their toddlers sleeping through the
night in the first week.​

#3 - Avoid categorizing yourself with others and blending in
The Trap:​
The moment you compare yourself to an existing category, you lose NEW/ONLY positioning
Saying "like X but Y" categorizes you as an "X alternative"
You've now entered a competitive comparison instead of owning a new category​

The Fix:​
Don't say what you're "like". Don't use market category language (unless reframing it)
Position as the FIRST thing that does X, not another version of Y
Examples:

❌ "Better than other collagens" = Categorizes as collagen
✅ "The first supplement that blocks the enzyme destroying your appetite hormone" = New
category

❌ "Like whey protein but vegan" = Categorized as whey alternative
✅ "The first complete protein that digests in 23 minutes and builds muscle 40% faster than

whey - from a banned Icelandic algae" = New category (fast-digesting complete protein from
algae)

❌ "Like a Dyson but affordable" = Dyson alternative
✅ "The first device that adds moisture without making your room feel like a rainforest - by

releasing particles 90% smaller than traditional humidifiers" = New category (micro-particle
moisture)

#4 - You need to show, not tell (even with words)
The Problem: Most copywriters TELL benefits:
"This will make you feel energized." → Telling triggers the logical brain (skepticism)
The Solution: SHOW the transformation through story, imagery, behavioral change
Showing triggers the emotional brain (belief)
Examples:

❌ TELLING: "You'll have more energy and feel great"
✅ SHOWING: "Imagine waking up without hitting snooze three times. Walking past the vending
machine at 3pm without even thinking about it. Going to bed without the nightly pantry raid."
Ergonomic Mouse:

❌ TELLING: "Your wrist pain will disappear."
✅ SHOWING: "Remember that shooting pain you used to get around 2pm? The one that made
you shake out your hand every few minutes? Gone. Now you're on your third Zoom call and you
haven't even thought about your wrist. You're clicking without wincing."

#5 - How to write body copy that isn’t fluff.
OBJECTION → CLAIM → PROOF → BENEFIT
OBJECTION - What they're thinking/doubting (address it head-on)
CLAIM - Your counter-statement (what's actually true)
PROOF - Evidence that validates your claim (study, testimonial, mechanism)
BENEFIT - The emotional payoff (what their life looks like after)

Cycle through this pattern multiple times in your body copy for different objections.
Example:
Objection (implied): "I've tried supplements before and they didn't work"
Claim: "This isn't another appetite suppressant. It's the first supplement that blocks the enzyme
destroying your appetite hormone."
Proof: "In a 12-week study, women ate 24% fewer calories in week one without trying."
Benefit: "Imagine eating only 1,200 calories and feeling satisfied. Not deprived. Just... done
eating."

#6 - The Roles of Market Awareness Levels

Market awareness is something that for you right now is important to understand.
However, you should only be focusing from the levels of most aware to problem aware.
Unaware is not something I would recommend for anyone doing less than $100K per month.
This is something that will be saved for EVOLVE.
For now, just understand that when you write an ad, you must go through each stage
Problem Aware → Solution Aware → Product Aware → Most Aware
Solution Aware → Product Aware → Most Aware

Product Aware → Most Aware

BIG CREDITS:
$100M Offers - Alex Hormozi
This is a must-read for anybody in the online marketing space, if you ask me. Not only does this
apply to copywriting, but it also applies to your actual offer on your landing page.​
https://www.amazon.com/100M-Offers-People-Stupid-Saying-ebook/dp/B099QVG1H8/

Take Their Money - Kyle Milligan
This is a great copywriting book for those who are just getting into copywriting and need some
kind of simplified, easy-to-follow guide.
A Massive amount of this document is credited to him.
It has an aggressive name, but the principles this teaches are what I think anybody who wants
to be successful in eCom has to know.​
https://www.amazon.com/Take-Their-Money-Write-Really-ebook/dp/B0B94NRCV

Eugene Schwartz Breakthrough Advertising:
This is the godfather of copywriting books (and is the core of the EVOLVE program)​
https://breakthroughadvertisingbook.com/lp3-breakthrough-advertising/

And so that's why it's phenomenal at identifying things. It's phenomenal at filling in the gaps. And
it's amazing at gathering things, okay.
But AI should just never be your sole strategy or your relied upon strategy, at least for now, okay.
And just as a caveat, before I kind of go a little bit more, I mean, this is a shorter video, but we will
be likely optimizing a series of prompts around this copywriting program. And there is a much

still sorting it out.

writes you a script and you get a script back, you now have the knowledge to be able to optimize

optimize the bridge.

right way, as a result of the information in here. And that is personally my goal is not to give you

wouldn't stick what I'm teaching you.
